title: Benchmarking quantum trial wavefunctions for phaseless auxiliary-field quantum Monte Carlo
abstract: The phaseless auxiliary-field quantum Monte Carlo (ph-AFQMC) method is a stochastic imaginary-time projection technique for computing ground-state properties of strongly correlated quantum systems, with accuracy that depends critically on the choice of trial wavefunction. Here, we investigate ph-AFQMC with trial states prepared using parameterized quantum circuits. In this work, we present a comprehensive benchmarking study of quantum trial wavefunctions spanning unitary coupled-cluster, Hamiltonian-informed, Jastrow-inspired, and adaptively constructed ansatze. The benchmarking evaluates accuracy, expressibility, and scalability of these ansatze within the QC-AFQMC framework. We test these ansatze on linear hydrogen chains under bond stretching and find that several ansatz families produce chemically accurate ph-AFQMC energies across the dissociation curve. We have performed simulations using the CUDA-Q quantum development platform on the GPU partition of the Perlmutter supercomputer. When comparing ansatze at similar numbers of variational parameters, we find that different ansatz families yield comparable ph-AFQMC results despite exhibiting substantially different variational energies, optimization costs, and circuit depths. Our results indicate that the variational energy of an ansatz is not always a reliable indicator of its quality for ph-AFQMC and reveal instances of over-parameterization. In the strongly correlated regime, trial wavefunctions obtained from adaptive ansatze, exemplified here by ADAPT-VQE with the UCCSD operator pool, can outperform their fixed-ansatz counterparts (UCCSD) in terms of projected energies while using substantially more compact circuits, providing a flexible route to optimize quantum resources within the ph-AFQMC framework.
one_liner: Benchmarks quantum-circuit trial states for QC-assisted ph-AFQMC and finds compact adaptive ansatze can deliver chemically accurate energies.
why_it_matters: It clarifies which quantum ansatz families are worth deploying in hybrid many-body simulation, helping allocate limited circuit depth and optimization budget.
detailed_summary: The paper benchmarks several parameterized quantum-circuit trial states inside the phaseless auxiliary-field quantum Monte Carlo workflow, including UCC, Hamiltonian-informed, Jastrow-inspired, and adaptive ansatze. On stretched linear hydrogen chains, multiple families achieve chemically accurate projected energies across the dissociation curve despite large differences in variational energy, parameter count, and circuit depth. A key technical result is that variational energy is not a reliable proxy for ph-AFQMC trial quality, and adaptive constructions such as ADAPT-VQE with a UCCSD pool can outperform fixed UCCSD using more compact circuits in strongly correlated regimes.
main_result: Several quantum ansatz families yield chemically accurate ph-AFQMC energies, with adaptive ansatze offering better projected accuracy-to-circuit-size tradeoffs in hard correlation regimes.
application_angle: This guides practical hybrid quantum-classical simulation workflows for chemistry and materials by identifying trial-state designs that use quantum resources more efficiently.

title: Exponential speedups in fault-tolerant processing of quantum experiments
abstract: Quantum information processing has the potential to substantially enhance how we learn from physical experiments, but coupling a quantum processor to an experimental sample introduces noise that can exponentially degrade learning even when the processor itself is fault-tolerant. In this work, we show that fault tolerance can nevertheless be leveraged to recover exponential speedups by embedding the unknown system into an arbitrarily high-distance quantum code with only constant error overhead and running a fault-tolerant learning algorithm. Using this $\textit{quantum uploading}$ procedure, we prove that both classical shadow tomography and the estimation of cubic observables can be performed exponentially faster than by any adaptive strategy that does not immediately upload the state into encoded memory. These separations hold even when the uploading stage is substantially noisier than the bare experimental interface. To prove them, we introduce the Heisenberg learning tree method, a flexible tool for obtaining learning lower bounds when the limited resource is not quantum replicas but an experimentally motivated constraint such as noise. We numerically illustrate the speedups in an astronomical imaging application, where quantum processing of individual uploaded photons locates an exoplanet obscured by a bright star using orders of magnitude fewer shots than unencoded baselines. Our results establish fault-tolerant quantum computation as a valuable tool for learning from quantum experiments.
one_liner: Shows fault-tolerant quantum processors can recover exponential learning speedups from noisy experiments via encoded quantum uploading.
why_it_matters: It turns fault tolerance into a practical advantage for experimental inference, suggesting quantum hardware can outperform any non-uploading strategy even with noisy interfaces.
detailed_summary: The authors prove that an unknown experimental quantum state can be uploaded into a high-distance code with only constant extra error, enabling fault-tolerant post-processing that preserves exponential learning advantages. They establish separations for classical shadow tomography and cubic observable estimation against any adaptive strategy that does not immediately encode the state, using a new Heisenberg learning tree lower-bound method. Numerical results for astronomical imaging show that encoded photon processing can localize an exoplanet near a bright star with orders-of-magnitude fewer shots than unencoded baselines.
main_result: Immediate encoded uploading followed by fault-tolerant learning provably achieves exponential speedups over all strategies that keep processing at the noisy experimental interface.
application_angle: The framework is directly relevant to quantum-enhanced sensing and imaging systems where fragile experimental states must be processed under realistic noise.

title: Sapphire Photonic Crystal Fiber Sensor
abstract: Sapphire optical fiber shows great promise for remote sensing in extreme environments approaching 2000 degC, by using laser-processing to form a single-mode waveguide within it. However, for practical application, longer devices with high manufacturability and reliability are required. We report the design, modeling, fabrication, and optimization of an index-guiding sapphire photonic crystal fiber Bragg grating temperature sensor. The device is fabricated using femtosecond laser direct writing to inscribe both the photonic crystal waveguide and the Bragg grating. A spatial light modulator was used to compensate for the mismatch between the immersion objective and the high-index oil used. This improved the aspect ratio and suppressed cracking during fabrication, for higher reliability. The design results in a 6-fold reduction in fabrication time over an equivalent depressed cladding waveguide, significantly reducing the cost of manufacture. Devices up to 7 cm long were fabricated and spliced to standard single-mode fiber. The propagation loss was estimated to be 0.7 dB/cm and the Bragg gratings had a bandwidth of approximately 0.12 nm. Devices were tested in a furnace showing a temperature sensitivity of between 19.0-32.3 pm/degC over a range 25-1200 degC. These longer devices have the potential to enable practical high precision extreme temperature monitoring in many applications, with lower manufacturing cost and higher reliability.
one_liner: A femtosecond-written sapphire photonic crystal fiber Bragg sensor enables long, lower-cost extreme-temperature monitoring up to 1200 C.
why_it_matters: It advances deployable photonic sensing for harsh environments by improving manufacturability, reliability, and fiber compatibility in sapphire devices.
detailed_summary: This work designs and fabricates an index-guiding sapphire photonic crystal fiber with an integrated Bragg grating, both inscribed by femtosecond laser direct writing. Spatial-light-modulator correction improves the written mode shape and suppresses cracking, enabling 7 cm devices that splice to standard single-mode fiber. The architecture cuts fabrication time by about 6x versus equivalent depressed-cladding waveguides, with measured loss near 0.7 dB/cm, grating bandwidth around 0.12 nm, and temperature sensitivity of 19.0-32.3 pm/degC from 25 to 1200 C.
platform_or_system: sapphire photonic crystal fiber Bragg sensor
main_result: The authors demonstrate long sapphire photonic crystal fiber Bragg sensors with robust fabrication and high-temperature response over 25-1200 C.
application_angle: The device is suited to industrial furnaces, turbines, and other extreme-environment monitoring settings where silica fibers fail.

title: Optimizing Quantum Entanglement Preservation in a Qubit-Qubit System with Dzyaloshinskii Moriya Interaction under Noisy Magnetic Fields via Feedback Control
abstract: Quantum entanglement is a key resource for quantum information processing and sensing, but it is severely degraded by environmental noise. We extend the previous study by Moosavi Khansari and Kazemi Hasanvand [27] of entanglement dynamics in a qubit qubit system with Dzyaloshinskii Moriya (DM) interaction and static magnetic fields to the realistic case of time varying, stochastic magnetic fields. We derive a stochastic Lindblad master equation and simulate quantum trajectories to quantify the negativity under colored noise. We then design a proportional integral feedback protocol that dynamically adjusts the DM interaction strength D_z (t) to maintain negativity near a target value. The feedback stabilized state is used as a probe for quantum metrology: we compute the quantum Fisher information (QFI) for estimating an unknown static field B_0. Our simulations show that feedback increases the time averaged negativity from 0.21 to 0.42 for α=1 at noise amplitude σ=0.5, leading to a factor 2.4 improvement in sensitivity over the classical shot noise limit. This work provides a practical route to protect entanglement in noisy environments and enhances quantum sensing performance.
one_liner: Feedback-controlled Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya coupling doubles entanglement under magnetic noise and boosts field-sensing performance.
why_it_matters: It links real-time entanglement stabilization to measurable metrology gains, providing a control recipe for noisy quantum sensors.
detailed_summary: The paper models a two-qubit system with Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction under time-varying stochastic magnetic fields using a stochastic Lindblad master equation and quantum trajectories. A proportional-integral feedback loop dynamically tunes the DM interaction strength to keep the negativity near a target level despite colored noise. In simulation at noise amplitude 0.5 and alpha=1, the protocol raises time-averaged negativity from 0.21 to 0.42 and yields a 2.4x sensitivity improvement beyond the classical shot-noise limit via enhanced quantum Fisher information for static-field estimation.
platform_or_system: two-qubit spin system with DM interaction
main_result: Closed-loop control of the DM coupling substantially preserves entanglement under noisy fields and translates that preservation into stronger field-estimation sensitivity.
application_angle: The approach is relevant to spin-based quantum sensors that need active control to maintain metrological advantage in realistic noisy environments.

title: Designing a Satellite Serviced Quantum Network Backbone for Concurrent Global Connectivity
abstract: Satellite-serviced quantum networks pose an architectural problem distinct from classical satellite networking: because entanglement cannot be copied, and long-lived buffering is technologically constrained for near-term devices, useful end-to-end service requires fixed optical ground infrastructure and simultaneous multi-hop path availability. We investigate the design of a satellite-serviced quantum backbone aimed at supporting concurrent global connectivity across a traffic matrix of major population and financial centers under finite waiting-time constraints. Using a discrete-time simulator, we evaluate performance using two architecture-level metrics: (i) time-to-connectivity, and (ii) latency-conditioned average active-link strength. Across a broad parameter sweep, we identify three dominant architectural effects. First, anisotropic ground-station lattices reduce time-to-connectivity relative to longitudinally collapsed and isotropic baselines by aligning ground infrastructure with latitude-dependent satellite access. Second, multi-inclination LEO constellations reduce waiting times for strong connectivity compared to single-inclination constellations at fixed satellite budgets by providing additional visibility for a diverse latitude set. Third, multi-party satellite service policies alleviate per-satellite concurrency bottlenecks and substantially reduce time-to-connectivity at stringent traffic-matrix thresholds. We further show that satellite altitude is the dominant physical lever shaping the visibility--loss trade-off, strongly affecting both connectivity latency and achievable link strength, while orbital plane count and satellite packing provide secondary refinements at fixed altitude. Together, these results delineate the architectural conditions required for scalable, concurrent entanglement connectivity in satellite-serviced quantum networks.
one_liner: A system-level study identifies constellation and ground-network choices needed for concurrent global satellite quantum connectivity.
why_it_matters: It moves satellite quantum networking from point links toward scalable backbone design under realistic memory and concurrency constraints.
detailed_summary: Using a discrete-time simulator and finite waiting-time constraints, the authors study end-to-end entanglement service across a traffic matrix of major global hubs. They show anisotropic ground-station layouts reduce time-to-connectivity, multi-inclination LEO constellations improve visibility diversity at fixed satellite budgets, and multi-party service policies relieve per-satellite concurrency bottlenecks. The analysis also finds satellite altitude is the dominant physical knob controlling the visibility-loss tradeoff, while orbital plane count and packing provide secondary optimization levers.
main_result: Concurrent global entanglement connectivity is best supported by anisotropic ground infrastructure, multi-inclination constellations, and multi-party service policies, with altitude as the primary design lever.
application_angle: These results inform practical architecture decisions for future wide-area quantum internet backbones linking financial, governmental, and metropolitan centers.

title: Validity and Limits of Low Order Hybridization Expansion Approaches for Multi-Orbital Systems
abstract: Low-order hybridization expansion methods such as the non-crossing approximation (NCA) and the one-crossing approximation (OCA) are widely used impurity solvers in the study of strongly correlated systems, yet their accuracy in genuine multi-orbital settings remains poorly understood. Using the decoupled orbital limit as a controlled reference point, we derive analytic results connecting multi-orbital restricted propagators and Green's functions to their single-orbital counterparts, identify the diagrammatic mechanisms responsible for the breakdown of low-order methods in multi-orbital settings, and determine their regimes of applicability. Our central finding is that the accuracy of these methods is governed by the least correlated orbital: i.e., the orbital with the most rapidly decaying retarded Green's function. That orbital's properties are transferred to all other orbitals through a spurious coupling generated by the truncated expansion, thereby suppressing correlation-induced features such as the Kondo resonance. This occurs even in orbitals that are themselves strongly correlated within single-orbital calculations using the same approximation scheme. We confirm this numerically across representative two-orbital model systems in the steady-state, systematically identifying the parameter regimes in which low-order methods succeed or fail. Our results provide a practical guide for assessing when insights from single-orbital calculations carry over to multi-orbital settings, and serve as a benchmark for the development and validation of higher-order multi-orbital impurity solvers.
one_liner: This study exposes when NCA and OCA fail in multi-orbital impurity problems by tracing errors to the least correlated orbital.
why_it_matters: It gives a concrete validity map for widely used approximate solvers, reducing the risk of misinterpreting transport and correlation signatures in complex devices.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze low-order hybridization expansion methods in multi-orbital impurity models using the decoupled-orbital limit as a controlled reference. They derive analytic relations between multi-orbital restricted propagators and Green's functions and show that truncated diagrams induce a spurious inter-orbital coupling governed by the least correlated orbital. This artificial transfer suppresses correlation features such as the Kondo resonance across orbitals, and numerical tests on representative two-orbital steady-state models map the parameter regimes where NCA and OCA remain reliable or break down.
main_result: The work shows that low-order impurity solvers can inherit the fastest-decaying orbital's behavior across all orbitals, causing systematic loss of genuine correlation physics.
application_angle: It provides a practical benchmark for modeling correlated quantum devices and materials where solver choice affects hardware-relevant transport predictions.

title: Free-standing circular Bragg gratings enabling efficient GaAs quantum dot entangled photon pair sources
abstract: Deterministic and bright quantum light sources based on scalable semiconductor technologies are a crucial building block for future quantum communication networks. While circular Bragg gratings (CBGs) are highly effective for extracting light from solid-state quantum emitters, conventional architectures rely on complex multi-layer processing or flip-chip bonding, which introduce detrimental strain and limit scalability. Here, we present a fabrication-minimal approach to realize monolithic, free-standing CBG cavities with deterministically positioned single GaAs quantum dots (QDs). By utilizing aspect-ratio-dependent etching (ARDE) in a single-step top-down process, we achieve the necessary vertical structural asymmetry for directional emission without requiring bottom reflectors. Finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations validate this geometry, predicting free-space extraction efficiencies up to $68 \, \%$ and coupling efficiencies of $40 \, \%$ into a lensed single-mode fiber ($\text{NA} = 0.6$). Experimentally, the deterministically coupled QD-CBG devices yield a photoluminescence intensity enhancement of up to $\times 700$ compared to unprocessed planar QDs, reaching integrated count rates of $45 \, MHz$. Furthermore, the suspended membrane architecture effectively relaxes residual strain, significantly reducing the average exciton fine-structure splitting from $7.3 \, μeV$ in planar QDs to $1.3 \, μeV$ in the CBGs. Interferometric measurements confirm that the fabrication process preserves the optical quality of the emitters, with average coherence times of $70 \, ps$. By bridging optimized FDTD design with precise nanofabrication and robust optical performance, these results establish free-standing GaAs CBGs as a highly scalable platform for bright and coherent entangled photon pair sources.
one_liner: A monolithic free-standing GaAs circular Bragg grating boosts quantum-dot brightness while suppressing fine-structure splitting for entangled-photon emission.
why_it_matters: It points to a simpler, scalable semiconductor source of bright entangled photon pairs with better strain control and efficient fiber-compatible extraction.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a single-step top-down fabrication route for free-standing circular Bragg gratings with deterministically positioned GaAs quantum dots, using aspect-ratio-dependent etching to create vertical asymmetry without a bottom reflector. FDTD predicts up to 68% free-space extraction efficiency and 40% coupling into a lensed single-mode fiber. Experimentally, the devices show up to 700x photoluminescence enhancement and 45 MHz integrated count rates. The suspended membrane also relaxes residual strain, cutting average exciton fine-structure splitting from 7.3 to 1.3 micro-eV while preserving emitter coherence around 70 ps.
platform_or_system: GaAs quantum dots in free-standing circular Bragg gratings
main_result: The authors demonstrate bright, coherent, deterministically coupled GaAs QD emitters with strong extraction enhancement and reduced fine-structure splitting in a fabrication-minimal cavity design.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to deployable entangled-photon sources for quantum networks because it combines scalable processing, high brightness, and efficient fiber interfacing.

title: Rethinking How to Act: Action-Space Engineering for Reinforcement Learning-Based Circuit Routing in Distributed Quantum Systems
abstract: As it becomes increasingly difficult to monolithically scale a quantum processor, distributed quantum computing (DQC) offers an alternative by distributing qubits across multiple smaller interconnected quantum processor modules. In such an architecture, the challenge of quantum circuit compilation shifts from placing and routing qubits within one module to placing, routing and using the qubits efficiently across modules. In order to optimize circuit execution time, the right state-dependent networking decisions must be found, such as when and where to generate shared remote quantum states to support remote operations. Reinforcement learning (RL) provides a natural framework for this problem, generating a compilation policy that can generalize across different circuits. Building on the framework of Promponas et al. (2024), we introduce an agent that combines a novel action-space formulation with effective action-masking strategies. A comprehensive numerical comparison of the two approaches under different coupling constraints shows that our agent achieves improved training and inference performance with a relative reduction in the modeled execution time of up to 35\%.
one_liner: A redesigned RL action space cuts modeled execution time for distributed quantum circuit routing by up to 35%.
why_it_matters: Better routing policies are a practical lever for making modular quantum processors usable before fully fault-tolerant large monolithic chips exist.
detailed_summary: This paper studies reinforcement-learning-based compilation for distributed quantum computing, where qubits and entanglement resources must be managed across interconnected modules. The main contribution is a new action-space formulation combined with action masking, aimed at improving both training stability and inference quality. Numerical comparisons against the prior framework of Promponas et al. show better performance under different coupling constraints. The learned policy reduces modeled execution time by as much as 35% through better state-dependent networking decisions.
main_result: The proposed RL agent with engineered action space and masking achieves up to a 35% relative reduction in modeled execution time for distributed circuit routing.
application_angle: Compiler-level gains like this can improve throughput and latency on near-term modular quantum hardware without changing the underlying devices.

title: Quantum Algorithms for Magic Square Diophantine Equations
abstract: Magic-square constraints define Diophantine systems whose solutions, in several natural families, exhibit rigid periodic structure. We study this structure in an oracle setting, where a marked set of integers is given by black-box access and the goal is to decide whether it encodes a magic square. For $3\times 3$ magic squares and weighted variants, we prove explicit periodic characterizations that reduce detection to period finding. For larger orders, we identify a class of solutions built from repeated arithmetic patterns, which can be detected via the quantum Fourier transform. We then introduce a shifted-oracle method, based on interference between an oracle and its translates, that helps reconstruct solutions in structured cases. Together, these ingredients give a quantum framework for detecting and reconstructing certain magic-square solutions under suitable assumptions. We also derive finite bounds that make some instances exhaustively solvable and obtain Shor-based criteria for certifying non-existence in restricted number-theoretic settings. As an application, we sketch a quantum communication protocol based on an oracle encoding of a large magic-square solution.
one_liner: Structured magic-square Diophantine instances are recast into quantum period-finding and interference-based oracle algorithms.
why_it_matters: It links number-theoretic structure to quantum algorithm design and sketches a niche oracle-based communication use case built on that structure.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze oracle-defined sets of integers that may encode magic-square solutions and show that several structured families admit explicit periodic characterizations. For 3x3 and weighted cases, detection reduces to period finding, while certain larger-order constructions can be identified with the quantum Fourier transform. They also introduce a shifted-oracle interference method to help reconstruct structured solutions. The paper further derives finite exhaustive-search bounds and Shor-style criteria for certifying non-existence in restricted settings, then sketches a communication protocol using an oracle encoding of a large magic square.
platform_or_system: Oracle-model quantum algorithms for magic-square structures
main_result: The paper establishes quantum detection and reconstruction methods for structured magic-square solution families using period-finding, QFT, and shifted-oracle interference.
application_angle: Its practical relevance is mainly conceptual, suggesting specialized structured-oracle primitives that could inform niche quantum communication or verification protocols.

title: Photon Number Coherence of a Quantum Dot-Cavity System Excited Using the SUPER Scheme
abstract: To fulfill the security requirements of quantum cryptography, photon number coherence (PNC) of single photon sources has recently become an important figure of merit. Quantum dots (QDs) embedded in photonic microcavities offer a mature source of single photons, of which many properties can be tuned by the use of different excitation protocols or parameters. We show that the Swing-UP of quantum EmitteR population (SUPER) scheme can significantly decrease the PNC of the emitted photon, compared to resonant excitation. The reason for this is a laser-induced Stark shift, which effectively decouples the QD from the cavity during the SUPER excitation. Our calculations account for environmental effects such as phonons and radiative losses.
one_liner: SUPER excitation can suppress photon-number coherence in a QD-cavity single-photon source via a laser-induced Stark shift.
why_it_matters: Photon-number coherence is emerging as a security-relevant source metric for quantum cryptography, so excitation protocols must be judged on more than brightness or indistinguishability.
detailed_summary: The paper evaluates photon-number coherence in a quantum dot embedded in a photonic microcavity under the Swing-UP of quantum EmitteR population scheme. Modeling includes phonons and radiative losses and shows that SUPER excitation can significantly reduce photon-number coherence compared with resonant excitation. The mechanism is a laser-induced Stark shift that transiently decouples the dot from the cavity. This identifies a nontrivial tradeoff between population-control protocols and cryptographically relevant source quality.
main_result: The analysis shows that SUPER excitation degrades photon-number coherence relative to resonant driving because the induced Stark shift effectively decouples the emitter from the cavity.
application_angle: This helps source designers choose excitation schemes for secure photonic quantum communication where coherence in photon number can affect protocol security assumptions.

title: Permutation Routing on Ramanujan Hypergraphs with Applications to Neutral Atom Quantum Architectures
abstract: We consider the routing of neutral atoms on a reconfigurable lattice in terms of hypergraph transformations. We prove the routing number of a Ramanujan $(d,r)$-regular hypergraph on $N$ vertices satisfies $\mathrm{rt}(H) = Θ(\log N)$, where routing is via matchings in the clique expansion graph $G_{\mathrm{cl}}(H)$. Hypergraphs reframe the qubit routing problem by replacing Nenadov's two-sided spectral gap hypothesis with a one-sided condition based on eigenvalue centering. Song--Fan--Miao (SFM) coverings scale for Ramanujan families of every uniformity. A virtual overlay theorem establishes a capacity--depth tradeoff for 3D acousto-optic lens (AOL) architectures, with multi-layer stacking achieving $Θ(\log N)$ routing with $L = O(\log N)$ independent overlay layers. An abelian Alon--Boppana barrier shows that fixed-degree Cayley graphs on $\mathbb{Z}_n^2$ cannot be Ramanujan and affine derandomization on such graphs achieves 15--30% congestion reduction. Towers of $k$-fold Ramanujan coverings yield $\mathrm(H_L) = O(\log N)$ by recursive routing lift. Entanglement-assisted routing by pre-distributed Bell pairs achieves $O(\log N)$ teleportation depth with a stable crossover at $\sim\!4$ routing rounds. Displacement energy analyzes greedy adaptive routing, identifying stalling and a hybrid greedy--Valiant protocol achieving $\sim\!3\times$ speedup at practical scales. Hierarchical multi-scale routing achieves $O(\log^2 N / \log b)$ depth with boundary-only transfers at capacity $k = O(\sqrt{N} \log N)$, and $O(\log N)$ depth with optimal block size $b = Θ(\sqrt{n})$.
one_liner: Ramanujan-hypergraph routing gives logarithmic-depth atom transport and teleportation strategies for scalable neutral-atom architectures.
why_it_matters: Efficient routing is a core systems bottleneck in large neutral-atom machines, and the paper supplies asymptotic guarantees plus architecture-specific tradeoff analysis.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates neutral-atom routing on reconfigurable lattices in a hypergraph framework and proves that Ramanujan regular hypergraphs achieve routing number Theta(log N) via matchings in the clique expansion graph. It develops virtual overlay results for 3D acousto-optic lens architectures, showing multi-layer stacking can preserve logarithmic routing depth with logarithmically many overlay layers. Additional results cover entanglement-assisted routing with Bell pairs, hybrid greedy-Valiant schemes, affine derandomization, and hierarchical multi-scale protocols. Together these results characterize depth-capacity-congestion tradeoffs for large-scale atom-shuttling and teleportation-based movement.
platform_or_system: Neutral-atom arrays with reconfigurable AOL lattice control
main_result: The authors prove logarithmic-depth permutation routing for Ramanujan-hypergraph-based architectures and map those guarantees onto realistic neutral-atom and entanglement-assisted routing schemes.
application_angle: These results are useful for architecture design because they translate graph-theoretic routing structure into concrete scaling laws for moving qubits and scheduling nonlocal operations.

title: Programmable non-Gaussian quantum light source with state and temporal-waveform tunability
abstract: A versatile quantum light source capable of programmably generating a variety of quantum light is a key enabler for photonic quantum technologies. In particular, independent control over both the output quantum state and its temporal waveform is essential for realizing diverse functionalities and enhancing processing performance. However, conventional sources of optical non-Gaussian states, a crucial resource for photonic quantum information processing, typically emit fixed states with predetermined temporal waveforms, lacking their programmability. Here, we propose a programmable non-Gaussian quantum light source that offers independent and arbitrary tunability of both the quantum state and the temporal waveform within a single platform. As a distinctive feature, our approach employs a heralding scheme in which these two properties are indirectly engineered to user-defined targets by manipulating the light in the heralding channel, thereby avoiding optical losses associated with direct manipulation of the heralded quantum light. We develop a prototype and demonstrate the generation of single-photon, Schrödinger cat, and two-photon states in a variety of unconventional temporal waveforms without degradation in state quality. This platform provides a versatile tool for tailoring quantum light to specific applications, significantly expanding the capabilities of photonic quantum technologies.
one_liner: A heralded source programmably emits non-Gaussian states with independently tunable quantum state and temporal waveform.
why_it_matters: Independent control of state and temporal mode is a major practical requirement for interfacing photonic resources with diverse processors, memories, and networks.
detailed_summary: This work proposes and prototypes a programmable heralded source in which target quantum state and temporal waveform are both engineered indirectly through operations on the heralding arm. Because the heralded field is not directly manipulated, the approach avoids loss penalties that would normally degrade fragile non-Gaussian states. The authors demonstrate single-photon, Schrodinger-cat, and two-photon states across a range of unconventional temporal waveforms while maintaining state quality. The result is a single platform with independent tunability over two usually fixed source properties.
platform_or_system: Programmable heralded optical non-Gaussian light source
main_result: The prototype generates multiple non-Gaussian states with arbitrary temporal waveforms while preserving state quality by shaping only the heralding channel.
application_angle: This is highly relevant for photonic quantum technologies that need source outputs matched to specific memories, gates, multiplexers, or communication channels.

title: Entanglement Generation During Distribution via Spatial Superposition Entanglement Generation
abstract: The exploitation of quantum coherence at the level of propagation represents a powerful paradigm for quantum communication networks. In this work, we show that the coherent superposition of spatially distinct communication links enables entanglement generation inherently during distribution. Specifically, separable quantum states can be deterministically transformed into entangled states, when the noisy communication links they traverse are coherently superposed. Contrary to the conventional view of noise as a detrimental effect, we demonstrate that quantum noise itself can be transformed into a constructive resource for entanglement generation for both bipartite and multipartite entanglement. Given the practical feasibility of implementing spatial superposition in interferometric setups, our approach provides a feasible method for distributed entanglement engineering, opening new directions for quantum communication and networked quantum technologies.
one_liner: Coherently superposing distinct communication paths turns noisy transmission itself into a deterministic entanglement-generation mechanism.
why_it_matters: It reframes link noise from a liability into a network resource, offering a feasible new route to distributed entanglement generation in interferometric quantum links.
detailed_summary: The paper shows that separable states can be deterministically converted into entangled states when they traverse communication links arranged in a coherent spatial superposition. Rather than suppressing channel noise, the protocol exploits it constructively, and the authors extend the idea beyond bipartite to multipartite entanglement generation. The scheme is framed around interferometric implementations, which makes the proposal experimentally relevant for networked photonic quantum communication setups.
main_result: Deterministic bipartite and multipartite entanglement can be generated during transmission by coherently superposing noisy spatial channels.
application_angle: This could simplify quantum network architectures by embedding entanglement creation directly into distribution hardware instead of relying only on separate entanglement sources.

title: Construction of Quantum Rank-Metric Codes Using Hermitian Orthogonality
abstract: Stacked quantum memory is an architecture in which multiple layers of qubits are stacked. Quantum rank-metric codes are effective for error correction in stacked quantum memories. However, the previously proposed quantum Gabidulin codes based on the CSS construction had a problem: due to algebraic constraints, the applicable memory layouts were strictly limited to square shapes of odd length. In this paper, we first propose a framework for constructing quantum rank-metric codes from classical linear codes with symplectic self-orthogonality. Building upon this, we propose a new construction method for quantum Gabidulin codes by combining the Hermitian self-orthogonality of classical Gabidulin codes--utilizing the self-dual basis that exists when the extension degree of the finite field is even--with the quantum code construction method using Hermitian orthogonality by Matsumoto and Uyematsu. The proposed method succeeds in approximately doubling the ratio of the minimum rank distance to the number of physical qubits while maintaining the code rate. Furthermore, it eliminates the restriction of the conventional method that requires the number of cells and layers of the stacked memory to be odd, realizing the construction of quantum rank-metric codes applicable to memories with an even number of cells and layers. This construction improves the relative error correction capability of the stacked quantum memory architecture and increases the degree of freedom in design while preserving the code rate.
one_liner: A new Hermitian-orthogonality construction broadens quantum rank-metric codes for stacked memories while improving relative error-correction strength.
why_it_matters: It removes key geometry constraints in stacked quantum memories and boosts usable distance-to-qubit ratios without sacrificing code rate.
detailed_summary: The authors develop a general construction of quantum rank-metric codes from symplectically self-orthogonal classical linear codes, then derive new quantum Gabidulin codes using Hermitian self-orthogonality and self-dual bases over even-degree field extensions. Compared with earlier CSS-based constructions, the method approximately doubles the ratio of minimum rank distance to physical qubit count at fixed code rate. It also removes the prior requirement that stacked memory layouts have odd numbers of cells and layers, enabling even-by-even architectures.
main_result: The proposed code family improves relative rank-distance performance and supports stacked quantum memories with even numbers of cells and layers.
application_angle: These codes expand practical hardware layout freedom for multi-layer quantum memories while strengthening protection against correlated rank-type errors.

title: Tomogram-based quantifiers of nonclassicality dynamics in Kerr and cubic media
abstract: The reliable quantification of nonclassicality in quantum states under realistic decoherence remains a central challenge in advancing quantum technologies. Conventional quantifiers such as Wigner negativity, Mandel's $Q$-parameter, nonclassical depth, etc., are often experimentally intractable, non-unique, or insensitive to key quantum signatures. We demonstrate that tomogram-based measures, the homodyne nonclassical area and sum tomographic entropy, offer a robust, experimentally accessible alternative for quantifying nonclassicality dynamics, as they can be directly obtained from optical tomograms via balanced homodyne detection, avoiding density matrix reconstruction and ensuring feasibility. We study coherent, photon-added coherent, and even coherent states evolving in Kerr and cubic nonlinear systems, with environmental effects modelled using the Lindblad master equation under amplitude and phase damping. The homodyne nonclassical area, which quantifies the excess quadrature variance beyond that of a coherent state, tracks both the onset and decay of nonclassicality, clearly identifying fractional revivals, wave packet splitting, and macroscopic superpositions. We find that amplitude damping drives a rapid monotonic decay toward the vacuum, while phase damping allows partial revival features to survive longer. Complementing this, the sum tomographic entropy derived from conjugate-quadrature tomograms captures higher-order fractional revivals and phase-space interference through persistent entropy minima under weak damping. Our results establish homodyne-based quantifiers as powerful, real-time, and experimentally viable tools for tracking nonclassical dynamics in nonlinear optical media, offering a compelling alternative to conventional, experimentally challenging measures.
one_liner: Homodyne-accessible tomographic metrics track nonclassicality dynamics in nonlinear optical media without full state reconstruction.
why_it_matters: It offers experimentally practical diagnostics for real-time nonclassicality monitoring under decoherence using standard balanced homodyne measurements.
detailed_summary: This work studies coherent, photon-added coherent, and even coherent states evolving in Kerr and cubic nonlinear media under amplitude and phase damping modeled with Lindblad dynamics. The authors show that the homodyne nonclassical area and sum tomographic entropy, both extracted directly from optical tomograms, capture fractional revivals, wave-packet splitting, macroscopic superpositions, and decoherence trends. Amplitude damping causes rapid decay toward vacuum, while phase damping preserves revival signatures longer, with entropy minima remaining sensitive to higher-order interference effects.
platform_or_system: Nonlinear optical Kerr and cubic media
main_result: Tomogram-based observables derived from homodyne data robustly quantify nonclassicality dynamics and revival structure without density-matrix reconstruction.
application_angle: The method is directly usable in photonic experiments that need fast, low-overhead monitoring of nonclassical resources in noisy nonlinear platforms.

title: Valley-locked Optical Spin Skyrmions in Valley Photonic Crystal Waveguides
abstract: Optical skyrmions have attracted significant attention across diverse physical systems for their promising scenarios in ultra-precise metrology, optical information processing, and quantum technologies. However, the lack of effective method for their on-chip directional transport and manipulation impedes their applications in photonic integrated devices. Here, we demonstrate a photonic platform that utilizes topologically protected valley edge state to achieve robust on-chip directional transport of optical spin skyrmions. These skyrmions originate from spin-orbit coupling within the evanescent field at the valley photonic crystal surface and exist as eigenstates of the topologically protected edge state, ensuring their robust unidirectional propagation. Leveraging the valley degree of freedom of topological edge states, we further achieve valley-locked spin skyrmions, enabling flexible control over the polarity of spin skyrmions. By endowing spin skyrmions with topological protection in momentum space, our work provides an approach for robust on-chip transport and manipulation of spin skyrmions, thereby paving the way for expanding their application potential in photonic systems.
one_liner: Valley photonic crystal waveguides enable topologically protected, valley-locked transport and control of optical spin skyrmions on chip.
why_it_matters: It provides a realistic route to robust skyrmion manipulation in integrated photonics, important for information processing and precision optical functionality.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates that optical spin skyrmions can arise as eigenstates of topologically protected valley edge modes in a valley photonic crystal waveguide. These skyrmions are generated through spin-orbit coupling in the evanescent field and inherit robust unidirectional transport from the underlying edge state. By using the valley degree of freedom, the platform further realizes valley-locked skyrmions whose polarity can be flexibly selected and guided on chip.
main_result: Topologically protected valley edge states support robust directional transport and polarity control of optical spin skyrmions on an integrated photonic platform.
application_angle: This opens a path to chip-scale skyrmion-based photonic devices for routing, encoding, and metrology with enhanced robustness to disorder.

title: Readout failures in superconducting qubits due to TLS-defects in tunnel junctions
abstract: Material defects give rise to parasitic two-level systems (TLS) which present a major source of decoherence in superconducting qubits. Here, we study a strongly coupled TLS that resides in the tunnel barrier of transmon qubit. We use multi-photon spectroscopy and TLS strain tuning to explore the rich spectrum of the interacting three-partite system consisting of TLS, qubit, and its readout resonator. This reveals a strong effective resonant coupling between the TLS and the qubit's readout resonator which dresses the resonator states and results in a resonance frequency shift that spoils the readout signal. Our finding presents yet another way how material defects can interfere with qubit operation and hinder the realization of solid-state quantum processors.
one_liner: A tunnel-barrier TLS can hybridize with a transmon readout chain and shift the resonator enough to corrupt superconducting-qubit measurements.
why_it_matters: It identifies a concrete hardware failure mode where material defects directly spoil readout fidelity, not just coherence.
detailed_summary: Using multi-photon spectroscopy together with strain tuning of a strongly coupled defect, the authors map the spectrum of a three-partite system formed by a transmon, a tunnel-junction TLS, and the readout resonator. They find an effective resonant TLS-resonator coupling that dresses the resonator states and shifts the measurement frequency response. This mechanism degrades readout contrast and reveals a previously underappreciated pathway by which junction defects can impair processor operation.
main_result: A strongly coupled junction TLS can induce resonator-state dressing and frequency shifts that cause superconducting-qubit readout failures.
application_angle: The result directly informs materials screening, junction fabrication, and readout calibration strategies for scalable superconducting quantum processors.

title: Measuring Accuracy and Energy-to-Solution of Quantum Fine-Tuning of Foundational AI Models
abstract: We present an experimental study of energy-to-solution (ETS) of hybrid quantum-classical applications, enabled by direct instrumentation of power consumption of a Forte Enterprise trapped-ion quantum processor. We apply this methodology to a hybrid quantum-classical pipeline for quantum fine-tuning of foundational AI models, and validate the approach end-to-end on quantum hardware. Despite noise and limited qubit counts, the resulting models achieve accuracy competitive with and exceeding classical baselines such as logistic regression and support vector classifiers. Our results show that QPU energy consumption scales approximately linearly with qubit number for shallow circuits, while classical simulation exhibits exponential scaling, indicating a break-even for ETS around 34 qubits. The classification error improvement of the best quantum fine-tuned model over the best classical fine-tuned model considered in this study is around 24%. We further contextualize these findings with comparisons to tensor network methods. This work establishes energy-to-solution as a measurable and scalable metric for evaluating quantum applications and provides experimental evidence of favorable energy-accuracy trade-offs.
one_liner: Direct power measurements on a trapped-ion QPU quantify energy-to-solution for hybrid quantum fine-tuning of foundation-model pipelines.
why_it_matters: It brings a hardware-measured efficiency metric to quantum AI workloads and reports a plausible ETS break-even regime beyond classical simulation.
detailed_summary: The study instruments power consumption on a Forte Enterprise trapped-ion processor and evaluates end-to-end energy-to-solution for a hybrid quantum-classical workflow that fine-tunes foundational AI models. On hardware, the best quantum fine-tuned models achieve classification accuracy competitive with and sometimes exceeding logistic regression and SVC baselines, with about 24% lower error than the best classical fine-tuned model considered. The measured QPU energy scales roughly linearly with qubit number for shallow circuits, while classical simulation scales exponentially, suggesting an ETS break-even near 34 qubits.
main_result: Hardware-instrumented experiments show favorable energy-accuracy trade-offs for hybrid quantum fine-tuning, with projected ETS break-even around 34 qubits for shallow circuits.
application_angle: This provides a practical benchmarking framework for deciding when quantum accelerators may become useful for real ML pipelines under both accuracy and energy constraints.

title: Entanglement cost in non-local quantum computation
abstract: This is a book-length treatment of the subject of non-local quantum computation (NLQC). NLQC is a method for implementing quantum operations that interact two systems without directly bringing the systems together. Instead, a single round of communication and shared entanglement is used. NLQC has appeared in the context of quantum cryptography, computational complexity, communication complexity, quantum gravity, and other applications. The understanding of entanglement cost in NLQC is closely tied to questions in all of these areas. We review upper and lower bounds on entanglement cost, as well as some of the applications of NLQC and its connections to other subjects.
one_liner: A comprehensive analysis of how much shared entanglement is required to execute non-local quantum computations with one communication round.
why_it_matters: Clarifying NLQC entanglement costs informs the feasibility and limits of distributed quantum protocols used in cryptography, communication, and networked computing.
detailed_summary: This work gives a book-length treatment of non-local quantum computation, where separated parties implement a joint quantum operation using pre-shared entanglement and a single communication round. It reviews both upper and lower bounds on the entanglement resources needed for different NLQC tasks and connects these bounds to broader questions in cryptography, complexity theory, and physics. The emphasis is on resource scaling and on identifying when entanglement requirements become a practical bottleneck for distributed quantum processing.
main_result: The paper synthesizes the state of the art on entanglement-cost bounds for NLQC and their implications across multiple distributed quantum-information settings.
application_angle: It is practically relevant for assessing whether networked quantum protocols can realize remote operations within realistic entanglement budgets.

title: Fixed-detector tilt--defocus sensing by upstream source coding in a time-reversed Young interferometer
abstract: We propose a physically explicit sensing application of a time-reversed Young (TRY) interferometer: simultaneous monitoring of beam tilt and focus drift with a fixed detector. The task is relevant to compact optical relays, free-space links, fiber-coupling stages, and micro-optical alignment modules, where continuous tracking of pointing and focus is needed but downstream wavefront cameras or multiport analyzers are undesirable. Using a finite-width double-slit Fresnel model, we derive the exact local TRY response functions for tilt-like and defocus-like phase perturbations and compute the corresponding optimal upstream source codes numerically. The physical optimal codes are fringe-locked and differ qualitatively from the simple odd/even modes suggested by Gaussian toy models. Two source-coded scalar channels recover essentially all local Fisher information in the full source-resolved TRY record for the physical model considered here. Compared with downstream direct intensity sensing, TRY provides first-order access to the mixed tilt--defocus task with fixed detection; compared with ideal downstream matched-mode sorting, its advantage is architectural rather than fundamental.
one_liner: A time-reversed Young interferometer can sense beam tilt and defocus simultaneously with a fixed detector using optimized upstream source coding.
why_it_matters: The method offers a compact alignment and monitoring architecture for free-space and fiber-coupled optics without downstream wavefront cameras or complex analyzers.
detailed_summary: The authors derive exact local response functions of a finite-width double-slit Fresnel time-reversed Young interferometer to tilt-like and defocus-like phase perturbations. They numerically optimize upstream source codes and show that two scalar coded channels recover nearly all local Fisher information contained in the full source-resolved record. The optimal physical codes are fringe-locked and differ qualitatively from the odd/even modes predicted by simplified Gaussian models. Relative to direct intensity sensing, the scheme enables first-order access to the mixed tilt-defocus estimation task while keeping detection fixed.
main_result: Two optimized upstream source-coded channels extract essentially all local Fisher information for joint tilt and defocus sensing with a fixed detector in the modeled TRY system.
application_angle: This is relevant to compact optical relays, free-space quantum links, fiber-coupling stages, and micro-optical alignment modules that need low-complexity continuous tracking.

title: Robust spin-squeezing on quantum networks: the lesson from universality
abstract: We establish the conditions under which scalable spin squeezing can be achieved in interacting spin ensembles embedded in arbitrary, inhomogeneous network geometries. We identify two different forms of squeezing: OAT-like scalable squeezing is governed solely by the universal properties of the interaction graph and is controlled by its spectral dimension. In critical squeezing, on the other hand, the value of the spectral dimension only furnishes the necessary condition for scalable metrological gain, while the sufficient condition requires the model to lie below the symmetry breaking transition. Therefore, in quantum networks, the scaling of the spin-squeezing critical point emerges from a nontrivial interplay between xy-ferromagnetic universality and percolation universality. We apply this general theoretical framework to several experimental scenarios and discuss sharp and experimentally relevant conditions for achieving robust metrological gain on generic inhomogeneous structures, giving a unifying perspective for designing scalable quantum sensors across diverse quantum simulation platforms.
one_liner: A universality-based framework predicts when arbitrary spin networks can achieve scalable spin squeezing and robust metrological gain.
why_it_matters: It provides experimentally actionable design rules for building large, disordered quantum sensor networks that still deliver useful squeezing.
detailed_summary: The paper identifies two squeezing regimes in interacting spin ensembles on arbitrary inhomogeneous graphs: OAT-like scalable squeezing governed by the graph spectral dimension, and critical squeezing that additionally requires operation below the symmetry-breaking transition. The analysis ties squeezing thresholds to an interplay between XY-ferromagnetic universality and percolation universality. This yields sharp criteria for scalable metrological gain in disordered or irregular network geometries and is mapped onto several experimentally relevant scenarios.
platform_or_system: interacting spin ensembles on quantum networks
main_result: Scalable squeezing in generic spin networks is shown to be controlled by universal graph properties, with stronger critical-squeezing conditions set by both spectral dimension and proximity to symmetry breaking.
application_angle: The framework can guide the architecture of networked atomic, solid-state, or simulator-based sensors intended to retain precision enhancement despite geometric disorder.

title: Closed form logical error rate approximations for surface codes
abstract: We propose a novel method to calculate logical error rates in surface codes, assuming independent and identically distributed physical errors. We show how to use our method to analyze hypothetical quantum computers with various configurations and select designs with lower error rates. Currently, this requires expensive classical simulations of quantum decoders for various distances and physical error rates or inaccurate extrapolation from minimal experimental data. Instead, we use the symmetry of the problem to count the configurations that result in a logical error with our novel software. Given a physical error rate, we can deduce the probability of a logical error, to provably good accuracy. We include an analysis of measurement errors to allow a more complete comparison of different surface code implementations.
one_liner: Closed-form approximations estimate surface-code logical error rates without relying on expensive large-scale decoder simulations.
why_it_matters: Fast and accurate logical-error estimates are critical for choosing code distances and hardware designs in practical fault-tolerant quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a symmetry-based counting method to approximate logical error rates in surface codes under i.i.d. physical noise. Instead of repeated decoder simulations or crude extrapolations, their method enumerates configurations that lead to logical failure and provably achieves good accuracy. They also incorporate measurement errors, allowing more realistic comparisons across candidate surface-code implementations and hardware configurations.
main_result: The work provides closed-form, provably accurate logical-error approximations for surface codes, including the effect of measurement errors.
application_angle: This can materially accelerate design-space exploration for quantum hardware stacks by enabling rapid screening of code distances and operating points.

title: Exchange-Only Silicon Based Spin Qubits: Charge Noise, PINN Optimised Pulse Sequences,and Gate-Level Fidelity
abstract: Exchange-only (EO) spin qubits in silicon realise all-electrical qubit control through pairwise Heisenberg exchange interactions, making them attractive for scalable quantum computation. Their principal vulnerability is charge noise, which couples multiplicatively to the exchange coupling and degrades gate fidelity. We present a \emph{two-stage} Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) framework for per-gate pulse optimisation. In \textbf{Stage~I} (iterations~1--100) the PINN maximises the noise-averaged gate fidelity toward a threshold of $\Fth=0.99$; the pulse duration is held fixed at its nominal hardware value. Once the threshold is crossed, \textbf{Stage~II} (iterations~101--250) progressively compresses the total pulse time while maintaining $F\geq\Fth$ via continuous fine-tuning of the pulse-shape parameters. The cost function is a Monte-Carlo ensemble mean-squared error (MSE) averaged over $N_{\rm real}=2000$ quasi-static Gaussian noise realisations drawn fresh at every iteration. We benchmark the framework on the single-qubit gate set $\{X,Y,Z,H\}$ and the two-qubit set $\{X,Y,Z,H,\mathrm{CX}\}$ at noise levels $\sigmaJ/J\in\{1\%,5\%,10\%\}$. All single-qubit gates cross $\Fth$ within the first 100 iterations across all noise levels; Stage~II then reduces pulse durations by 20--40\% from their nominal values. The two-qubit gates follow the same two-phase behaviour, with the CX gate compressing from its nominal \SI{31}{\nano\second} to $\approx\SI{22}{\nano\second}$ at 1\% noise.
one_liner: Physics-informed neural networks optimize exchange-only silicon spin-qubit gates to resist charge noise while shortening pulse durations.
why_it_matters: Improving noisy all-electrical spin control directly advances scalable silicon quantum processors where fidelity and gate time are tightly constrained.
detailed_summary: This paper studies exchange-only silicon spin qubits, where control is implemented through pairwise Heisenberg exchange and is strongly affected by multiplicative charge noise. A two-stage PINN workflow first maximizes noise-averaged gate fidelity to at least 0.99 under Monte Carlo quasi-static Gaussian noise, then compresses pulse duration while preserving that threshold. Across 1%, 5%, and 10% noise levels, single-qubit X, Y, Z, and H gates all surpass the target in the first stage, and the second stage shortens pulses by 20-40%; for two-qubit control, the CX gate is reduced from 31 ns to about 22 ns at 1% noise.
main_result: The PINN-optimized control strategy achieves at least 0.99 noise-averaged fidelity for benchmark single- and two-qubit gates while substantially reducing gate times.
application_angle: The method is useful for calibrating faster, more noise-robust gate primitives in silicon spin-qubit hardware with fully electrical control.

title: Simulation-guided design of an integrated photonic cavity for frequency-multiplexed Spontaneous Parametric Down Conversion
abstract: Frequency-multiplexed entangled photon pair sources with narrow bandwidths and high pair generation efficiency are a key enabling technology for quantum networking. We present a simulation-based design study of an integrated photonic racetrack resonator source for spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) that simultaneously achieves all three properties. The central result is a simulated set of 90 doubly resonant signal/idler frequency-mode pairs with an effective Schmidt number of 89.62, average bandwidths of 1.08 GHz, a mean free spectral range of 51.9 GHz, and a total internal pair-generation-rate efficiency of 1.16 GHz/mW. Under deterministic wavelength-based splitting, the accessible frequency-state Schmidt number is reduced to 44.93. To support these predictions, we derive a closed-form analytical connection between classical cavity parameters (resonant frequencies, decay rates, coupling coefficients) and the quantum joint spectral amplitude and pair generation rate, extending the dispersive-medium quantization formalism of Raymer to the nonlinear optical cavity case. We demonstrate how classical electromagnetic field simulations can be combined with this analytical framework to predict quantum figures of merit for an integrated photonic source prior to fabrication. Fabrication and experimental validation are left for future work.
one_liner: A simulated integrated cavity-SPDC source supports about 90 narrowband frequency modes for multiplexed entangled-photon generation.
why_it_matters: High-dimensional, narrowband, efficient on-chip photon-pair sources are a key component for scalable quantum networking and multiplexed photonic interfaces.
detailed_summary: The authors present a simulation-guided design for an integrated photonic racetrack resonator performing doubly resonant SPDC with simultaneous narrow bandwidth, high internal efficiency, and many frequency bins. The modeled device yields 90 signal-idler frequency-mode pairs with an effective Schmidt number of 89.62, average bandwidth near 1.08 GHz, mean free spectral range of 51.9 GHz, and total internal pair-generation efficiency of 1.16 GHz/mW; deterministic wavelength splitting reduces the accessible Schmidt number to 44.93. They also derive a closed-form link between classical cavity parameters and the quantum joint spectral amplitude and pair rate, enabling pre-fabrication prediction of quantum performance from electromagnetic simulations.
platform_or_system: integrated photonic racetrack resonator SPDC cavity
main_result: The design study predicts an on-chip cavity SPDC source with roughly 90 narrowband frequency modes and high pair-generation efficiency suitable for frequency-multiplexed entanglement.
application_angle: This provides a concrete route toward chip-scale multiplexed entangled-photon sources for quantum repeater nodes, network interfaces, and spectrally matched quantum memories.

title: Spontaneous optical skyrmion generation by frequency doubling in underdense plasmas
abstract: Optical skyrmions have been widely explored in recent years. Among them, Stokes skyrmions require sophisticated wave engineering or photonic devices for their generation. We show that Stokes skyrmions can emerge spontaneously in second-order harmonic generation in underdense plasmas driven by optical vortices. The nonlinear response produces a structured frequency-doubled field whose polarization texture maps the Poincaré sphere. When plasma inhomogeneities are taken into account, the electron density gradient deforms the skyrmionic texture, enabling topological diagnosis of plasma density.
one_liner: Optical vortices in underdense plasma spontaneously generate frequency-doubled Stokes skyrmions without bespoke photonic structuring.
why_it_matters: It suggests a plasma-based route to topological light generation and uses skyrmion distortion as a diagnostic for plasma density gradients.
detailed_summary: The paper shows that second-order harmonic generation in underdense plasmas driven by optical vortices can spontaneously produce Stokes skyrmions in the polarization texture of the doubled-frequency field. The nonlinear plasma response creates a structured harmonic beam whose Stokes field maps the Poincare sphere, eliminating the need for complex external wave engineering. Including plasma inhomogeneity reveals that electron-density gradients deform the skyrmion texture in a systematic way. This links the topological structure of the emitted light to underlying plasma conditions.
main_result: The authors demonstrate spontaneous generation of frequency-doubled Stokes skyrmions in underdense plasma and show their deformation encodes plasma density gradients.
application_angle: This could enable compact sources of topological structured light and noninvasive optical diagnostics for plasma profiling.

title: Signatures of rare-earth elements in mineralogical form using laser-ablation dual-comb spectroscopy
abstract: Spectroscopy of laser-produced plasmas offers an avenue for real-time, standoff and non-preparatory sensing of rare-earth elements (REEs) within a mineralogical context with applications spanning exploration geology to ore body mapping to ore sorting. Demonstrations of laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy (LIBS) in rock samples have employed both atomic and molecular detection for REE sensors. In this work we evaluate a complementary technique of absorption spectroscopy, realized with dual-frequency combs. This approach provides multi-THz (nm) spectral coverage with simultaneous sub-GHz (pm) resolution. It can improve accuracy and line identification confidence in congested multi-species spectra, which makes it ideal for multi-species evaluations present within mineralogical samples. We analyze REE signatures in calibrated reference materials (CRMs) and a synthesized, REE-containing alloy for atomic, ionic and molecular (oxide) absorptions across three spectral windows. We identify lines from rare-earth and matrix elements, compare absorption line strengths and investigate their temporal evolution. For La I, Sm I and Ce I, preliminary limits of detection from 54-583 ppm are estimated for CRMs, using univariate analysis of selected transitions. Comparing the CRM signatures to those of REEs synthesized in a copper alloy, we observe that most all REE lines appear earlier and disappear faster in the CRM samples. We attribute these dynamics to matrix effects: Among other elements, the increased oxygen content in the CRM could favor molecular formation. For rock samples, observations will once again differ due to grain sizes and bonding mechanisms. Compared to LIBS, we can resolve individual REE and matrix lines with minimal spectral overlap. These proof-of-principle results form a foundation for further development of this laser-based method as a mining sensor.
one_liner: Laser-ablation dual-comb spectroscopy resolves rare-earth atomic, ionic, and oxide signatures in complex mineral-like samples at high resolution.
why_it_matters: It expands standoff mining spectroscopy with better line discrimination in congested spectra and ppm-level detection estimates for several rare-earth species.
detailed_summary: This work applies absorption-based dual-comb spectroscopy to laser-produced plasmas from calibrated rare-earth-containing materials and a synthesized alloy. The method provides multi-THz spectral coverage with sub-GHz resolution, enabling separation of overlapping lines from rare-earth and matrix species across three spectral windows. The authors identify atomic, ionic, and molecular oxide absorptions, track their temporal evolution, and estimate preliminary detection limits of 54-583 ppm for La I, Sm I, and Ce I using selected transitions. They also observe matrix-dependent timing differences between CRM and alloy samples, consistent with oxygen-assisted molecular formation effects.
main_result: The study demonstrates high-resolution identification of rare-earth spectral signatures in mineralogical reference materials with preliminary ppm-scale detection limits.
application_angle: The approach is relevant to exploration geology, ore mapping, and ore sorting where fast, noncontact rare-earth sensing in complex matrices is valuable.

title: Understanding the effects of competing spin-pair dephasing pathways in molecular spins
abstract: Molecular spins offer promise in emerging quantum technologies such as quantum sensing and computing. At low temperatures, nuclear spin-spin interactions affect electron spin coherence lifetimes through pure dephasing. Nuclear-spin noise can originate from spin pairs within a molecule itself, pairs in a surrounding environment system, or pairs in which one spin is on the molecule and the other in the environment. Improving coherence times requires detailed knowledge of the dominant sources of dephasing. Here, we analyze the decoherence behavior of two molecular qubit candidates with various ligands and in different nuclear-spin containing solvents. We apply an electronic-structure enhanced, non-Markovian perturbative theoretical method to connect experimentally comparable dephasing times to individual spin pairs. This analysis allows the development of a computational workflow to strategically improve coherence lifetimes in spin systems where decoherence is dominated by spin-spin dephasing.
one_liner: A theory-guided workflow disentangles which nuclear spin pairs dominate dephasing in molecular spin qubits across ligands and solvents.
why_it_matters: Pinpointing the main microscopic dephasing channels gives a practical path to chemically engineer longer-coherence molecular qubits and spin sensors.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze electron-spin pure dephasing in two molecular qubit candidates, accounting for competing nuclear spin-pair noise from intramolecular, environmental, and mixed molecule-environment pairs. They use an electronic-structure-enhanced non-Markovian perturbative framework to connect experimentally comparable dephasing times to specific microscopic spin-pair contributions. By comparing ligands and nuclear-spin-containing solvents, the study identifies which environments most strongly limit coherence. The result is a computational workflow for strategically modifying chemistry and surroundings to improve spin coherence lifetimes.
platform_or_system: molecular spin qubits in ligand/solvent environments
main_result: The paper identifies and separates competing spin-pair dephasing pathways in molecular qubits using a predictive theory workflow tied to realistic material choices.
application_angle: This can guide molecular design and host-environment selection for longer-lived quantum sensors and spin-based quantum processors.

title: Mitigating Classical Resource Costs in Quantum Error Correction via Generalized qLDPC Predecoding
abstract: Quantum-classical interfaces (QCIs) for fault-tolerant quantum computing must manage simultaneous, real-time decoding across thousands to millions of logical qubits. Scaling these architectures necessitates sharing expensive decoding resources among logical qubits, which introduces severe resource contention within the QCI. While resolving these bottlenecks through efficient resource distribution remains a persistent challenge, lightweight predecoding holds promise to alleviate strain on shared decoding components by decreasing average latency and decoder usage. Notably, research into both decoder allocation and predecoding has been strictly confined to the surface code. With the growing emphasis on general quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes, slower decoding speeds will intensify resource contention, while the inherent complexity of these codes will render manual predecoder design unfeasible. To address this gap, we introduce an automated framework designed to generate predecoders for arbitrary qLDPC codes. These automatically constructed predecoders autonomously process over 90% of the decoding workload, cutting overall decoder utilization by up to 3,963x. This includes a reduction of up to 72.71% in computationally demanding ordered statistics decoding (OSD). Furthermore, we detail a highly efficient, pipelined hardware design that allows for the concurrent decoding of approximately 1,200 bivariate bicycle (BB) code logical qubits using a single FPGA. When implemented as a cryogenic ASIC, the architecture scales to support between 36,000 and 360,000 BB code logical qubits, operating within a 1.5 W power limit at 4 K.
one_liner: Automated predecoders for arbitrary qLDPC codes cut heavy decoder usage by orders of magnitude and map efficiently onto FPGA or cryogenic ASIC hardware.
why_it_matters: It directly addresses the quantum-classical bottleneck in fault tolerance by making large-scale qLDPC decoding much more hardware-feasible.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces an automated framework that generates lightweight predecoders for arbitrary qLDPC codes, rather than relying on hand-designed logic tailored to the surface code. These predecoders handle more than 90% of decoding events and reduce overall decoder utilization by up to 3,963x, including up to 72.71% less ordered statistics decoding workload. The authors also present a pipelined hardware architecture capable of concurrently decoding about 1,200 bivariate bicycle code logical qubits on a single FPGA. In a cryogenic ASIC implementation, they project scaling to roughly 36,000-360,000 logical qubits within a 1.5 W power budget at 4 K.
platform_or_system: qLDPC decoder hardware for FPGA/cryogenic ASIC
main_result: The work demonstrates automated qLDPC predecoding that drastically lowers shared decoder demand and enables dense hardware support for large logical-qubit counts.
application_angle: This is important for practical fault-tolerant quantum computers where classical decoding throughput and cryogenic power are major scaling constraints.

title: Operating a bistable qubit
abstract: Parasitic two-level-system (TLS) defects limit the stability and performance of solid-state quantum processors. Their interaction with a qubit can cause discrete, stochastic shifts of the qubit frequency, making the qubit bistable. We experimentally demonstrate an adaptive protocol for operating a bistable qubit with high fidelity using a classical controller powered by a field-programmable gate array (FPGA). Our "1-bit feedback" protocol estimates the qubit's bistable frequency from only one single-shot measurement, reaching the information limit set by the qubit's intrinsic entropy. We validate the protocol in a superconducting qubit by suppressing TLS-induced Ramsey beating, and deploy it to stabilize gate fidelities over time with approximately 136 kHz estimation bandwidth and a 77% error reduction. Our approach provides a simple, yet fundamentally efficient strategy for mitigating dephasing errors induced by strongly coupled TLS defects, and may enable the operation of large future qubit arrays suffering from few remaining, discrete instabilities.
one_liner: An FPGA-based 1-bit feedback protocol stabilizes a bistable superconducting qubit using only one single-shot measurement per estimate.
why_it_matters: It offers a minimal-latency way to suppress TLS-induced frequency jumps, improving qubit stability without heavyweight calibration overhead.
detailed_summary: The authors study a superconducting qubit whose frequency randomly switches due to coupling to a parasitic two-level-system defect, producing bistability and dephasing. They implement a real-time '1-bit feedback' protocol on an FPGA that infers the qubit's bistable frequency from a single-shot measurement, operating near the information-theoretic limit set by the qubit entropy. Experimentally, the controller suppresses TLS-induced Ramsey beating and stabilizes gate fidelities over time with about 136 kHz estimation bandwidth. The reported performance includes a 77% reduction in error tied to the discrete instability.
main_result: The study demonstrates single-shot adaptive feedback that tracks and mitigates TLS-driven qubit bistability, substantially reducing dephasing-related error.
application_angle: This is directly useful for operating larger superconducting processors where a few residual discrete defects can otherwise dominate instability and calibration drift.

title: OptiLookUp: An Optical ROM-Based Loop up Table Engine for Photonic Accelerators
abstract: Read-only memory (ROM) provides deterministic access to predefined data mappings. Extending ROM concepts to the optical domain enables high-bandwidth, low-latency, and parallel memory access, but realizing compact and reconfigurable optical ROM remains challenging due to loss, wavelength control, and integration constraints. This work presents a high-speed, reconfigurable photonic ROM architecture implemented using integrated microring resonators (MRRs).
