The ROM encodes predefined input-output mappings directly in the spectral response of the photonic devices, enabling deterministic lookup-based operation without dynamic computation during readout. To improve scalability and reduce cumulative insertion loss, the architecture employs compact banked sub-arrays that are selectively addressed through an optical decoding mechanism. Reconfigurability is achieved using transistor-based optical selectors, allowing different ROM banks to be activated without physical light rerouting or interferometric structures. The proposed photonic ROM is designed and evaluated using device-level simulations based on the GlobalFoundries 45SPCLO silicon photonics platform. Simulation results demonstrate reliable operation at data rates up to 12.5 GHz, with stable light-to-current transfer characteristics obtained through integrated photodiode readout. The optical ROM can be used to implement nonlinear activation functions utilised in photonic accelerator architectures, including sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, and exponential mappings.
one_liner: A banked microring-based optical ROM performs deterministic lookup operations for photonic accelerators at simulated rates up to 12.5 GHz.
why_it_matters: It provides a plausible integrated optical memory primitive for fast nonlinear function evaluation in photonic AI hardware.
detailed_summary: This paper proposes OptiLookUp, an optical ROM architecture that encodes fixed input-output mappings into the spectral responses of integrated microring resonators. To limit insertion loss and improve scalability, the design uses banked sub-arrays with optical decoding and transistor-based optical selectors rather than interferometric routing. Device-level simulations on the GlobalFoundries 45SPCLO silicon photonics platform show stable light-to-current transfer through integrated photodiodes at data rates up to 12.5 GHz. The lookup engine is positioned to implement nonlinear activation functions such as sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, and exponential mappings in photonic accelerators.
main_result: The authors demonstrate through simulation a reconfigurable banked optical ROM for deterministic lookup-table computation on an integrated silicon photonics platform.
application_angle: This could reduce latency and energy for function evaluation inside photonic neural-network accelerators and other high-bandwidth optical computing systems.

title: Anderson Localization with Single Photons from a Quantum Emitter
abstract: Anderson localization of light is a fundamental emergent phenomenon in disordered systems. In arrays of coupled waveguides, it suppresses transport and causes photons to remain localized near the excitation site as coupling disorder increases. Here, we experimentally demonstrate Anderson localization using single photons emitted by a single-photon emitter in hexagonal boron nitride at room temperature. Despite the limited temporal coherence of the emitter, the photons undergo pronounced Anderson localization, evidenced by exponentially localized output intensity profiles in disordered waveguide lattices. Beyond the experimental demonstration, we develop a general theoretical framework for wave propagation in disordered tight-binding systems, showing that the configuration-averaged output intensity converges to a stationary spatial distribution at large propagation distances. In the case of off-diagonal disorder, this stationary profile is characterized by an effective localization length that exhibits a robust inverse-variance scaling with the disorder strength. These results establish defect-based room-temperature emitters as practical platforms for studying Anderson localization in integrated photonics and support their use in applications that exploit controlled disorder, including neuromorphic and quantum photonic architectures.
one_liner: Room-temperature single photons from an hBN emitter show clear Anderson localization in disordered waveguide lattices.
why_it_matters: It links practical solid-state single-photon sources to disorder-engineered integrated photonics, relevant for quantum transport studies and unconventional photonic hardware.
detailed_summary: The experiment injects single photons from a room-temperature hexagonal boron nitride defect emitter into disordered coupled-waveguide lattices and observes exponentially localized output intensity profiles. The work also develops a general tight-binding theory showing that configuration-averaged intensity converges to a stationary spatial distribution at long propagation distance. For off-diagonal disorder, the extracted effective localization length follows an inverse-variance scaling with disorder strength. The result is notable because it demonstrates localization physics despite limited photon temporal coherence from a practical emitter.
platform_or_system: hBN single-photon emitter coupled to disordered waveguide arrays
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrate Anderson localization of room-temperature single photons and identify a stationary disorder-averaged localization profile with inverse-variance scaling of localization length.
application_angle: Controlled-disorder photonic chips using deployable room-temperature emitters could support quantum simulation, neuromorphic photonics, and nonstandard integrated quantum hardware.

title: Fault-tolerant measurement-device-independent quantum key distribution with noisy non-Gaussian error correction
abstract: It is well known that the repeater node is an essential ingredient for the future global quantum network, which will enable high-rate private communication and entanglement distribution over very long distances. The near-term repeater architecture uses the measurement-based node that operate without both entanglement and quantum memory, which is the main idea of the measurement-device-independent quantum key distribution (MDI-QKD) protocol. The MDI-QKD protocol removes the trust condition from the inter repeaters, while its continuous variable (CV) version, when proposed, benefited from its deterministic nature, compatible with the classical devices, and shows a high rate for the short-range local area network (LAN). Whilst the theoretical backbone of CV-MDI-QKD protocol is well established, its secure transmission range is yet limited for practical LAN. In this study, we propose an enhanced scheme for the asymmetric CV-MDI-QKD protocol by using Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) oscillators-to-oscillators codes, where both loss error and operation error are suppressed to below the break-even point, without any delays caused by classical heralding signals. In particular, the proposed scheme, which correlates the noises of the data and ancilla via a pair of symplectic transforms, extracts the error syndromes from stabilizer measurements on the ancilla mode and informs the data mode for a corrective displacement. Numerical analysis shows the composable finite-size security of the protocol under the collective Gaussian attack, encompassing noiseless and noisy GKP states, with both wired (i.e., fiber-based) and wireless (i.e., free-space) configurations. In addition, we demonstrate that the residual errors of the GKP code can be further reduced by the concatenation method but has a trade-off between the layers number and the finite GKP squeezing.
one_liner: A GKP-assisted asymmetric CV-MDI-QKD scheme suppresses loss and operation noise while preserving finite-size composable security.
why_it_matters: It pushes measurement-device-independent QKD toward more practical metro and access-network deployment across fiber and free-space links.
detailed_summary: The paper augments asymmetric continuous-variable MDI-QKD with oscillator-to-oscillator GKP error correction that correlates data and ancilla noise through symplectic transforms, then extracts syndromes from stabilizer measurements for corrective displacement. The analysis targets both loss and operation errors and evaluates composable finite-size security against collective Gaussian attacks. Numerical results cover noiseless and noisy GKP states in both fiber and wireless free-space settings, and also study concatenation as a residual-error reduction strategy. The tradeoff between concatenation depth and finite GKP squeezing is made explicit.
main_result: The proposed protocol shows numerically that GKP-based non-Gaussian correction can reduce residual errors below break-even while maintaining composable finite-size security in asymmetric CV-MDI-QKD.
application_angle: This architecture is relevant to near-term secure quantum networking because it extends MDI-QKD performance without requiring trusted relay measurements, long-lived memories, or heralding delays.

title: Quantitative cavity-enhanced photothermal dynamics in TMDC-integrated ultrahigh-Q microcavities
abstract: We investigate photothermal effects in monolayer transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs) integrated with an ultrahigh-Q silica microcavity. Launching a continuous-wave laser into a cavity resonance enables controlled intracavity heating, allowing direct observation of excitonic photoluminescence (PL) modulation. A distinct redshift of the PL peak energy is observed as the pump wavelength is tuned across resonance. This behavior is quantitatively reproduced by a temperature-dependent bandgap model that combines the Varshni relation with the thermo-optic response of the microcavity, from which the local temperature rise can be estimated. We further find that PL collected through a fiber waveguide exhibits spectral and temporal characteristics markedly different from free-space emission, indicating selective coupling of the microcavity to specific excitonic channels. These results provide a quantitative framework for understanding photothermal effects in TMDC-microcavity hybrid systems and offer a versatile approach for all-optical control and probing of thermal states in integrated nanophotonic devices.
one_liner: TMDC monolayers integrated with ultrahigh-Q silica microcavities show quantitatively modeled cavity-enhanced photothermal PL shifts.
why_it_matters: It provides a calibrated route to all-optical thermal control and readout in hybrid nanophotonic devices that mix excitonic materials with ultra-high-Q resonators.
detailed_summary: The study probes monolayer TMDCs on ultrahigh-Q silica microcavities by tuning a CW pump across a cavity resonance and monitoring excitonic photoluminescence modulation. The observed PL redshift is quantitatively reproduced with a temperature-dependent bandgap model combining the Varshni relation and the cavity thermo-optic response, enabling local temperature estimation. Emission collected through a fiber waveguide differs spectrally and temporally from free-space PL, indicating selective coupling to specific excitonic channels. This gives a concrete framework for analyzing thermal dynamics in hybrid cavity-material systems.
platform_or_system: TMDC monolayer integrated with ultrahigh-Q silica microcavity
main_result: The authors quantitatively connect resonance-driven intracavity heating to excitonic PL shifts and identify fiber-coupled signatures of selective excitonic channel coupling.
application_angle: Such calibrated photothermal control can be used for integrated optical tuning, thermal state probing, and hybrid photonic device engineering for emitters and modulators.

title: Centralizing Task-based Approach to Quantum Network Control
abstract: For the last decade, layered stacks have dominated the way of reasoning about architectures for quantum networks. However, layered architectures impose stringent design and timing constraints on quantum networks, adding additional latency to the time required to serve an entanglement generation request. Moreover, increasing delays from the layered approach to network control causes degradation of state, effectively minimizing achievable fidelities. In this work we simulate a resource-centric, task-based approach to quantum network control by utilizing a centralized controller. Using the SeQUeNCe quantum network simulator, we implement the centralized controller which tracks quantum memory availability across all nodes, and schedules objectives in an offline fashion using a priority-based scheduler. We evaluate the performance of this controller on multiple topologies (bottleneck, grid, star, caveman) of significant scale, with varying reservation patterns; thereby we demonstrate the viability of the resource-centric task-based quantum network control framework for scaling. Our simulation results show that the caveman and grid topologies have a higher fraction of delivered requests with low delay compared to the star topology, but with a higher fraction of highly delayed requests as well. Furthermore, we find a linear shift of the CDFs in terms of queue size for all topologies depending on the reservation delay. More interestingly, we conclude that the CDFs of priority queues for the star topology converge fast into saturation for increasing request arrival rates, demonstrating together with the other results that the framework is robust for high load scenarios in quantum networks.
one_liner: A centralized, task-based quantum network controller improves request delivery and remains robust under heavy simulated load.
why_it_matters: Control-plane efficiency directly affects entanglement latency and fidelity, so better schedulers are important for scalable quantum internet architectures.
detailed_summary: Using the SeQUeNCe simulator, the authors implement a centralized controller that tracks quantum memory across nodes and schedules tasks offline with a priority-based resource-centric approach. They benchmark bottleneck, grid, star, and caveman topologies under varying reservation patterns and request rates. The results show topology-dependent tradeoffs in delivered-request fraction and delay, including fast saturation of star-topology priority-queue CDFs under high load and linear CDF shifts with reservation delay. The study argues that abandoning rigid layered control can reduce latency penalties that otherwise degrade entangled-state quality.
platform_or_system: centralized controller for simulated quantum repeater networks
main_result: The simulations demonstrate that a centralized task-based control framework can scale across multiple network topologies while maintaining useful delivery and delay behavior at high load.
application_angle: The work is practically relevant for orchestration software in early quantum networks, where controller design can materially impact throughput, latency, and end-to-end fidelity.

title: Demonstration of a compact optical resonator-based displacement sensing technique with sub-femtometer precision
abstract: We demonstrate sub-femtometer displacement-sensing results achieved with a compact optical resonator-based laser interferometry technique called heterodyne cavity-tracking, intended for local displacement or inertial sensing with ultra-high sensitivity. Displacement sensing at this sensitivity is required for ambitious improvements to current gravitational-wave detectors and to enable future ground- and space-based observatories. The optical topology employs a centimeter-scale dynamic cavity incorporating a proof mass, and the relative length fluctuations of this cavity are measured using a heterodyne readout. The fundamental limits of the technique lie significantly below the femtometer level and are ultimately defined by the coating thermal noise of the cavity mirrors. In our experimental demonstration, we achieve a sub-femtometer per Hz$^{1/2}$ displacement sensitivity for Fourier frequencies above 8 Hz and a sub-picometer per Hz$^{1/2}$ sensitivity above 3 mHz, with the sensitivity at lower frequencies limited by mechanical and temperature-induced noise sources. When the length of the dynamic cavity was intentionally actuated, the technique could track a maximum motion of about 0.6 $μ$m, thereby achieving a dynamic range of roughly ten orders of magnitude in displacement sensing. We thus demonstrate the key features of this scheme - sub-femtometer performance and a dynamic range spanning ten orders of magnitude - in a laboratory setting, paving the way for development of an integrated system. Such a system is a currently unrealized technology that is necessary for precision physics experiments in the coming decades.
one_liner: A compact heterodyne cavity-tracking interferometer achieves sub-femtometer displacement sensitivity with about ten orders of dynamic range.
why_it_matters: It advances a compact sensing architecture for precision metrology, inertial sensing, and future gravitational-wave instrumentation.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses a centimeter-scale dynamic optical cavity with a proof mass and heterodyne cavity-tracking readout to sense cavity-length fluctuations. It reports sub-femtometer per root-hertz sensitivity above 8 Hz and sub-picometer per root-hertz sensitivity above 3 mHz, with low-frequency performance limited by mechanical and thermal disturbances. Intentional actuation shows tracking of motions up to about 0.6 micrometers, giving an overall dynamic range near ten orders of magnitude. The authors identify coating thermal noise as the ultimate fundamental limit for the technique.
platform_or_system: compact heterodyne cavity-tracking optical resonator sensor
main_result: The system demonstrates sub-femtometer displacement sensitivity together with large-range tracking, validating a compact resonator-based interferometric sensing scheme.
application_angle: This sensing approach is attractive for integrated local displacement and inertial sensors needed in gravitational-wave detectors and other precision physics platforms.

title: Analytical two-pulse control of universal single-qubit gates in rotational ultracold NaCs molecules
abstract: Complex control protocols and sensitivity to experimental imperfections have limited the practical implementation of quantum gate operations. Here, we present an analytical framework for universal single-qubit gates using rotational states of ultracold NaCs molecules. By encoding qubits in the lowest rotational energy levels, we employ a first-order Magnus expansion to derive closed-form unitary evolution from an optimized two-pulse sequence. This approach establishes precise amplitude and phase conditions for arbitrary single-qubit rotations, achieving gate fidelities above 0.9999 in numerical simulations. We further demonstrate that complex multi-gate sequences, including phase-locked operations, can be executed with minimal population leakage into auxiliary states. The time-dependent molecular orientation is shown to faithfully encode both the gate truth table and coherence dynamics, enabling practical gate tomography via weak-field polarization detection. Our analytical method is also applicable to other molecules and physical platforms, offering a potential path to high-fidelity, scalable molecular quantum processors.
one_liner: An analytical two-pulse protocol realizes universal single-qubit gates in ultracold NaCs rotational states with simulated fidelity above 0.9999.
why_it_matters: It reduces control complexity for molecular qubits while preserving very high fidelity, a key requirement for scalable neutral-molecule processors.
detailed_summary: The paper encodes qubits in the lowest rotational states of ultracold NaCs molecules and derives closed-form gate dynamics from an optimized two-pulse sequence using a first-order Magnus expansion. This yields explicit amplitude and phase conditions for arbitrary single-qubit rotations, with numerical fidelities exceeding 0.9999. The authors also show low leakage during longer phase-locked gate sequences and propose weak-field polarization detection of time-dependent molecular orientation for gate tomography. The framework is designed to transfer to related molecular and driven multilevel platforms.
main_result: The authors derive and validate an analytical two-pulse control method for universal single-qubit operations with very high simulated fidelity and minimal leakage.
application_angle: Simpler analytic pulse recipes and built-in tomography observables could make molecular-qubit hardware easier to calibrate and scale in real experiments.

title: Quantum Resource Estimation for Minimising Energy Grid Losses
abstract: Distribution network reconfiguration (DNR) can minimise power losses by identifying the optimal topology of the electricity grid. Determining the minimum loss configuration is NP-hard, and classical optimisation methods struggle to scale to real-world distribution grids. This paper explores the use of gate-based quantum computing to solve DNR for power loss reduction. We formulate DNR as a higher-order unconstrained binary optimisation (HUBO) problem, avoiding the need for auxiliary variables, thereby reducing the required number of qubits. This is applied to a real medium voltage (MV) network operated by Alliander, a Dutch distribution system operator (DSO). For each biconnected component in the network graph, we construct the corresponding HUBO, derive the cost and mixer operators, and determine the number of required logical qubits and rotation gates. These are then mapped to physical qubits and execution time estimates using quantum resource estimation (QRE). The results suggest that the quantum resource requirements depend not only on component size but also on structural characteristics such as connectivity and cyclicity. Overall, the novelty of this work lies in directly framing the optimisation problem as a HUBO, applying it to real-world MV networks, and performing a QRE to assess future feasibility.
one_liner: This work estimates the qubits, gates, and runtime needed to attack real distribution-network reconfiguration via gate-based quantum optimization.
why_it_matters: It ties a concrete power-grid optimization problem to explicit quantum hardware resource forecasts, clarifying where practical advantage may or may not emerge.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates distribution network reconfiguration for power-loss minimization as a higher-order unconstrained binary optimization problem, avoiding auxiliary variables and thereby reducing logical qubit overhead. Using a real medium-voltage network from Alliander, the authors derive cost and mixer operators for each biconnected component and translate those circuits into logical and physical resource estimates. Their quantum resource estimation shows that required qubits, rotation gates, and execution time depend not only on instance size but also on graph structure such as connectivity and cyclicity.
platform_or_system: gate-based quantum optimization for MV power-grid networks
main_result: The authors provide component-level logical-to-physical resource estimates for solving real DNR instances and show that structural graph features strongly affect quantum cost.
application_angle: It gives utilities and quantum architects a realistic baseline for whether future fault-tolerant hardware could address grid-loss reduction tasks.

title: Harnessing DEN models for quantum computing tasks on neutral atom QPUs
abstract: We present our work on effectively representing unit-disk graphs on the registers of neutral atom quantum machines. Specifically, we aimed to embed graphs corresponding to proteins and cellular antenna networks into unit-disk graphs, ensuring compatibility with the registers of two real QPUs: Orion Alpha by PASQAL and Aquila by QuEra. To address machine-specific constraints, we made adjustments and integrated Distance Encoder Networks (DEN) from our previous work. Despite these challenges, we successfully embedded up to 76% of protein-representing graphs for a quantum machine learning classification task on the Aquila QPU, and all subgraphs derived from 90 antenna geographical positions in Turin, Italy, on the Orion Alpha QPU. In the latter case, the graphs represented instances of the graph coloring problem, which we tackled using the hybrid quantum-classical algorithm BBQ-mIS. These promising results underscore the effectiveness and versatility of our embedding approach for representing unit-disk graphs on neutral atom quantum computers across diverse applications.
one_liner: A DEN-based embedding workflow maps application graphs onto real neutral-atom QPU registers for protein and wireless-network tasks.
why_it_matters: Embedding is a central bottleneck for neutral-atom computing, and this work shows application-driven graph instances can be placed on real hardware at useful scale.
detailed_summary: The authors adapt Distance Encoder Networks to embed problem graphs as unit-disk graphs compatible with neutral-atom hardware constraints on QuEra Aquila and PASQAL Orion Alpha. They target two application classes: protein-derived graphs for quantum machine-learning classification and antenna-network subgraphs for graph coloring with the hybrid BBQ-mIS algorithm. Experimentally, they report successful embedding of up to 76% of protein-representing graphs on Aquila and all tested subgraphs from 90 antenna locations on Orion Alpha.
platform_or_system: neutral-atom QPUs with unit-disk graph embeddings
main_result: The method successfully places substantial fractions of real application graphs onto Aquila and Orion Alpha while respecting machine-specific geometric constraints.
application_angle: It improves the path from domain datasets to executable neutral-atom workloads in quantum ML and combinatorial optimization.

title: Probing the robustness of various self-testing protocols for mulipartite entangled states
abstract: Device-independent certification of multipartite entangled states plays a central role in a wide range of practical applications, including quantum networks, conference key agreement, and verifiable distributed quantum computation. A particularly important class of multipartite entangled states is the class of Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) states. Many Bell operators have been proposed to self-test GHZ states. However, in practical scenarios, due to imperfections and the finite collection of statistics, the observed statistics do not satisfy the ideal self-testing relations. Hence, it becomes essential to investigate and compare the robustness of the different self-testing protocols. In this work, we investigate the robustness of self-testing schemes constructed from Bell operators due to Svetlichny and Mermin--Ardehali--Belinskii--Klyshko (MABK), using the analytic operator-inequality framework developed by Kaniewski [\href{https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevLett.117.070402}{Phys. Rev. Lett. 117, 070402 (2016)}]. We derive lower bounds on the extractable fidelity as a function of the observed value of these Bell operators. Although these protocols self-test the same underlying state, they exhibit markedly different levels of robustness. By comparing the resulting fidelity bounds, we demonstrate that the self-testing scheme based on the Svetlichny's Bell operator is the more robust among the two. Our results thus identify the Svetlichny operator based self-testing protocol as the most favorable candidate for device-independent certification of GHZ states in realistic, noisy experimental scenarios.
one_liner: This paper compares GHZ self-testing protocols and finds Svetlichny-based certification more robust than MABK under realistic noise.
why_it_matters: Robust device-independent certification is essential for deployable multipartite quantum networks, especially when finite statistics and imperfections dominate.
detailed_summary: Using Kaniewski's analytic operator-inequality framework, the authors derive lower bounds on extractable fidelity as a function of observed Bell-operator violations for multipartite GHZ-state self-testing. They compare protocols based on the Svetlichny and MABK Bell operators, both of which target the same underlying entangled state. The resulting fidelity bounds show a clear robustness advantage for the Svetlichny construction in noisy, nonideal experimental regimes.
main_result: Svetlichny-operator self-testing yields stronger robust fidelity guarantees than MABK for noisy multipartite GHZ-state certification.
application_angle: The result directly informs protocol choice for practical quantum-network tasks such as conference key agreement and verifiable distributed computation.

title: BBQ-mIS: a parallel quantum algorithm for graph coloring problems
abstract: Among the limitations of current quantum machines, the qubits count represents one of the most critical challenges for porting reasonably large computational problems, such as those coming from real-world applications, to the scale of the quantum hardware. In this regard, one possibility is to decompose the problems at hand and exploit parallelism over multiple size-limited quantum resources. To this purpose, we designed a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm, i.e., BBQ-mIS, to solve graph coloring problems on Rydberg atoms quantum machines. The BBQ-mIS algorithm combines the natural representation of Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problems onto the machine Hamiltonian with a Branch&Bound (BB) approach to identify a proper graph coloring. In the proposed solution, the graph representation emerges from qubit interactions (qubits represent vertexes of the graph), and the coloring is then retrieved by iteratively assigning one color to a maximal set of independent vertexes of the graph, still minimizing the number of colors with the Branch&Bound approach. We emulated real quantum hardware onto an IBM Power9-based cluster, with 32 cores/node and 256 GB/node, and exploited an MPI-enhanced library to implement the parallelism for the BBQ-mIS algorithm. Considering this use case, we also identify some technical requirements and challenges for an effective HPC-QC integration. The results show that our problem decomposition is effective in terms of graph coloring solutions quality, and provide a reference for applying this methodology to other quantum technologies or applications.
one_liner: BBQ-mIS is a parallel hybrid algorithm that uses Rydberg-atom MIS primitives plus Branch-and-Bound to solve graph coloring under qubit limits.
why_it_matters: It addresses a core near-term constraint by decomposing larger application graphs across limited quantum resources while retaining solution quality.
detailed_summary: The method encodes graph structure directly into qubit interactions on neutral-atom hardware, exploiting the natural maximum independent set mapping of Rydberg systems. Graph coloring is then recovered iteratively by assigning colors to maximal independent vertex sets while a Branch-and-Bound layer minimizes the total number of colors. The implementation uses MPI-enabled parallelism on IBM Power9 emulation infrastructure and shows effective decomposition with good coloring quality, while also surfacing requirements for HPC-quantum integration.
platform_or_system: Rydberg-atom graph-coloring with hybrid HPC-QC execution
main_result: The proposed parallel decomposition strategy produces effective graph-coloring solutions while fitting the size constraints of current quantum hardware.
application_angle: It offers a practical template for scaling combinatorial workloads onto near-term neutral-atom platforms through hybrid parallel orchestration.

title: Sensitivity limits of non-stationary quantum sensors
abstract: The concept of the dissipative quantum limit (DQL) was first put forward in 1980s and was analyzed in detail much later in Ref. [Phys. Rev. A 103, 043721 (2021)] for the particular case of stationary (invariant with respect to a shift of time) systems. Here we extend that analysis to the general non-stationary case.
one_liner: The paper extends dissipative quantum-limit analysis from stationary sensors to the general non-stationary sensing regime.
why_it_matters: Many realistic sensors are time-dependent, so sharpening fundamental sensitivity bounds in non-stationary operation is important for experimental design.
detailed_summary: Building on prior work that established the dissipative quantum limit for stationary systems, this paper generalizes the framework to sensors without time-translation invariance. The focus is on determining how dissipation constrains achievable sensitivity in dynamically varying measurement settings. By extending the limit theory beyond stationary assumptions, the work provides a more general foundation for evaluating realistic quantum sensor performance.
main_result: The authors generalize dissipative quantum-limit sensitivity analysis to the fully non-stationary case.
application_angle: It helps experimentalists judge whether time-dependent sensor protocols can offer real gains before hitting fundamental dissipation-driven limits.

title: Neural optimization for quantum architectures: graph embedding problems with Distance Encoder Networks
abstract: Quantum machines are among the most promising technologies expected to provide significant improvements in the following years. However, bridging the gap between real-world applications and their implementation on quantum hardware is still a complicated task. One of the main challenges is to represent through qubits (i.e., the basic units of quantum information) the problems of interest. According to the specific technology underlying the quantum machine, it is necessary to implement a proper representation strategy, generally referred to as embedding. This paper introduces a neural-enhanced optimization framework to solve the constrained unit disk problem, which arises in the context of qubits positioning for neutral atoms-based quantum hardware. The proposed approach involves a modified autoencoder model, i.e., the Distances Encoder Network, and a custom loss, i.e., the Embedding Loss Function, respectively, to compute Euclidean distances and model the optimization constraints. The core idea behind this design relies on the capability of neural networks to approximate non-linear transformations to make the Distances Encoder Network learn the spatial transformation that maps initial non-feasible solutions of the constrained unit disk problem into feasible ones. The proposed approach outperforms classical solvers, given fixed comparable computation times, and paves the way to address other optimization problems through a similar strategy.
one_liner: A neural embedding framework using Distance Encoder Networks improves constrained unit-disk placement for neutral-atom quantum architectures.
why_it_matters: Better hardware-aware embedding directly expands which real optimization problems can be represented on neutral-atom processors within practical runtimes.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a modified autoencoder, the Distance Encoder Network, together with an Embedding Loss Function to solve the constrained unit-disk problem arising in neutral-atom qubit placement. The network learns spatial transformations that map initially infeasible graph layouts into feasible embeddings that satisfy geometry-driven hardware constraints. Under comparable compute-time budgets, the approach outperforms classical solvers and suggests a reusable neural strategy for related hardware-mapping problems.
main_result: The DEN-based optimizer finds feasible constrained unit-disk embeddings more effectively than classical alternatives at matched runtime budgets.
application_angle: It can reduce one of the main compilation bottlenecks for neutral-atom QPUs, improving utilization on application-relevant instances.

title: Experimental demonstration of a coherent detector blinding attack on a real CV-QKD system
abstract: Continuous-variable quantum key distribution provides a theoretical unconditionally secure solution to distribute symmetric keys among users in a communication network. However, the practical devices used to implement these systems are intrinsically imperfect, and, as a result, open the door to eavesdropper attacks. In this work, we present a novel implementation of a coherent detector blinding attack, in which the eavesdropper hinders the capability of the receiver to properly estimate the channel parameters, hiding the impact of their collective attack. Our results show that excess noise in excess of 2.5 SNU can be reliably hidden by the eavesdropper, thus demonstrating the feasibility of the attack. We also discuss how our attack strategy can be further improved to allow for even stronger attacks (by using more advanced modulation formats), and propose some countermeasures to prevent it.
one_liner: A real continuous-variable QKD setup is experimentally shown vulnerable to a coherent detector-blinding attack that hides large excess noise.
why_it_matters: It exposes a practical security gap in deployed CV-QKD hardware and motivates concrete countermeasures for trusted quantum communication links.
detailed_summary: The paper experimentally demonstrates a coherent detector-blinding attack against a real CV-QKD receiver. By impairing the receiver's channel-parameter estimation, the eavesdropper can conceal the disturbance from a collective attack while keeping the system apparently within acceptable operating conditions. The authors report that excess noise above 2.5 shot-noise units can be reliably hidden, and they outline stronger variants using more advanced modulation formats. They also discuss countermeasures aimed at restoring trustworthy parameter estimation and detector security.
main_result: The authors show in experiment that a coherent detector-blinding strategy can reliably hide more than 2.5 SNU of excess noise in a practical CV-QKD setup.
application_angle: The work is directly relevant to fielded quantum-secure links because it identifies an exploitable implementation loophole and informs hardening of commercial CV-QKD systems.

title: Quantum Spin Liquid State of a Dual-Species Atomic Array on Kagome Lattice
abstract: Dual-species arrays of ultracold neutral atoms have recently attracted increased interest due to the ability to independently control different atomic species and tune the interatomic interactions. This capability provides additional flexibility essential for both quantum computing and quantum simulation. In this work we theoretically investigate a quantum spin liquid (QSL) state to be simulated on a programmable quantum simulator based on a dual-species atomic array, arranged on a Kagome lattice. The Kagome lattice is formed by corner sharing triangles. This specific spatial arrangement enhances the competing interactions between atoms and is often considered as a model for realising QSL states. When the atoms are excited into Rydberg states, long-range interactions result in Rydberg blockade. The geometric frustration of the Kagome lattice, combined with the Rydberg blockade, drives the system into exotic phases with topological order and long-range entanglement. To drive an array into the QSL state, we use a sweep-quench-sweep protocol, when the atoms are quasiadiabatically excited into Rydberg state with individually controlled detuning from the resonance for each atomic species. The filling fraction, indicating emergence of a QSL state, is represented by a density of Rydberg excitations. We identified the conditions required for QSL state in a dual-species array with non-uniform interaction energies. We calculated the correlation length and studied the mutual information as a function of the size of the subset of the system. The existence of a topological order was proved by estimating the Kitaev-Preskill topological quantum entanglement entropy.
one_liner: A dual-species Rydberg atom Kagome array is proposed as a programmable route to quantum spin liquid physics with topological order.
why_it_matters: It maps a realistic neutral-atom hardware architecture to an exotic many-body phase relevant for scalable quantum simulation and correlated-state engineering.
detailed_summary: This work theoretically studies a programmable dual-species neutral-atom array arranged on a Kagome lattice and driven into Rydberg states. Using a sweep-quench-sweep protocol with species-dependent detunings, the authors identify conditions under which geometric frustration and nonuniform blockade interactions support a quantum spin liquid regime. They analyze filling fraction, correlation length, and mutual information, and estimate the Kitaev-Preskill topological entanglement entropy to verify topological order. The study ties controllable hardware parameters to signatures of long-range entanglement in a nontrivial many-body state.
main_result: The paper identifies a control protocol and parameter regime where a dual-species Kagome atom array can realize signatures consistent with a quantum spin liquid and topological order.
application_angle: It provides a concrete recipe for using programmable neutral-atom hardware to emulate topological quantum matter beyond simple benchmark Hamiltonians.

title: A Critical Comment on 'Entropy Computing: A Paradigm for Optimization in Open Photonic Systems'
abstract: In this article, we take a close look at Entropy Quantum Computing (EQC), a computational paradigm developed by Quantum Computing Inc. (QCi), which deviates from mainstream quantum computing by embracing rather than battling environmental noise and decoherence arXiv:2407.04512 . In their words this approach purports EQC as an open quantum system that turns "entropy into super-power fuels of its computing engine". We show that some of the claims in the main article can be made more rigorous, and yet these are still not good enough to beat state of the art classical algorithms on conventional classical computers. Note that these conclusions reflect the technology's current early stage of development and are not meant to discourage its pursuit. Continued rigorous exploration is necessary to fully assess the long-term viability and potential advantages of this distinct computational approach.
one_liner: A technical critique argues that current entropy-computing claims in open photonic systems do not yet outperform leading classical optimization methods.
why_it_matters: It sharpens evaluation standards for unconventional photonic computing platforms and helps separate hardware novelty from demonstrated computational advantage.
detailed_summary: The authors critically examine the Entropy Quantum Computing paradigm proposed for optimization in open photonic systems. They reformulate several claims more rigorously and then assess whether the strengthened statements imply an advantage over conventional classical algorithms. Their conclusion is that, at the current stage of the technology, the case for superior optimization performance is not established. The paper is methodological rather than experimental, focusing on careful benchmarking criteria for noisy photonic optimization hardware.
main_result: The main result is that a more rigorous reading of current entropy-computing claims still does not support a demonstrated advantage over state-of-the-art classical optimization methods.
application_angle: For applied photonic computing, the paper is useful as a due-diligence reference on what evidence is needed before claiming practical performance gains.

title: Adversarial Effects on Expressibility and Trainability in Distributed Variational Quantum Algorithms
abstract: Distributed quantum algorithms offer a promising pathway to scale variational quantum algorithms beyond the constraints of noisy intermediate-scale quantum hardware. However, existing approaches implicitly assume a trusted entanglement-sharing layer across quantum processors. We show that this assumption introduces a fundamental vulnerability: adversarial perturbations of shared entanglement induce structured gate-level noise that directly impacts quantum learning. We develop a framework that maps entanglement-level perturbations to gate-level noise via an explicit Kraus representation. To quantify their impact, we introduce Kraus expressibility, a metric that generalizes unitary expressibility to noisy quantum channels. We then establish a trade-off between Kraus expressibility and trainability of noisy quantum circuits through gradient variance analysis. Our analysis reveals that an adversary can manipulate Kraus expressibility to maintain sufficiently large cost gradients (avoiding barren plateaus) while systematically biasing optimization toward incorrect solutions. We validate these findings through numerical simulations, demonstrating adversarial degradation of expressibility and trainability.
one_liner: Adversarially perturbed shared entanglement is shown to bias distributed VQAs through structured noisy channels without simply killing trainability.
why_it_matters: It identifies a security and robustness issue for modular quantum processors, where compromised entanglement links can subtly corrupt learning outcomes.
detailed_summary: The paper studies distributed variational quantum algorithms under adversarial perturbations of the inter-processor entanglement resource. It derives an explicit Kraus-channel mapping from entanglement-level tampering to gate-level noise and introduces Kraus expressibility as a noisy analogue of unitary expressibility. Through gradient-variance analysis, the authors establish a trade-off between expressibility and trainability in the noisy setting. Simulations show an attacker can preserve nonvanishing gradients while steering optimization toward incorrect solutions, making the attack difficult to detect by standard barren-plateau diagnostics.
platform_or_system: distributed variational quantum processors with shared entanglement
main_result: The authors show that adversarial manipulation of shared entanglement can induce structured noise that biases distributed VQA optimization while retaining apparently trainable gradients.
application_angle: This matters for networked and modular quantum hardware because secure entanglement distribution becomes part of the trusted computing stack, not just the communication layer.

title: Deterministic positioning of circular Bragg gratings using atomic force lithography for high-performance quantum dot light sources
abstract: Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) grown by molecular beam epitaxy are excellent quantum emitters, but their random spatial distribution hinders deterministic coupling to optical microcavities. We demonstrate a room-temperature atomic force microscopy (AFM)-assisted nano-oxidation lithography technique enabling QD positioning with a radial displacement of $51(28)$ nm. Free-standing asymmetric circular Bragg gratings incorporating AFM-positioned GaAs QDs exhibit a $245$-fold photoluminescence enhancement and fine-structure splitting (FSS) comparable to bulk QDs. Polarization-resolved spectroscopy and finite-difference time-domain simulations show robust emission for displacements up to $50$ nm (Stokes parameter $\lvert S \rvert < 0.05$ ). The devices display stable FSS and polarization imbalance below $5 \, \%$ , confirming precise, reproducible alignment and potential for high fidelity devices. This scalable approach enables deterministic integration of high-performance QDs with photonic cavities, advancing practical quantum light sources for quantum information technologies.
one_liner: AFM-assisted lithography deterministically aligns GaAs quantum dots to circular Bragg gratings with strong emission enhancement and low polarization error.
why_it_matters: It advances scalable fabrication of high-performance solid-state single-photon sources by turning randomly positioned emitters into reproducible cavity-coupled devices.
detailed_summary: The authors use room-temperature AFM nano-oxidation lithography to place photonic circular Bragg grating structures relative to individual GaAs quantum dots with a radial displacement of 51(28) nm. Free-standing asymmetric gratings fabricated this way yield a 245-fold photoluminescence enhancement while maintaining fine-structure splitting comparable to bulk dots. Polarization-resolved measurements and FDTD simulations indicate robust operation for emitter offsets up to 50 nm, with |S| < 0.05 and polarization imbalance below 5%. The results demonstrate precise and repeatable emitter-cavity registration compatible with high-fidelity quantum-light devices.
platform_or_system: GaAs quantum dots in circular Bragg grating cavities
main_result: The work demonstrates deterministic QD-to-cavity alignment that produces 245-fold photoluminescence enhancement with low polarization imbalance and preserved fine-structure splitting.
application_angle: This fabrication route is directly relevant to manufacturable bright quantum-light sources for photonic quantum computing, networking, and metrology.

title: Resource-efficient parallel entanglement generation for multinode quantum networks via time-bin multiplexing
abstract: Nonlocal entanglement generation among multiple remote quantum nodes provides a critical foundation for a variety of counterintuitive quantum applications. The exponential loss of photons transmitting over optical fibers sets an upper limit for entangling these quantum nodes. Here, we propose a resource-efficient and parallel protocol for entangling multiple remote quantum nodes via time-bin multiplexing. The transmission of a single photon with qudit-encoding in the time-bin mode enables entangling multiple stationary qubits in parallel, when single photons and individual stationary qubits interfaces are used and photon-state modulations are properly introduced before subsequently impinging the photon into each interface. Our protocol can generate parallel multipartite entanglement among ($N\geq3$) quantum nodes with the dimension of the photonic time bins independent of $N$, exponentially reducing the requirements for the coherence time of the stationary qubits and for the complexity of the photonic modulations. These distinct features make our protocol particularly advantageous for the development of multinode quantum networks.
one_liner: A time-bin-multiplexed single-photon protocol can entangle many remote nodes in parallel with fixed photonic dimension overhead.
why_it_matters: It offers a more resource-efficient path to scalable multinode quantum networking under realistic photon-loss and memory-coherence constraints.
detailed_summary: This paper proposes a protocol for generating nonlocal entanglement across multiple remote quantum nodes using a single photon encoded in time-bin qudit modes. By modulating the photonic state before successive interactions with individual stationary-qubit interfaces, the scheme entangles several nodes in parallel rather than sequentially. A key technical claim is that the required time-bin dimension does not grow with node number N for N>=3, which reduces coherence-time demands on memories and simplifies photonic control. The approach targets multipartite entanglement generation in lossy fiber-linked architectures with better resource scaling than straightforward alternatives.
platform_or_system: time-bin photonic links interfaced with stationary qubits
main_result: The main result is a parallel multinode entanglement protocol whose photonic time-bin dimension remains independent of the number of nodes while reducing memory and modulation overhead.
application_angle: The scheme is practically relevant for distributed quantum networks because it improves entanglement-generation efficiency under the severe loss and coherence limits of real optical links.

title: Theoretical Calculation of Electron Transfer Between Calcium Ground-State Atoms and Rydberg Atoms
abstract: We calculated the electronic interaction associated with the exchange of an electron between an atom of calcium excited to a Rydberg state ($n\sim 10-15$) and another, neighbouring calcium atom in its ground state. In this range the Rydberg states have an energy that is comparable to the electron affinity of Ca, enabling resonant or near resonant charge transfer at large internuclear separations (200-700 $a_0$). We calculated the interaction strength while systematically and critically assessing the approximations made, and found it to be large, ranging from $10^{-5}$ $E_h$ (70 GHz) to $10^{-8}$ $E_h$. Charge transfer is thus expected to be efficient and to significantly affect the molecular dynamics at a range of internuclear distances where ultralong range Rydberg molecules also exist.
one_liner: Theory predicts strong long-range resonant charge transfer between ground-state and Rydberg calcium atoms.
why_it_matters: It identifies a sizable interaction channel in alkaline-earth Rydberg systems that could strongly shape neutral-atom quantum devices and ultralong-range molecular physics.
detailed_summary: The paper calculates electron-exchange coupling between a calcium atom in a Rydberg state with principal quantum number around 10-15 and a neighboring ground-state calcium atom. In this regime, the Rydberg energies are resonant or nearly resonant with the Ca electron affinity, enabling charge transfer over large separations of roughly 200-700 Bohr radii. The authors critically assess approximations in the interaction model and find coupling strengths spanning about 10^-5 to 10^-8 Hartree, corresponding to very large frequency scales. The results imply efficient charge transfer in the same distance range where ultralong-range Rydberg molecules form, potentially altering dynamics in alkaline-earth Rydberg platforms.
main_result: The study predicts efficient long-range charge transfer with interaction strengths from about 10^-5 to 10^-8 Hartree for calcium Rydberg-ground-state atom pairs.
application_angle: Understanding and controlling this transfer channel is relevant for designing stable alkaline-earth neutral-atom hardware and exploiting or avoiding molecular-state effects.

title: Real-time Krylov Diagonalisation for Open Quantum Systems
abstract: In this chapter we present how real-time quantum subspace methods can be modified to simulate open quantum systems in Lindblad form. We apply these methods to a two-photon driven superconducting Kerr resonator and show how they allow to estimate the Liouvillian gap in the Kerr Cat qubit regime. This is based on work done between November 2025 and January 2026 and on the talk given in early February 2026 at the Kwekfest 2026 conference, to celebrate L.C. Kwek's lifetime achievements.
one_liner: Real-time Krylov subspace methods are extended to Lindblad dynamics and used to extract Liouvillian gaps in a Kerr-cat device.
why_it_matters: It offers a hardware-relevant simulation route for dissipative bosonic qubits, where open-system spectra directly govern memory lifetimes and control performance.
detailed_summary: The work reformulates real-time quantum subspace or Krylov diagonalization methods so they can simulate open quantum systems described by Lindblad master equations. The approach is applied to a two-photon driven superconducting Kerr resonator operating in the Kerr-cat-qubit regime. Using the modified subspace method, the authors estimate the Liouvillian gap, a central quantity controlling dissipative relaxation and stabilization timescales. This positions real-time Krylov methods as a practical reduced-order tool for analyzing driven-dissipative superconducting hardware.
main_result: The authors show that real-time Krylov diagonalization can be adapted to Lindblad dynamics and used to estimate the Liouvillian gap of a Kerr-cat qubit system.
application_angle: Fast characterization of dissipative bosonic qubits can guide operating-point selection and error-bias engineering in superconducting quantum hardware.

title: Telecom-band quantum memory with chlorine defects in silicon carbide
abstract: Realization of quantum memory with a photonic interface in the telecommunication bands in a wafer-scalable platform is a central requirement for long-distance quantum networks. Silicon carbide (SiC) provides a technologically mature host for integrated quantum photonics, yet only a limited number of defects combine spin functionality with telecom emission. Here we report on chlorine-based defects in 4H-SiC as a platform for telecom-band quantum memory. The emission of these defects spans the entire telecommunication range with zero-phonon lines in the O- and C-bands and a Debye-Waller factor of up to $39 \, \%$. Time-resolved photoluminescence measurements reveal a short excited-state lifetime in the sub-nanosecond range. We demonstrate that these defects are spin-active even at room temperature, exhibiting optically detected magnetic resonances (ODMR) in the sub-GHz frequency range. Using ODMR spectroscopy and Ramsey interferometry, we resolve the hyperfine structure arising from the interaction with $^{35}\mathrm{Cl}$ nuclear spins. The ODMR spectra exhibit complex behaviour in an external magnetic field due to mixing of electron-nuclear spin states, which is well reproduced by our simulations. The spin relaxation and coherence times are in the sub-microsecond range, limited by rapid quenching of the ODMR contrast and attributed to charge-state metastability. The combination of telecom-band emission, coherent spin control and compatibility with wafer-scale fabrication positions Cl-related defects in SiC as a promising platform for chip-scale quantum memories with spin-photon interfaces operating in the fiber-optic telecommunication windows.
one_liner: Chlorine defects in 4H-SiC combine telecom-band emission with room-temperature spin activity for chip-scale quantum memory prospects.
why_it_matters: This is a rare wafer-compatible defect platform with telecom photonic access and coherent spin control, directly targeting deployable quantum repeater and memory nodes.
detailed_summary: The paper reports chlorine-related defects in 4H-SiC with zero-phonon lines spanning the O- and C-band telecom windows and Debye-Waller factors up to 39%. Time-resolved photoluminescence shows sub-nanosecond excited-state lifetimes, while ODMR demonstrates room-temperature spin activity in the sub-GHz range. Ramsey and ODMR spectroscopy resolve hyperfine coupling to 35Cl nuclear spins, and the field dependence is reproduced by electron-nuclear spin-mixing simulations. Spin relaxation and coherence remain sub-microsecond and are limited by charge-state metastability, but the combined photonic and spin properties make the defect a strong telecom-memory candidate.
main_result: The work demonstrates room-temperature spin-active chlorine defects in 4H-SiC with telecom-band emission, resolved hyperfine structure, and coherent spin control.
application_angle: A wafer-scalable SiC defect emitting in fiber telecom bands is highly relevant for integrated quantum memories, network transducers, and compact spin-photon nodes.

title: Ensemble Engineering to Overcome Destructive Cancellation in Quantum Measurements
abstract: On noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices, expectation values of many observables are obtained through sampling-based approximations to trace-like quantities. A central limitation of this approach is destructive cancellation under near-uniform ensembles, which can render physically relevant signals effectively unresolvable. Here we show that this limitation is not simply statistical, but reflects a structural mismatch between ensemble weights and the operator-dependent sign structure of the measured correlator. We introduce a general framework for mitigating this effect through quantum ensemble engineering, in which the sampling distribution is encoded directly in the prepared quantum state. By reformulating correlators in a basis-resolved representation, we make the origin of cancellation explicit and derive strategies for aligning ensemble weights with operator structure. We realize this approach using two complementary circuit constructions: a Grover-type amplitude amplification protocol that provides a structure-aligned benchmark, and an oracle-free shallow circuit designed for near-term hardware constraints. Using the infinite-temperature correlation function as a representative setting, we demonstrate on IBM quantum processors with up to 20 qubits that engineered ensembles expose operator-resolved contributions that are strongly suppressed under uniform averaging. We identify a practical tradeoff between amplification strength and noise robustness, extend the framework to multi-qubit diagonal observables, and outline a path toward non-diagonal generalizations. These results position ensemble engineering as a new tool for improving measurement efficiency in near-term quantum algorithms.
one_liner: Engineered quantum ensembles suppress destructive cancellation and improve observable estimation on NISQ processors up to 20 qubits.
why_it_matters: It addresses a concrete measurement bottleneck in near-term quantum computing by reshaping sampling distributions to recover signals hidden by uniform averaging.
detailed_summary: The authors show that weak measured signals can arise from a structural mismatch between uniform ensemble weights and the sign structure of the target correlator, not just finite sampling noise. They introduce quantum ensemble engineering, where the sampling distribution is encoded in the prepared state to align with operator structure in a basis-resolved picture. Two implementations are developed: a Grover-style amplitude-amplification benchmark and an oracle-free shallow circuit better suited to hardware. Experiments on IBM processors with up to 20 qubits demonstrate stronger recovery of operator-resolved contributions for infinite-temperature correlation functions and multi-qubit diagonal observables.
main_result: The paper demonstrates that structure-aligned ensemble preparation can expose correlator contributions strongly suppressed under uniform sampling on up to 20-qubit hardware.
application_angle: The technique can reduce measurement overhead in near-term algorithms for dynamics, materials, and many-body correlators without requiring deeper fault-tolerant circuits.

title: Attosecond-Stable Two-Dimensional Spectroscopy by a Sagnac-Based Modulating System and a sub-4-fs Continuum Source
abstract: We present a two-dimensional electronic spectroscopy (2DES) platform driven by a novel Coherent Loop-based Integrated Modulating and Beamsplitting System (CLIMBS). Coupled with an octave-spanning multiple-plate continuum (MPC) source, CLIMBS enables broadband, phase-coherent measurements with attosecond-level time delay precision. Its Sagnac-inspired, nearly common-path geometry provides exceptional long-term phase stability without active feedback, eliminating beam walk-off and preserving beam pointing during delay scans. Delay calibration using spectrally resolved interferometric fringes yielded a wedge angle in excellent agreement with the designed geometry, confirming precise, linear coherence time control. The MPC technique generates broadband excitation pulses spanning 550--980 nm and temporally compressed to 3.7 fs. This bright, few-cycle source enables simultaneous interrogation of widely separated electronic and vibronic transitions, with high temporal and spectral resolution, allowing 2DES to capture vibronic cross peaks, energy-transfer pathways, and undistorted ground-state bleaching (GB), stimulated emission (SE), and excited-state absorption (ESA) features across a broad spectral window. System performance was benchmarked on chlorophyll-a in methanol, where the excitation bandwidth fully covers the $Q_x$ and $Q_y$ bands, ensuring distortion-free spectra. The nearly collinear configuration of CLIMBS eliminates beam walk-off during delay scanning, supports ultrabroadband few-cycle 2DES enabled by the high-brightness MPC source, and maintains attosecond-level phase stability, providing a simple and robust platform for high-fidelity multidimensional spectroscopy.
one_liner: A Sagnac-based 2DES setup reaches attosecond delay stability with a 3.7 fs octave-spanning continuum source.
why_it_matters: It delivers a robust ultrabroadband multidimensional spectroscopy platform for resolving coupled electronic and vibronic dynamics with minimal drift or scan artifacts.
detailed_summary: The work introduces CLIMBS, a coherent-loop integrated modulating and beamsplitting system with a nearly common-path Sagnac-like geometry for two-dimensional electronic spectroscopy. Combined with a multiple-plate continuum source spanning 550-980 nm and compressed to 3.7 fs, it enables phase-coherent broadband measurements with attosecond-level delay precision without active feedback. Interferometric fringe calibration confirms linear and accurate coherence-time control, while the nearly collinear geometry suppresses beam walk-off during scans. Benchmark measurements on chlorophyll-a show undistorted GB, SE, and ESA features and full coverage of widely separated transitions including Qx and Qy bands.
main_result: The authors demonstrate attosecond-stable, ultrabroadband 2DES using a Sagnac-inspired modulating system and a 3.7 fs octave-spanning excitation source.
application_angle: The platform is directly useful for high-fidelity studies of energy transfer, vibronic coupling, and ultrafast photoactive materials where stability and bandwidth are limiting factors.

title: Space-Time Tradeoffs of Pauli-Based Computation in Distributed qLDPC Architectures
abstract: Pauli-based computation (PBC) provides a universal framework for executing fault-tolerant quantum algorithms using Pauli measurements and magic states. In monolithic architectures, the serialized nature of PBC directly ties runtime to a circuit's T-gate count, making it slow on metrics like circuit depth. However, in distributed quantum computing (DQC), the primary bottleneck is remote Bell pair generation. We investigate the tradeoff between error-correcting code block size and execution time of PBC within the Q-Fly architecture at intermediate scale, limiting individual node capacities to reflect near-term constraints while supplying abundant network nodes to minimize routing and compilation effects. We find that large qLDPC code blocks outperform the surface code baseline in terms of execution time by up to an order of magnitude when evaluated against quantum optimization algorithms. By moving groups of qubits to free nodes to bypass the sequential bottleneck of PBC, the large-block architecture minimizes network operations and achieves faster overall execution. This demonstrates that PBC is a competitive model in the distributed regime, establishing it as a practical compilation baseline for qLDPC systems before invoking more efficient transversal or homological gates.
one_liner: Distributed qLDPC architectures can make Pauli-based computation markedly faster than surface-code baselines in networked quantum systems.
why_it_matters: It reframes a key compilation tradeoff for modular fault-tolerant machines, where network costs rather than T-count dominate practical runtime.
detailed_summary: The paper studies how Pauli-based computation behaves in distributed quantum computing architectures where remote Bell-pair generation is the central bottleneck. Within the Q-Fly setting, the authors compare code-block size and execution time under near-term node-capacity constraints but abundant network nodes. They find that larger qLDPC blocks can outperform a surface-code baseline by up to an order of magnitude in runtime for quantum optimization workloads. The speedup comes from moving qubit groups to free nodes to bypass PBC serial bottlenecks while reducing the number of network operations required.
main_result: The study shows that large-block qLDPC implementations of Pauli-based computation can achieve up to order-of-magnitude runtime gains over distributed surface-code baselines.
application_angle: The results inform architecture and compiler choices for modular quantum computers, especially photonic-networked or repeater-linked systems where entanglement distribution is costly.

title: The power of entanglement in distributed quantum machine learning
abstract: The quantum internet aims to interconnect distant devices and enable large-scale computation through distributed quantum algorithms. One of the key obstacles is communication latency during computation. Even separations of a few hundred kilometers introduce millisecond-scale delays, which exceed the coherence times of many solid-state qubit platforms. In contrast, entanglement can be established beforehand and used as a practical resource to reduce communication complexity between remote nodes. Here we examine the utility of entanglement in distributed quantum machine learning for binary classification tasks. Drawing an analogy with the CHSH game, we show that entanglement improves classification accuracy across all datasets considered. We also find that excessive entanglement may degrade performance by reducing the effective dimension of the parameter space. This highlights the importance of using an appropriate amount and structure of entanglement in data embedding. Our findings bridge nonlocality and machine-learning advantage, providing a pathway toward distributed quantum computation beyond coherence-time constraints.
one_liner: Pre-shared entanglement boosts binary classification accuracy in distributed quantum machine-learning nodes under latency constraints.
why_it_matters: It shows how entanglement can trade communication for performance in networked quantum computing where link delays exceed qubit coherence times.
detailed_summary: The paper studies distributed quantum machine learning for binary classification when remote nodes suffer communication latency incompatible with short coherence times. Using a CHSH-inspired construction, it shows that pre-established entanglement improves classification accuracy across all tested datasets relative to non-entangled schemes. The analysis also finds that too much or poorly structured entanglement can hurt performance by shrinking the effective parameter-space dimension. This identifies entanglement structure as a tunable resource for practical networked quantum algorithms.
main_result: Pre-shared entanglement consistently improves distributed binary-classification performance, but the gain depends on using the right amount and structure of entanglement.
application_angle: The result is relevant for quantum internet and modular hardware settings where offline entanglement can reduce online communication overhead during inference.

title: One-dimensional polarization-hybrid photonic crystal molecules
abstract: Photonic molecules, i.e. artificial structures composed of coherently coupled optical cavities, are paradigmatic systems for investigating fundamental phenomena across photonics, quantum optics and topological physics. In recent years, photonic integrated circuits have emerged as a particularly powerful platform for their realization, exploiting also additional synthetic dimensions afforded by the degrees of freedom of light. To date, however, photonic molecule implementations have relied almost entirely on geometries defined by spatial coupling and lattice symmetries rather than polarization. Here, we introduce a fundamentally new class of photonic molecules in which polarization is exploited as the primary dimension in the device response. By harnessing fundamental guided-mode couplings sustained by engineered Bragg gratings in photonic waveguides, we establish a new paradigm to access in 1D formats the coupled-resonator physics traditionally associated with higher-dimensional or free-space systems, demonstrating prototypical devices which can support Fano resonances or resonance-splitting for signals in the telecom band. Besides corroborating the theoretical predictions, experimental realizations in thin film lithium niobate open new prospects for the further exploration of novel reconfigurable topological, non-Hermitian and quantum photonic circuits, relying on the intrinsic nonlinear and electro-optic functionalities of this platform.
one_liner: A 1D lithium-niobate photonic-crystal molecule uses polarization as the main synthetic dimension to realize telecom-band coupled-cavity physics.
why_it_matters: It adds a compact, reconfigurable route to integrated photonic molecules with direct relevance to topological, nonlinear, and quantum telecom circuits.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce one-dimensional polarization-hybrid photonic crystal molecules in which polarization, rather than spatial layout, defines the key coupling dimension. Engineered Bragg gratings in photonic waveguides create guided-mode couplings that reproduce coupled-resonator phenomena such as Fano resonances and resonance splitting in the telecom band. They experimentally realize the concept on thin-film lithium niobate, matching the theoretical predictions. The platform naturally supports electro-optic tuning and nonlinear functionality for more advanced circuits.
main_result: They experimentally demonstrate polarization-defined 1D photonic molecules with telecom-band Fano resonances and resonance splitting in integrated lithium niobate devices.
application_angle: This architecture is promising for compact reconfigurable quantum and classical photonic processors, especially telecom-band communication and tunable on-chip light manipulation.

title: Variational Joint Magnetometry and Gradiometry on Dipolar Spin Chains
abstract: Estimating a uniform magnetic field B0 and its spatial gradient g on a dipolar-coupled spin chain calls for a multiparameter figure of merit. The GHZ state, optimal for single-parameter Heisenberg-limited sensing, has a rank-one quantum Fisher information matrix with det(Q^GHZ) = 0 at every chain length N, ruling it out for the two-parameter problem. We present a variational framework that takes det(F) as the objective and a hardware-motivated layered dipolar circuit as the ansatz. Both encoding generators are diagonal in the computational basis, which reduces the search for the quantum Fisher information benchmark to a probability-simplex optimization and yields a tractable best-found benchmark det(Q*) against which variational performance is compared. The same diagonal structure makes the classical Fisher information depend only on basis-state probabilities under any single-qubit decoder, so encoder and decoder parameters are co-trained with CMA-ES in a single run. Decoder optimization past fixed Ramsey adds at most a few percentage points across the grid, in contrast to the persistent decoder gains seen in our prior single-parameter work. Variational probes at L = 3 reach 0.92 of the best-found benchmark at N = 5, a 4.2x SQL advantage in det(F), and concentrate on a four-string motif of the two GHZ extrema and two half-chain-flip strings whose structure follows from the Dicke-sector decomposition of the two generators.
one_liner: Variationally optimized dipolar spin-chain probes enable joint magnetic-field and gradient sensing well beyond the standard quantum limit.
why_it_matters: It targets a realistic multiparameter sensing task where GHZ probes fail, and delivers hardware-motivated states with strong practical gains.
detailed_summary: The work addresses simultaneous estimation of a uniform magnetic field and its spatial gradient in dipolar-coupled spin chains, using det(F) as the multiparameter objective. Because GHZ states give a rank-one quantum Fisher matrix for this task, the authors optimize layered dipolar-circuit ansatz states and jointly train encoder and single-qubit decoder settings with CMA-ES. For N=5 and depth L=3, the learned probes reach 92% of a best-found quantum benchmark and achieve a 4.2x SQL advantage in det(F). The optimal states concentrate weight on four characteristic bit-string components tied to the generators' Dicke-sector structure.
main_result: A shallow variational dipolar ansatz attains near-benchmark joint field-and-gradient sensitivity and exceeds the SQL by 4.2x in the determinant figure of merit.
application_angle: The method is directly relevant to quantum magnetometers that must estimate both field strength and spatial variation using realistic shallow control circuits.

title: Quantum metrology of mixed states via purification
abstract: We introduce new formulations of the quantum Cramér-Rao bound (QCRB) and the Holevo Cramér-Rao bound (HCRB) in multi-parameter quantum metrology via purification, where we show their values for any mixed state are connected to that for its purification with nuisance parameters introduced on the environmental system. Using this technique, we develop a new method for asymptotically attaining either the HCRB or twice the QCRB for arbitrary mixed states using random purification channels and individual measurements.
one_liner: Purification-based metrology reformulates mixed-state QCRB and Holevo bounds and gives an asymptotic route to attain them with random channels.
why_it_matters: It offers a constructive framework for approaching optimal multiparameter precision in realistic noisy, mixed-state sensing settings.
detailed_summary: The paper recasts the quantum and Holevo Cramér-Rao bounds for arbitrary mixed states through purifications that introduce nuisance parameters on an environment. This links the estimation limits of the mixed state to those of a purified larger system, yielding a new analytic viewpoint on multiparameter precision bounds. Building on that connection, the authors propose random purification channels with individual measurements that asymptotically attain either the Holevo bound or twice the QCRB. The result is general and applies beyond special state families.
main_result: The authors derive purification-based forms of the QCRB and HCRB and show an asymptotic measurement strategy that reaches the HCRB or 2xQCRB for arbitrary mixed states.
application_angle: This framework is useful for designing near-optimal sensing protocols in noisy platforms where mixed states are unavoidable, including solid-state and optical sensors.

title: Phase-Reference Control of Steady-State Entanglement in Open Quantum Systems
abstract: We show that steady-state entanglement in open quantum systems is controlled by the phase reference of a phase-sensitive reservoir. Using a covariance-matrix approach for Gaussian-preserving dynamics, we demonstrate that purely local, phase-sensitive dissipation can generate entanglement when combined with coherent coupling. The steady state exhibits a finite entangled region with an optimal squeezing strength that maximizes both the magnitude and thermal robustness of entanglement. We find that coherent coupling does not enhance entanglement monotonically, but instead regulates the conversion of local squeezing into nonlocal correlations. Importantly, the coupling dependence is controlled by the phase reference of the squeezed reservoir: phase-locked (rotating-frame) and laboratory-frame implementations yield qualitatively distinct steady states and entanglement structure. These results establish phase-sensitive reservoir engineering as a controllable route to steady-state entanglement in continuous-variable systems. Steady-state entanglement in phase-sensitive open systems depends explicitly on the reservoir phase reference and is not invariant under changes of that reference.}
one_liner: Phase-sensitive local dissipation can generate and tune steady-state entanglement, with the reservoir phase reference dictating the entanglement structure.
why_it_matters: It turns reservoir phase into a control knob for robust continuous-variable entanglement generation in open photonic systems.
