detailed_summary: The paper formulates the mapping of QUBO problems to neutral-atom hardware as a constrained unit disk graph embedding problem, where geometric atom placement determines adjacency through the blockade radius. A neural-network-based method transforms an initial, infeasible embedding into a valid arrangement that matches the target problem graph. This directly addresses the physical compilation step needed to realize Ising-like interactions in 2D or 3D atom arrays. Reported experiments show better performance than Gurobi on the tested instances.
main_result: The authors show that a neural embedding method can generate feasible unit-disk graph layouts for QUBO instances more effectively than a Gurobi baseline.
application_angle: Better embedding pipelines can increase usable problem sizes and reduce compilation overhead for neutral-atom quantum optimization workflows.

title: Harnessing a 256-qubit Neutral Atom Simulator for Graph Classification
abstract: Neutral atom platforms are analogue quantum simulators that offer the possibility to map graphs onto a 2D qubit register using programmable Rubidium atoms arrays, whose valence electrons' energy state is used as qubits, using optical tweezers. This makes it possible to implement algorithms for solving graph combinatorial optimization and Quantum Machine Learning (QML) tasks, such as graph classification. However, the restrictions of real hardware, as well as the very low number of publicly available machines, make such implementation non-trivial. In this work, we manage to compute the Quantum Evolution Kernel (QEK) to extract the features from graphs of the PROTEINS dataset using the 256-qubits Aquila platform (available through AWS) and then we apply classical Machine Learning (ML) techniques for the final classification. The method is benchmarked against classical kernels, resulting in slightly better performance, proving the effectiveness of the method, even in the case of a noisy quantum simulator.
one_liner: A 256-qubit Aquila neutral-atom simulator is used to compute quantum evolution kernels for graph classification on PROTEINS.
why_it_matters: It is a rare hardware-scale QML demonstration on a publicly accessible neutral-atom platform, showing a concrete graph-learning use case.
detailed_summary: This work maps graph instances to a 256-qubit Aquila neutral-atom platform and computes a Quantum Evolution Kernel to extract features for graph classification. The resulting quantum features are passed to classical machine-learning models for final prediction on the PROTEINS dataset. The authors benchmark against classical kernels and report slightly improved performance despite hardware restrictions and simulator noise. The paper is notable for using a large publicly available neutral-atom system for a nontrivial QML workflow.
main_result: The study demonstrates graph classification using quantum evolution kernel features generated on Aquila, with slightly better performance than classical kernel baselines.
application_angle: It suggests neutral-atom simulators can support practical hybrid quantum-ML pipelines for structured data tasks under realistic hardware constraints.

title: Scheduling Entanglement Flows in Multi-channel Quantum Networks
abstract: This paper addresses resource allocation for entanglement distribution in multi-channel quantum networks. A system model is proposed that integrates a multi-channel quantum network architecture with heterogeneous link characteristics and user-centric entanglement request handling, including queuing and retry mechanisms. Classical allocation methods for assigning channels and quantum processors to generate entanglement between end nodes are implemented, including the Dynamic Efficient algorithm, Static Efficient algorithm, and the Success Enhancement algorithm. In addition, a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)-based allocation approach driven by a reward function is proposed. These methods are evaluated through multi-slot simulations using metrics such as request delay, total number of successful entanglement requests, network capacity utilization, and the entanglement request handling rate. The results show that Dynamic Efficient achieves the lowest delay, while Success Enhancement improves the number of successful requests through multipath allocation. The PPO-based method provides the best overall balance by improving capacity utilization and achieving both low delay and a high number of successful entanglement requests.
one_liner: Classical heuristics and PPO are compared for scheduling entanglement generation across heterogeneous multi-channel quantum networks.
why_it_matters: Efficient entanglement-flow scheduling is central to turning quantum networks from link demos into usable shared infrastructure.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a system model for multi-channel quantum networks with heterogeneous links, user-centric entanglement requests, queues, and retries. It evaluates Dynamic Efficient, Static Efficient, and Success Enhancement allocation schemes against a PPO-based policy over multi-slot simulations using delay, throughput, utilization, and request-handling metrics. Dynamic Efficient minimizes delay, Success Enhancement improves successful requests via multipath allocation, and PPO provides the best overall tradeoff. The work therefore treats entanglement distribution as an operational scheduling problem rather than only a physical-layer one.
platform_or_system: multi-channel quantum network with heterogeneous links
main_result: The PPO-based scheduler achieves the best overall balance of low delay, high capacity utilization, and strong entanglement-request success in the simulated network setting.
application_angle: These scheduling methods are relevant for operating future quantum internets and distributed quantum-computing backbones with scarce entanglement resources.

title: Quantum Proper Scoring Rules: Minimax Estimation and Resource-Theoretic Advantages
abstract: We generalize proper scoring rules to the quantum domain, replacing probability distributions with density operators. We define Quantum Value Functionals via operator convex generators and establish a complete duality theory yielding proper quantum scoring rules. We derive minimax optimal bounds for quantum state tomography under McCarthy-type incentives, proving a Quantum Cramér-Rao-McCarthy Bound that explicitly links minimax risk to the curvature of the generating function and the Quantum Fisher Information. We quantify the economic value of quantum resources (coherence, entanglement, adaptivity) in forecasting tasks, establishing scaling separations between classical and quantum estimation strategies. Our results guide the design of quantum sensors, incentive-compatible quantum data markets, and robust quantum machine learning protocols.
one_liner: Introduces proper scoring rules for quantum states and derives minimax tomography bounds tied to quantum Fisher information.
why_it_matters: It links estimation incentives, achievable sensing precision, and quantum resources in a framework relevant to sensor design and quantum data evaluation.
detailed_summary: The paper extends proper scoring rules from classical distributions to density operators using operator-convex generators, yielding a dual characterization of proper quantum scoring rules. It derives a Quantum Cramér-Rao-McCarthy bound that connects minimax estimation risk to generator curvature and the quantum Fisher information. The authors also quantify how coherence, entanglement, and adaptivity improve forecasting and estimation tasks relative to classical strategies.
main_result: It establishes a complete theory of quantum proper scoring rules and proves minimax tomography bounds explicitly governed by quantum Fisher information and scoring-rule curvature.
application_angle: The framework can guide incentive-compatible benchmarking of quantum sensors, tomography pipelines, and quantum-data markets where forecast quality must be scored rigorously.

title: Band-Selective LDOS Engineering of Yb/Er Upconversion: an Electromagnetic-Kinetic Diagnostic Framework
abstract: A central challenge in plasmonic upconversion is coupling between near-field engineering at the pump wavelength and local-density-of-optical-states (LDOS) engineering at the emission wavelengths. Here we show that a corrugated SU8/Au/Al2O3 grating coated with a dense NaYF4:Yb(20%),Er(5%) upconversion nanoparticle (UCNP) monolayer realises a band-selective platform: a broad plasmonic resonance near 670 nm aligned with the red 4F9/2 -> 4I15/2 Er3+ transition modulates the red decay rate by +/-15% as a function of the Al2O3 spacer thickness d, while the green 2H11/2 / 4S3/2 -> 4I15/2 transition is experimentally invariant (|k/k_ref - 1| < 1% across all d). The pump field at 980 nm is monotonically suppressed below the free-space reference (<f_exc> from 0.27 to 0.48 between d = 5 and 25 nm), so observables cleanly probe the emission-side LDOS without pump-side interference. We rationalise these results with a coupled electromagnetic-kinetic framework combining full-wave FDTD pump enhancement and orientation-averaged Purcell factors with a six-level Yb/Er rate-equation model separating radiative, intrinsic nonradiative and environment-induced nonradiative channels. The framework reproduces the 670 nm extinction resonance, the +/-10-15% red-band decay-rate modulation, and the monotonic decrease of the green/red ratio with d, but predicts a monotonic red-band trend that misses the experimental dip at d = 15 nm and over-predicts a green-band reduction (k/k_ref^550 approx. 0.73 vs. 1.00). Ridge-tip smoothing (h_round in {0, 5, 10} nm) shifts Purcell factors by only 1-3%, ruling out apex shape as the dominant cause. The framework thus serves as a diagnostic tool, isolating the green-band discrepancy as needing corrections beyond the half-ellipse model - likely grain-boundary damping in the evaporated gold or extra non-radiative channels at 550 nm not in the six-level kinetic model.
one_liner: A plasmonic grating platform isolates emission-side LDOS effects in Yb/Er upconversion and explains them with a coupled EM-kinetic model.
why_it_matters: Band-selective control of upconversion decay channels is useful for engineering nanoscale emitters for optical interfaces, diagnostics, and spectrally selective photonic devices.
detailed_summary: Using a corrugated SU8/Au/Al2O3 grating coated with NaYF4:Yb,Er nanoparticles, the authors realize a red-selective plasmonic resonance near 670 nm while keeping the green channel effectively unchanged. Experiments show red decay-rate modulation of about plus/minus 15% with spacer thickness, whereas the green decay rate stays within 1% of reference and the 980 nm pump is monotonically suppressed, cleanly isolating emission-side LDOS effects. A full-wave FDTD plus six-level rate-equation model reproduces most trends and pinpoints residual discrepancies to missing loss channels or imperfect metal-response modeling.
main_result: The device demonstrates selective modulation of the red Er3+ emission decay rate without measurable change in the green-band decay, enabling a clean LDOS diagnostic.
application_angle: This offers a practical route to spectrally selective emission engineering in nanophotonic light sources, wavelength converters, and optical interface materials.

title: Real-time Surface-Code Error Correction Using an FPGA-based Neural-Network Decoder
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC) is essential for achieving low error rates required for fault-tolerant quantum computation. In stabilizer-based codes such as the surface code, errors are inferred from repeated syndrome measurements and corrected by a classical decoder. To prevent error accumulation, decoding must be performed with both high throughput and low latency to keep pace with the QEC cycle and enable real-time feedback for universal logical operations. Here we report a hardware-integrated control architecture featuring an FPGA-based neural-network (NN) decoder and experimentally demonstrate real-time surface-code (distance-3) QEC on a superconducting quantum processor. The system achieves a deterministic closed-loop latency of 550 ns, including 124 ns for NN decoding, enabling feedback corrections within a 1.25 us QEC cycle. We show that real-time decoding and feedback correction achieve logical performance comparable to offline decoding while maintaining robustness against varying error conditions. We further demonstrate mid-circuit feedback correction in non-Clifford logical circuits, where Pauli-frame updating alone becomes insufficient. Our results establish a low-latency hardware architecture for embedded QEC control and provide a pathway towards scalable fault-tolerant quantum computing systems.
one_liner: An FPGA neural-network decoder performs real-time distance-3 surface-code correction on superconducting qubits with 550 ns closed-loop latency.
why_it_matters: Low-latency embedded decoding is a core systems requirement for scalable fault-tolerant processors, especially when mid-circuit feedback is needed.
detailed_summary: The work integrates a neural-network decoder into FPGA control hardware for repeated surface-code syndrome processing on a superconducting quantum processor. The full feedback loop is deterministic at 550 ns, with 124 ns spent in decoding, fitting inside a 1.25 microsecond QEC cycle. The authors show logical performance comparable to offline decoding and demonstrate mid-circuit feedback in non-Clifford logical circuits where simple Pauli-frame tracking is insufficient.
platform_or_system: superconducting surface-code processor with FPGA control
main_result: It experimentally demonstrates real-time closed-loop surface-code error correction and feedback within a 1.25 microsecond cycle using an FPGA-based neural decoder.
application_angle: The result strengthens the hardware stack needed for practical fault-tolerant quantum computers by showing embedded decoding fast enough for live correction and logical control.

title: Transit Noise in Spin Squeezing Experiments with Coated Rubidium Vapor Cell
abstract: Spin squeezing can suppress quantum projection noise via interparticle entanglement, therefore enabling measurement sensitivities beyond the standard quantum limit. In practice, however, the Gaussian and finite intensity profiles of the optical probe beam induce spatially inhomogeneous atom-light interactions. As polarized atoms move within a vapor cell, they experience position-dependent optical intensities, generating transit noise that limits spin squeezing performance. Here, we investigate the transit noise in a coated rubidium vapor cell through combined theoretical analysis and experimental measurements. By varying the probe beam diameter, we quantify the dependence of transit noise on beam size and atomic Larmor frequency. Our results show that, for a vapor cell with fixed dimensions, the transit noise increases as the probe beam spot area decreases. Moreover, when the Larmor frequency is below the characteristic linewidth of the transit noise, the noise contribution becomes larger. We further calculated and measured spin squeezing for different beam sizes and found an experimental difference of $2.7 \pm 0.2$ dB between 2~mm and 0.6~mm, similar to the theoretical prediction of $3.0 \pm 0.3$ dB. Theoretical analysis under conditions of stronger squeezing shows that transit noise becomes an even more critical limiting factor. These results provide practical guidance for optimizing probe beam parameters and suppressing transit noise in spin squeezing experiments.
one_liner: Measures and models transit noise in coated Rb vapor-cell spin squeezing, showing strong dependence on probe size and Larmor frequency.
why_it_matters: Transit noise is a concrete experimental limiter in squeezed-spin sensors, so quantifying it directly informs how to push atomic metrology beyond the SQL.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes how atomic motion through a finite Gaussian probe beam produces spatially varying atom-light coupling and excess noise in coated rubidium vapor cells. By varying beam diameter and Larmor frequency, the authors show transit noise rises as probe area shrinks and worsens when the Larmor frequency falls below the transit-noise linewidth. They also measure a 2.7 plus/minus 0.2 dB squeezing difference between 2 mm and 0.6 mm beams, consistent with a 3.0 plus/minus 0.3 dB theoretical prediction.
main_result: It identifies probe-beam geometry and spin-precession frequency as key control parameters for transit-noise-limited squeezing in vapor-cell experiments.
application_angle: The study provides actionable optimization rules for atomic magnetometers and spin-squeezed photonic interfaces that rely on room-temperature vapor cells.

title: Quantum Realizability of Probabilistic Theories Stable under Teleportation
abstract: The classification of general probabilistic theories (GPTs) whose CHSH value is stable under arbitrary rounds of teleportation and entanglement swapping was obtained in Dmello and Gross work and consists of seven families, indexed by characters of the Klein four-group $K_4$, the cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}_4$, and the dihedral group $D_4$. The question of which of these families admits a realization within standard quantum mechanics was left open. In this work we resolve this question completely. Using elementary representation theory, we prove that exactly two families are quantum-realizable, namely $χ^{K_4}_{1234}$ and $χ^{D_4}_{125}$, while the remaining five admit no quantum realization.
one_liner: Completely classifies which teleportation-stable probabilistic theories can actually be realized within standard quantum mechanics.
why_it_matters: It sharply separates physically implementable teleportation-like correlation structures from mathematically allowed but non-quantum ones, tightening foundations for quantum communication.
detailed_summary: Building on a prior classification of GPT families whose CHSH value remains stable under arbitrary teleportation and entanglement swapping, the authors ask which families admit a standard quantum realization. Using representation-theoretic arguments, they show that only two families, one tied to K4 and one to D4, are quantum realizable, while the other five are excluded. The result closes a concrete realizability question for teleportation-stable nonclassical theories.
main_result: Exactly two of the seven previously identified teleportation-stable GPT families are shown to be realizable in quantum mechanics.
application_angle: This constrains the space of candidate correlation models relevant to teleportation, repeater protocols, and communication primitives built on entanglement swapping.

title: Scalar and Vector Airborne Platform Calibration Using Quantum and Classical Magnetometers and Inertial Sensors
abstract: Airborne magnetometry requires rigorous calibration to isolate geomagnetic signals from sensor errors and platform magnetic fields. This magnetic compensation is needed for applications like geophysical exploration and magnetic anomaly navigation. The standard approach utilizes a quantum scalar Optically Pumped Magnetometer (OPM) and a less sensitive fluxgate vector sensor for attitude information. This configuration typically results in a scalar approximation of the platform field. Advancements in high-sensitivity Diamond Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) vector magnetometers now enable a re-evaluation of the standard hardware configuration and full vector calibration models. We show through rigorous theoretical analysis that scalar calibration models are robust to misalignment. Vector calibration models, however, are intrinsically first-order sensitive to attitude errors, irrespective of the accuracy of the magnetic field measurements. These errors arise from inaccurate representation of the background field direction in the body frame, and can amplify small orientation errors into noticeable calibration residuals. Using realistic sensor models and flight trajectories, we evaluate different sensor configurations for magnetic calibration and assess the use of onboard Inertial Navigation Systems (INS) as an independent attitude reference to enable stable compensation. Our results suggest that quantum vector magnetometers like NV sensors are not sufficient to solve the attitude bottleneck for airborne vector magnetic calibration. However, as a single sensor capable of providing both absolute field and directional measurements, they may offer benefits regarding sensor placement, synchronization, and scalar calibration accuracy.
one_liner: Assesses airborne magnetic calibration using OPMs, NV vector magnetometers, and inertial sensors, exposing an attitude-error bottleneck.
why_it_matters: It clarifies where quantum magnetometers help in deployed sensing systems and where navigation-grade attitude knowledge still dominates calibration quality.
detailed_summary: The paper studies scalar and vector airborne magnetic calibration with realistic sensor and trajectory models for quantum and classical magnetometer configurations. It proves scalar calibration is robust to sensor misalignment, whereas vector calibration is intrinsically first-order sensitive to attitude errors because the background-field direction is imperfectly represented in the body frame. Simulations indicate that even high-sensitivity NV vector magnetometers do not remove this bottleneck, though they remain attractive as compact combined absolute-plus-directional field sensors.
main_result: The central result is that vector magnetic compensation is fundamentally limited by attitude-reference errors, not simply by magnetic sensor sensitivity.
application_angle: This directly informs aircraft and drone magnetometry architectures for geophysical surveying and magnetic-anomaly navigation by showing where quantum sensors add value and where better inertial referencing is required.

title: Geometrical control of topology with orbital angular momentum modes
abstract: We study how the topological properties of a one-dimensional staggered lattice, loaded into states with orbital angular momentum $l=1$, can be controlled simply by tuning the relative angle between sites. The original system under consideration can be depicted as a Creutz ladder model when unwrapping the different state circulations in a synthetic dimension. Depending on the hopping strengths of the chain, different topological regimes may be accessed by changing the ladder angle, as determined by the value of the winding number of the chain. We analytically and numerically explore the different available regimes, and determine the number of topologically protected edge states that exist in each case. We also study the emergence of band inversion across topological transitions and show that it agrees with the winding number calculations, thus serving as an additional topological marker. Then, we propose a realistic experimental implementation in a photonic waveguide system, where the topological transition manifests as a sudden change of the behavior of the propagation of light in the system.
one_liner: OAM-loaded staggered lattices can switch topological phase by simply rotating the relative angle between sites.
why_it_matters: It gives a geometry-only tuning knob for topological photonic transport and edge-state control in experimentally realistic waveguide platforms.
detailed_summary: The paper maps a 1D staggered lattice populated with orbital-angular-momentum l=1 states onto an effective Creutz ladder by unwrapping opposite circulations into a synthetic dimension. By varying the relative angle between sites, the authors drive transitions between distinct topological regimes characterized by different winding numbers and numbers of protected edge states. They further track band inversion across the transition and show consistency between inversion behavior and the winding-number diagnosis. A photonic-waveguide implementation is proposed where the transition should appear as an abrupt change in light propagation behavior.
main_result: The authors show that the lattice angle alone can tune winding number, band inversion, and the count of topologically protected edge states in an OAM-based staggered lattice.
application_angle: This offers a practical route to reconfigurable topological photonic devices where transport and edge localization are controlled through geometry rather than complex material changes.

title: Network-Mediated Capacitive Coupling Drives Fast OTOC Saturation in Superconducting Circuits
abstract: We investigate the dynamical and spectral consequences of capacitance-network-mediated interactions in superconducting transmon arrays beyond effective nearest-neighbor descriptions. While weak coupling regimes are well captured by an effective nearest-neighbor interacting models, we show that increasing capacitive connectivity induces a pronounced departure from this approximation in dynamical observables. Using Out-of-Time-Ordered Correlators (OTOCs), we demonstrate that such network-mediated couplings significantly accelerate operator scrambling, leading to rapid saturation compared to the nearest-neighbor limit. This dynamical crossover is accompanied by a shift in spectral statistics away from Poissonian behavior toward level repulsion, with the ratio parameter remaining intermediate between Poisson and Gaussian Orthogonal Ensemble (GOE) limits. This indicates the emergence of partial ergodicity rather than fully developed quantum chaos. As this behavior arises within experimentally realistic regimes of current superconducting transmon devices, identifying when network-mediated couplings qualitatively alter information dynamics is directly relevant for scalable superconducting architectures.
one_liner: Capacitance-network couplings in transmon arrays drive much faster operator scrambling than nearest-neighbor models predict.
why_it_matters: It identifies a realistic hardware effect that can qualitatively change dynamics, calibration targets, and scalability assumptions in superconducting processors.
detailed_summary: The work studies superconducting transmon arrays with interactions mediated by the full capacitance network rather than a truncated nearest-neighbor effective model. As capacitive connectivity increases, out-of-time-ordered correlators saturate much faster, indicating accelerated operator scrambling and a strong dynamical departure from local-coupling expectations. Spectrally, the system moves away from Poisson statistics toward level repulsion, but remains between Poisson and GOE limits, consistent with partial ergodicity rather than full chaos. The parameter regime is presented as compatible with current experimental transmon hardware.
main_result: Network-mediated capacitive couplings are shown to produce rapid OTOC saturation and intermediate level statistics signaling partial ergodicity in realistic transmon circuits.
application_angle: For quantum processor design, the result implies that nonlocal circuit parasitics can materially affect information spreading, crosstalk, and performance as systems scale.

title: Block Permutation Routing on Ramanujan Hypergraphs for Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computing
abstract: We analyze permutation routing of rigid blocks representing surface code patches of $d_C^2$ atoms on a reconfigurable lattice with hypergraph transformations. For a hypergraph $H$, code distance $d_C$, $s=d_C^2$, number of blocks $N_L$, and guard distance $g$, we show the block routing number $\mathrm{rt}_B(H, s, g) = Θ(d_C \log N_L)$. A spectral analysis of the quotient graph $Q(G_{\mathrm{cl}}(H), B)$ (blocks as supervertices) shows that the spectral ratio $β_Q < 1$ is preserved in the high-connectivity regime. Negative association of block permutations and congestion bounds are used for random intermediate configurations. Serialization establishes that each quotient routing phase requires $O(d_C)$ physical sub-steps due to the block footprint width. A lower bound $\mathrm{rt}_B = Ω(d_C \log N_L)$ follows from combining the spectral lower bound on quotient phases with the traversal cost per phase. We include error model analysis grounded in recent experimental results, syndrome extraction protocols (stop-and-correct, rolling active fault-tolerant (AFT) measurement, and adaptive deformation), and integration with lattice surgery compilation via the Litinski protocol. Composition with the correlated-decoding scheme reduces syndrome-extraction overhead from $O(d_C)$ to $O(1)$ per correction window, leaving routing as the leading-order contributor to the integrated $O(d_C \log N_L)$ depth. Spectral inheritance is organized in a hierarchy: exact (Haemers interlacing on equitable partitions), perturbative (Weyl bounds for near-equitable partitions, a practically relevant case for surface-code patches), and universal (higher-order Cheeger). Methods extend directly to QCCD trapped-ion architectures under the same regime condition, with junction crossings replacing AOD transports as the elementary single-hop translation.
one_liner: Rigid surface-code patches on Ramanujan-like hypergraphs can be routed in optimal Θ(d_C log N_L) depth.
why_it_matters: It gives a concrete architectural routing bound for large fault-tolerant machines where moving code patches, not decoding, can dominate execution depth.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes permutation routing of rigid blocks representing surface-code patches of size d_C^2 on reconfigurable lattices modeled by hypergraph transformations. Using spectral properties of the quotient graph, congestion bounds, and serialization arguments, it proves an asymptotically tight routing number Θ(d_C log N_L), with each quotient-routing phase costing O(d_C) physical substeps because of the block footprint. The authors also integrate syndrome-extraction and decoding considerations, arguing that correlated decoding can compress correction overhead to O(1) per window, leaving routing as the leading-order cost. The framework is extended to QCCD trapped-ion architectures by replacing AOD transport with junction crossings.
main_result: The main result is an asymptotically optimal block-routing depth of Θ(d_C log N_L) for moving fault-tolerant code patches on high-connectivity reconfigurable lattices.
application_angle: This directly informs architecture and compiler co-design for large-scale fault-tolerant quantum computers where patch motion and lattice surgery scheduling set throughput.

title: Planar chiral nanoantenna for excitation-chirality-controlled hot spot modulation and emitter-coupled circularly polarized emission
abstract: A planar chiral plasmonic nanoantenna exhibiting an excitation-chirality-dependent hot spot in a nanogap is numerically investigated. Additionally, the underlying design principles are examined, providing a broadly applicable framework for engineering chiral nanoantennas through controlled geometrical or modal asymmetry. The hot spot can be turned on and off by changing the handedness of the exciting circularly polarized light (CPL). This effect stems from the rationally designed interference of plasmonic modes excited by the linearly polarized orthogonal components of CPL. The hot spot exhibits maximal near-field dissymmetry factor (about -2) at a wavelength of 842 nm. The intensity at the hot spot can also be continuously modulated by varying the excitation ellipticity and handedness, approaching a modulation depth of 100%. These attributes enable chirality- and ellipticity-dependent switching and dynamic modulation of the plasmonic near field. Moreover, placing a quantum emitter in the gap generates almost perfectly circularly polarized emission, offering a simple yet effective avenue to realize nanoscale circularly polarized single-photon sources.
one_liner: A planar chiral nanoantenna toggles a nanogap hot spot with light handedness and yields near-perfect circularly polarized emitter output.
why_it_matters: It combines chirality-selective near-field control with an on-chip path toward circularly polarized nanoscale single-photon emission.
detailed_summary: The study numerically designs a planar chiral plasmonic nanoantenna whose nanogap hot spot depends strongly on the handedness and ellipticity of circularly polarized excitation. The effect arises from engineered interference between plasmonic modes driven by the orthogonal linear components of the incident field, producing a near-field dissymmetry factor around -2 at 842 nm and modulation depth approaching 100%. By placing a quantum emitter in the gap, the structure produces almost perfectly circularly polarized emission. The paper also extracts general design rules based on geometrical and modal asymmetry for broader chiral nanoantenna engineering.
main_result: The antenna enables handedness-controlled on/off and continuous modulation of a nanogap hot spot, while an embedded emitter radiates nearly perfectly circularly polarized light.
application_angle: This is relevant for chiral sensing, polarization-programmable nanophotonics, and compact circularly polarized single-photon sources for quantum optics.

title: Photonic-crystal microresonator-based LiDAR engine
abstract: Self-injection-locked (SIL) narrow-linewidth lasers based on high-Q microresonators are promising sources for frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) LiDAR, but the SIL mechanism as well as its key characteristics such as the frequency sweep range and the noise performance are often determined by uncontrolled backscattering in the resonator. Here, we investigate a tunable SIL laser based on a corrugated photonic-crystal (PhC) microresonator in which the feedback strength is set by design. Numerical and experimental results show that stronger SIL feedback expands the sweep range accessible through resonator modulation while also impacting the phase-noise and linewidth during sweeping, revealing a trade-off between frequency tunability and noise performance. Using CMOS-compatible microheater tuning (sub-1 V driving voltage), we demonstrate linearized up- and down-chirps with 224 THz/s over approximately 3 GHz and, in a proof-of-concept ranging experiment, measure a 10 m fiber length with a standard deviation below 3 mm. These results establish PhC microresonators with engineered SIL feedback as robust, compact, CMOS-compatible LiDAR engines.
one_liner: A photonic-crystal microresonator with engineered self-injection locking acts as a compact FMCW LiDAR engine.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates a CMOS-compatible route to low-voltage, narrow-linewidth chirped lasers with millimeter-level ranging precision.
detailed_summary: The authors study a self-injection-locked laser using a corrugated photonic-crystal microresonator whose feedback strength is designed rather than left to uncontrolled backscattering. They show experimentally and numerically that stronger feedback increases the modulation-accessible sweep range but changes linewidth and phase-noise performance, establishing a tunability-noise tradeoff. With CMOS-compatible microheater tuning below 1 V, they generate linearized up- and down-chirps at 224 THz/s over about 3 GHz. In a ranging test, the system measures a 10 m fiber length with under 3 mm standard deviation.
main_result: The device demonstrates low-voltage linear FMCW chirps and proof-of-concept ranging with sub-3 mm precision using engineered self-injection locking.
application_angle: It is a strong candidate for compact integrated LiDAR and precision ranging modules where size, CMOS compatibility, and controllable noise matter.

title: Dimeric Perylene-Bisimide Organic Molecules: Fractional-Time Control of Quantum Resources
abstract: In this work, we explore the dynamics of quantum correlations, namely coherence, entanglement, and nonlocality associated with a Bell state, in a dimeric arrangement of organic PBI molecules, mediated by dipole-dipole interactions, under time-fractional dynamics. Within the framework of the time-fractional Schrödinger equation (TFSE) with Caputo fractional derivatives, we explore system dynamics for different values of the fractional order $τ$, transition energies, interaction strength, and purity $p$. We employ the relative entropy of coherence, logarithmic entanglement entropy and concurrence, and CHSH inequality to estimate system dynamics associated with coherence, entanglement, and nonlocality, respectively. These findings highlight the role of the fractional order $τ$ in system dynamics, including memory effects and relaxation, and thereby bring together ideas from fractional calculus and quantum information theory perspectives and discuss methodologies to control and utilize these molecular quantum correlations.
one_liner: Fractional-time dynamics in PBI molecular dimers provide a tunable handle over coherence, entanglement, and Bell nonlocality.
why_it_matters: It points to molecular quantum platforms where memory-like fractional dynamics could be used to shape useful quantum correlations.
detailed_summary: The paper models a dipole-dipole-coupled dimer of perylene-bisimide molecules with a time-fractional Schrödinger equation using Caputo derivatives. By sweeping the fractional order τ along with transition energies, interaction strength, and state purity, it tracks relative-entropy coherence, logarithmic entanglement entropy, concurrence, and CHSH nonlocality. The analysis shows that τ strongly influences relaxation, memory effects, and the persistence of quantum correlations. This frames fractional dynamics as a control parameter for molecular-scale quantum information behavior.
main_result: The main result is that the fractional-order parameter τ can systematically modulate coherence, entanglement, and Bell nonlocality in a coupled organic molecular dimer.
application_angle: If realized experimentally, this could support engineered molecular quantum photonic elements where environmental memory is exploited rather than merely suppressed.

title: Plasma effects on lifetimes and screening of Rydberg excitons
abstract: We simulate the effects of a neutral electron--hole plasma on Rydberg excitons in cuprous oxide (Cu$_2$O), focusing on the validity of Debye screening and the role of plasma-induced thermalization. Unlike atomic Rydberg states, excitons in Cu$_2$O consist of quasiparticles with comparable effective masses whose orbital frequencies can exceed the plasma frequency, invalidating the assumption of a stationary screened charge. Using two complementary approaches, a classical orbit model and a harmonic-oscillator representation evolved via the truncated Wigner approximation, we study exciton lifetimes and interaction screening under realistic plasma conditions. We find numerically that plasma-induced scattering induces finite exciton lifetimes with specific scaling relations with plasma density, principal quantum number $n$ and temperature, possibly providing an explanation for experimentally observed deviations from the $n^3$ scaling at high principal quantum numbers. By explicitly computing time-averaged electric fields, we show that Debye screening overestimates the screening of the exciton's internal field, especially for high angular momentum states. Furthermore, we demonstrate that exciton-exciton interactions are not Debye screened at separations comparable to the Debye length for Rydberg excitons that are well resolvable in absorption measurements.
one_liner: Simulations show neutral plasmas shorten Cu2O Rydberg-exciton lifetimes while Debye theory overstates internal-field screening.
why_it_matters: It refines how solid-state Rydberg excitons behave in realistic plasmas, directly affecting device design and interpretation of high-n exciton experiments.
detailed_summary: The paper models Rydberg excitons in cuprous oxide interacting with a neutral electron-hole plasma using both a classical orbit picture and a truncated-Wigner harmonic-oscillator approach. It finds plasma-induced scattering gives finite lifetimes with explicit scaling versus plasma density, principal quantum number, and temperature, offering an explanation for deviations from the expected n^3 lifetime scaling at large n. By computing time-averaged internal fields, the authors show static Debye screening substantially overestimates screening, especially for high-angular-momentum states. They further argue exciton-exciton interactions remain largely unscreened at separations near the Debye length when the excitons are spectroscopically resolvable.
platform_or_system: Cu2O Rydberg excitons in electron-hole plasma
main_result: The main result is that plasma scattering, not simple static Debye screening, controls high-n Rydberg-exciton lifetimes and interaction screening under realistic conditions.
application_angle: This improves predictive models for excitonic quantum platforms and optoelectronic devices that rely on long-lived, strongly interacting Rydberg excitons.

title: Release-free electro-optomechanical crystal modulator
abstract: Electro-optic modulation is central to classical optical communications and emerging quantum technologies. High-confinement optomechanical crystal modulators enable microwave-optical transduction through strong optomechanical interactions and offer a promising interface between superconducting qubits and optical fibers. However, their performance is limited by thermal noise from optical absorption. Release-free optomechanical crystals provide improved thermal anchoring but have not yet been integrated into a microwave-optical transducer. Here, we demonstrate a release-free electro-optomechanical transducer combining strong optomechanical interactions in silicon with the efficient piezoelectricity of lithium niobate via micro-transfer printing. We observe electro- and optomechanical coupling rates compatible with quantum-level operation when co-integrated with a superconducting microwave circuit. This advance moves release-free electro-optomechanical devices toward practical microwave-optical interfaces.
one_liner: A release-free silicon-lithium-niobate electro-optomechanical crystal reaches coupling rates compatible with quantum microwave-optical transduction.
why_it_matters: Better thermal anchoring tackles a key noise bottleneck in microwave-to-optical interfaces needed to connect superconducting qubits to fiber networks.
detailed_summary: The work demonstrates a release-free electro-optomechanical crystal that combines strong silicon optomechanics with lithium-niobate piezoelectric actuation through micro-transfer printing. Avoiding suspended release improves thermal anchoring and directly targets excess thermal noise from optical absorption, a major limitation in conventional devices. The authors measure both electro- and optomechanical coupling rates at levels consistent with quantum-regime operation and discuss co-integration with a superconducting microwave circuit. The result is a more practical device architecture for coherent microwave-optical conversion.
main_result: The device achieves electro- and optomechanical coupling strengths compatible with quantum-level microwave-to-optical transduction while preserving improved thermal management.
application_angle: It is a concrete hardware step toward linking cryogenic superconducting quantum processors with low-loss optical fiber communication channels.

title: Engineering Quantum Many-Body Scars through Lattice Geometry
abstract: Quantum many-body scars enable persistent non-ergodic dynamics in otherwise thermalizing systems, yet their stabilization typically relies on fine-tuned initial states or engineered Hamiltonian perturbations. Here we show that lattice geometry alone can serve as a powerful and experimentally accessible control knob for inducing and enhancing scarring. By transforming a one-dimensional chain into a quasi-one-dimensional triangle-decorated lattice, we find that the fully polarized state -- normally thermalizing in the PXP model -- exhibits pronounced fidelity revivals, slow entanglement growth, and strong overlap with a tower of weakly entangled eigenstates. We trace this behavior to a geometry-induced restructuring of the constrained Hilbert space, whereby the adjacency graph decomposes into hypercube subgraphs that enforce coherent population transfer and stabilize an emergent approximate $\mathrm{su}(2)$ algebra. We propose a direct implementation in programmable arrays of tweezer-trapped Rydberg atoms, where the triangle-decorated geometry can be realized using spatial light modulators and the resulting scarring dynamics probed via time-resolved measurements of excitation density. Our results establish lattice connectivity as a design principle for engineering non-ergodic dynamics in constrained quantum systems.
one_liner: Triangle-decorated Rydberg lattices induce strong many-body scarring from geometry alone, with fidelity revivals and suppressed entanglement growth.
why_it_matters: It turns lattice connectivity into an experimentally accessible control knob for stabilizing nonthermal dynamics in programmable neutral-atom hardware.
detailed_summary: Starting from the PXP model, the authors show that replacing a 1D chain with a quasi-1D triangle-decorated lattice makes the fully polarized state exhibit pronounced revivals and slow entanglement growth. They attribute this to a geometry-driven restructuring of the constrained Hilbert space, where adjacency graphs decompose into hypercube-like subgraphs supporting coherent transfer and an emergent approximate su(2) algebra. The mechanism does not rely on Hamiltonian fine-tuning or specially prepared initial perturbations beyond the geometric design. An implementation path is proposed using tweezer-trapped Rydberg atoms with spatial light modulators and time-resolved excitation measurements.
main_result: The main result is that lattice geometry alone can generate and enhance quantum many-body scarring, producing robust revivals from an otherwise thermalizing initial state.
application_angle: This offers a design route for long-lived coherent dynamics and state retention in neutral-atom quantum simulators and potentially in analog quantum processors.

title: Systematic construction of quantum many-body scars in frustrated Rydberg arrays
abstract: Quantum many-body scars in Rydberg atom arrays have thus far only been observed on bipartite lattices, leaving open the question of whether and how they survive frustration, and what the appropriate initial states are that lead to nonthermal dynamics. We introduce a graph-theoretic framework to find suitable candidates for scarring on arbitrary lattices. Our framework predicts two distinct mechanisms: type-I scars generalize the bipartite case by using locally entangled states to overcome mild frustration, while type-II scars exploit strong frustration to pin part of the lattice, leaving the remainder to oscillate freely. We numerically demonstrate both mechanisms and uncover an exponential family of scarred trajectories on the hexagonal lattice that can encode information protected from thermalization. Our results establish scarring as a generic feature of Rydberg systems beyond one dimension and provide an experimentally accessible route to systematically probing non-thermal dynamics in quantum simulators.
one_liner: A graph-theoretic framework identifies two scar mechanisms in frustrated Rydberg lattices, including an exponential set of protected trajectories.
why_it_matters: It expands scar engineering beyond bipartite arrays and suggests experimentally useful nonthermal subspaces for storing information in neutral-atom systems.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a general graph-theoretic construction for finding scarred dynamics in arbitrarily connected Rydberg lattices, including frustrated geometries. It identifies type-I scars, which use locally entangled states to overcome mild frustration, and type-II scars, where strong frustration pins part of the lattice while the rest oscillates. Numerical studies confirm both mechanisms and reveal an exponential family of scarred trajectories on the hexagonal lattice. These trajectories form nonthermal dynamical sectors that can encode information resistant to thermalization.
main_result: The main result is a systematic recipe for constructing scars in frustrated Rydberg lattices, with explicit demonstrations of stable nonthermal trajectories on experimentally relevant geometries.
application_angle: The framework gives neutral-atom experiments a practical way to engineer long-lived dynamical subspaces for robust analog simulation and transient information protection.

title: Two Layers, No Swaps: Biplanar SPOQC Architecture Improves Runtime of Fermi-Hubbard Simulation
abstract: We estimate the cost of simulating the two-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model on a biplanar spin-optical quantum computing (SPOQC) architecture. Qubits are encoded in the honeycomb Floquet code, and we use a circuit-level noise model with explicit timings for each native physical operation. We benchmark lattice surgery and magic state preparation within each plane, and transversal CNOT gates between corresponding logical qubits across planes. We compile a plaquette-based Trotterization of the time evolution operator, mapping the two spin sectors of the Fermi-Hubbard model onto two physical planes. This architectural co-design eliminates fermionic swap operations and reduces the depth of each Trotter step to $4t_{\mathrm{synth}} + 90$ logical timesteps, where $t_\mathrm{synth}$ is the logical timestep cost of arbitrary-angle rotations, compared to $6t_\mathrm{synth} + 354$ in prior single-plane compilations. All error sources - algorithmic (Trotter), logical noise, magic state infidelity, and rotation synthesis - are treated jointly within a single 1% diamond norm budget. For an $L\times L$ lattice with hopping amplitude $t$ and on-site interaction strength $U$, setting $L=8$ and $U/t=8$, we estimate a total runtime of approximately $2$ hours using $1.35\times 10^6$ physical qubits. We find that fallback-based rotation synthesis methods become a scalability bottleneck: the probability that all $L^2$ parallel rotations succeed on the first attempt vanishes exponentially with system size, causing the failure branch to dominate the expected runtime already at moderate $L$.
one_liner: A biplanar spin-optical architecture cuts Fermi-Hubbard simulation depth by removing fermionic swaps between spin sectors.
why_it_matters: It shows how architecture-aware compilation can materially reduce runtime and expose the true scaling bottlenecks for fault-tolerant quantum simulation.
detailed_summary: This work estimates the end-to-end cost of simulating the 2D Fermi-Hubbard model on a biplanar spin-optical quantum computing architecture with qubits encoded in the honeycomb Floquet code. By mapping the two spin sectors onto separate planes and using inter-plane transversal CNOTs, the compilation eliminates fermionic swap overhead and reduces each Trotter step from 6t_synth + 354 to 4t_synth + 90 logical timesteps. The analysis jointly budgets Trotter, logical, magic-state, and rotation-synthesis errors within a 1% diamond-norm target. For an 8x8 lattice at U/t=8, the paper estimates about 2 hours runtime using 1.35 million physical qubits, while highlighting fallback-based rotation synthesis as the dominant scalability bottleneck.
platform_or_system: biplanar SPOQC with honeycomb Floquet code
main_result: The main result is a substantially lower-depth fault-tolerant compilation for Fermi-Hubbard simulation enabled by a two-plane architecture that removes fermionic swap operations.
application_angle: It provides concrete resource estimates and architectural guidance for building hardware and compilers aimed at useful fault-tolerant materials simulation.

title: Squeezed Vibrational States in Superfluid Helium
abstract: Ultrafast birefringence oscillations observed in superfluid helium provide evidence for anisotropic quantum squeezing of quasiparticle pairs. The measured response is a superposition of contributions from all vibrational modes, with dominant contributions from rotons, maxons, and Pitaevskii's plateau. The nonzero initial phase follows naturally from multimode interference.
one_liner: Ultrafast birefringence in superfluid helium indicates multimode squeezed vibrational quasiparticle states with anisotropic quantum correlations.
why_it_matters: It points to superfluid helium as a platform for observing and controlling nonclassical collective excitations relevant to quantum metrology and quantum fluids.
detailed_summary: The authors interpret ultrafast birefringence oscillations in superfluid helium as evidence for anisotropic squeezing of vibrational quasiparticle pairs. The measured signal is modeled as a coherent superposition of many modes, with rotons, maxons, and the Pitaevskii plateau contributing most strongly. A nonzero initial phase emerges naturally from multimode interference rather than requiring an ad hoc phase offset. The result ties time-resolved optical probing directly to nonclassical collective-state dynamics in a quantum fluid.
main_result: The main result is experimental evidence consistent with multimode squeezed vibrational states in superfluid helium inferred from ultrafast birefringence dynamics.
application_angle: Optical access to squeezed collective modes could support ultrasensitive probing of quantum fluids and new bosonic platforms for quantum-enhanced sensing.

title: BARFI-Q: Quantum-Enhanced Block Attention Residual Fusion Framework for Multivariate Time-Series Forecasting in Atom Interferometry
abstract: Atom interferometry generates heterogeneous multivariate temporal streams governed by phase evolution, fringe dynamics, control variables, and auxiliary sensing measurements. Accurate forecasting of these signals is important for predictive monitoring, phase correction, and intelligent quantum sensing, but it requires effective modeling of long-range temporal dependencies and interactions among multiple sensing sources. This paper proposes BARFI-Q, a Quantum-Enhanced Block Attention Residual Fusion framework for multivariate time-series forecasting in atom interferometry. BARFI-Q integrates patch-based embedding, dual-branch temporal modeling, hierarchical fusion, adaptive block-attention residual aggregation, and a quantum feature-mapping module. Unlike conventional Transformer-based forecasting models with fixed additive residual paths, BARFI-Q adaptively reuses cross-depth information and enhances the fused latent representation through quantum feature mapping. To respect phase periodicity, the forecasting target is represented in circular space using sine and cosine components. Experiments show that BARFI-Q consistently outperforms strong baseline models across repeated runs and different historical window sizes. Fusion ablation results further confirm the benefit of jointly modeling channel-wise and spatial feature interactions. These results indicate that multiscale temporal learning, hierarchical fusion, adaptive residual routing, and quantum-enhanced latent transformation provide an effective framework for atom-interferometric time-series forecasting.
one_liner: BARFI-Q uses quantum-enhanced latent mapping and adaptive residual attention to improve atom-interferometer time-series forecasting.
why_it_matters: Better forecasting of interferometer signals could enable predictive stabilization, phase correction, and smarter operation of precision quantum sensors.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces BARFI-Q, a multivariate forecasting architecture tailored to atom-interferometric data streams containing phase, fringe, control, and auxiliary sensing channels. Its pipeline combines patch embeddings, dual-branch temporal modeling, hierarchical fusion of channel-wise and spatial interactions, adaptive block-attention residual routing, and a quantum feature-mapping stage. To handle phase periodicity, targets are represented in circular coordinates via sine and cosine components. Across repeated runs and varying history lengths, the model outperforms strong baselines and ablations show that joint multiscale fusion materially improves prediction quality.
main_result: BARFI-Q consistently beats baseline multivariate forecasters on atom-interferometry signal prediction across multiple window sizes and repeated trials.
application_angle: The method is directly relevant to predictive monitoring and real-time correction pipelines for deployed atom-interferometric sensing systems.

title: Non-Markovian delay-assisted sensing with waveguide-coupled quantum emitters
abstract: We show that in a minimal setup of two waveguide-coupled quantum emitters, separated by long distances and subject to an external field, time-delayed feedback can be a resource for sensing field gradients. While the field gradient induces a detuning between the emitters; the large interatomic separations render the system dynamics non-Markovian. We show that the quantum Fisher information (QFI) for estimating the detuning parameter, and thereby the field gradient, is enhanced in the presence of non-Markovian delay. Such an enhancement can be attributed to the formation of atom-photon quasi-bound states that enable the field to interact with the emitters for longer times, thereby gaining more information about their relative detunings. Additionally, in the presence of delay, the interaction between the emitters is mediated via multiple spectral modes of the field, further enhancing the sensing capabilities of the system. Our results establish non-Markovian time-delayed feedback and multimode reservoirs as a resource for distributed quantum sensing with waveguide-coupled quantum emitters.
one_liner: Time-delayed feedback between distant waveguide-coupled emitters boosts gradient sensing via non-Markovian dynamics and quasi-bound states.
why_it_matters: It turns propagation delay and multimode reservoir effects from a nuisance into a metrological resource for distributed quantum sensing.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze two quantum emitters coupled through a waveguide and subjected to an external field gradient that induces relative detuning. At large separations, retardation makes the dynamics non-Markovian, and the resulting delay-assisted feedback increases the quantum Fisher information for estimating the detuning. The enhancement is linked to atom-photon quasi-bound states that prolong interaction time and to multimode field-mediated coupling between emitters. The work establishes delayed waveguide dynamics as a concrete resource for sensing spatial field gradients.
main_result: Non-Markovian propagation delay enhances detuning-estimation QFI in a two-emitter waveguide system, improving field-gradient sensing performance.
application_angle: This points to photonic distributed sensors that deliberately exploit long-distance delay lines and reservoir engineering for higher sensitivity.

title: Resolving magnetic-sublevel structure in Rydberg Autler-Townes spectra with arbitrary RF polarization
abstract: We investigate the role of magnetic sublevels in Autler-Townes spectra of Rydberg atoms driven by radio-frequency (RF) fields with arbitrary polarization. While conventional treatments predict two symmetric sidebands from independent mJ transitions, experiments have reported additional unexplained spectral features. We show that these arise from elliptical RF polarization, which coherently couples multiple magnetic sublevels and requires a full multi-level treatment. We develop and diagonalize a Hamiltonian including all coupled mJ sublevels, predicting polarization-dependent degeneracies that produce two, three, or four resolved peaks. Using long-wavelength transitions and an anechoic environment we realize homogeneous RF fields that for the first time enable complete resolution of the mJ-dependent dressed states. We observe excellent agreement with theory as the RF ellipticity is varied. These results demonstrate that RF polarization fundamentally modifies Autler-Townes spectra and provide a consistent framework for interpreting magnetic-sublevel structure, with implications for Rydberg-based RF electrometry and polarimetry.
one_liner: A full multi-sublevel model explains and resolves polarization-dependent Rydberg Autler-Townes peak structure under arbitrary RF polarization.
why_it_matters: It sharpens the interpretation of Rydberg RF spectra needed for accurate electrometry and polarimetry hardware.
detailed_summary: This work shows that elliptical RF polarization coherently mixes multiple magnetic sublevels in Rydberg Autler-Townes spectroscopy, invalidating simpler pictures based on independent mJ transitions. By constructing and diagonalizing the full coupled-sublevel Hamiltonian, the authors predict polarization-dependent degeneracies that yield two, three, or four resolvable dressed-state peaks. Using long-wavelength transitions in an anechoic environment, they realize sufficiently homogeneous RF fields to fully resolve the mJ-dependent structure. The measured spectra track the theoretical predictions as RF ellipticity is varied.
main_result: The study experimentally resolves magnetic-sublevel dressed states and quantitatively matches a full multi-level theory across arbitrary RF polarization states.
application_angle: The result improves calibration and interpretation for Rydberg-based RF sensors used in field-strength and polarization measurements.

title: Operationally Admissible Post-Quantum Correlations from a Standard Quantum Walk
abstract: It is shown that a standard one-dimensional coined discrete-time quantum walk can generate operationally admissible post-quantum correlations in a coin-position Bell scenario, without any modification of its unitary nearest-neighbor dynamics. Post-quantum features enter exclusively through an extended operational preparation of the coin, described by a complementarity-violating Hermitian trace-one operator, while physical consistency is enforced solely at the level of observable statistics via admissibility and no-signaling. The extended preparation admits an experimental emulation through a two-component quasiprobability reconstruction over physical coin states, at the price of an increased sampling overhead. The walk-generated coin-position entanglement can support CHSH values exceeding Tsirelson's bound, even though the walk dynamics remains fully standard. We also show that physically natural coarse-grained position measurements can render such post-quantum correlations operationally inaccessible, strongly suppressing observable Bell violations. The purpose here is to contrast the separation between the existence of post-quantum behavior and its accessibility under realistic measurement constraints.
one_liner: A standard coined quantum walk can emulate operationally admissible post-quantum Bell correlations through extended coin-state preparation.
why_it_matters: It separates exotic correlation generation from measurement accessibility, clarifying what realistic photonic-style measurements can actually reveal.
detailed_summary: The paper considers a one-dimensional coined discrete-time quantum walk in a coin-position Bell scenario and shows that post-quantum correlations can arise without modifying the walk's unitary nearest-neighbor dynamics. The nonstandard ingredient is an extended coin preparation described by a complementarity-violating Hermitian trace-one operator, which can be experimentally emulated through a quasiprobability decomposition over physical coin states. With this preparation, the walk-generated entanglement can produce CHSH violations above Tsirelson's bound. However, physically natural coarse-grained position measurements strongly suppress the observable violation, making the post-quantum behavior operationally inaccessible in realistic settings.
main_result: Standard quantum-walk dynamics combined with extended coin preparation can generate admissible post-quantum Bell statistics, though coarse-grained measurements largely hide them.
application_angle: The work is relevant to photonic quantum-walk platforms as a guide to which nonclassical effects survive realistic detection constraints.

title: Quantum Simulation of the Real-time Dynamics in the multi-flavor Gross-Neveu Model at the utility scale using Superconducting Quantum Computers
abstract: We present a scalable quantum simulation framework for real-time dynamics of the multi-flavor Gross-Neveu model in 1+1 dimensions. Using superconducting quantum processors at utility scale, we develop a hardware-efficient Trotterization whose per-step circuit depth scales with fermion flavor number rather than total system size, enabling simulations beyond 100 qubits. A central contribution of this work is the Localized Diagonal Operator Approximation (LDOA), which systematically reduces the overhead associated with quartic interactions. We formulate diagonal unitary synthesis as a structured least-squares problem in phase space and obtain analytic solutions via the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse. This formulation provides a principled and quantitatively controlled approximation: in the small Trotter-step regime, the unitary error is directly linked to the phase reconstruction error and vanishes asymptotically as the Trotter step size decreases. This establishes a clear mathematical foundation for the LDOA while significantly reducing two-qubit gate counts and circuit depth, and is broadly applicable to diagonal quantum operators with long-range structure, making it particularly well suited for quantum hardware with limited qubit connectivity. Using these techniques, we run large-scale simulations on IBM superconducting processors and study real-time observables, including density-density correlators. We benchmark against exact diagonalization and tensor network-based methods, finding strong agreement across system sizes. These results show that combining hardware-aware circuit design with rigorous approximations enables practical near-term simulation of interacting fermionic field theories and provides a scalable pathway toward more complex quantum field theory simulations.
one_liner: Utility-scale superconducting processors simulate real-time Gross-Neveu dynamics using hardware-efficient Trotterization and a new diagonal-operator approximation.
why_it_matters: It pushes interacting fermionic field-theory simulation toward practically useful scales on existing superconducting hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors develop a scalable framework for simulating the multi-flavor Gross-Neveu model in 1+1D on superconducting quantum computers, with per-step circuit depth scaling with flavor count rather than total lattice size. Their key technical contribution, the Localized Diagonal Operator Approximation, treats quartic interactions through a structured least-squares phase-space formulation with analytic pseudoinverse solutions. In the small-step regime, the unitary approximation error is directly tied to phase reconstruction error, giving a controlled accuracy story while reducing two-qubit cost. Large-scale runs on IBM hardware reproduce real-time observables such as density-density correlators in strong agreement with exact diagonalization and tensor-network benchmarks.
main_result: The paper demonstrates accurate utility-scale real-time simulation of an interacting fermionic field theory on superconducting hardware with substantially reduced circuit depth.
application_angle: This is a concrete hardware-oriented path toward larger quantum simulations of many-body and high-energy models on near-term superconducting platforms.

title: Eigenstate-Selective Entangled Two-Photon Absorption in Monolayer WSe$_2$
abstract: We show that the Bell-state phase of a polarization-entangled photon pair controls the biexciton eigenstate distribution produced by entangled two-photon absorption (ETPA) in monolayer WSe$_2$. In a frequency-nondegenerate ladder scheme, two independent valley pathways ($K$ and $K'$) share no intermediate state, so the biphoton phase sets the relative amplitude between them. Within the valley-symmetric limit this phase factorizes from the material response, and the resulting selection rule partitions the excitation among biexciton eigenstates according to the Bell-state phase $\varphi$. The symmetric Bell state ($\varphi = 0$) selectively drives bright eigenstates, while the antisymmetric state ($\varphi = π$) drives the exchange-dark eigenstate. No classical polarization source reproduces this $\varphi$-dependent eigenstate distribution. Including valley dephasing and intervalley scattering at 4~K, the phase-scan visibility exceeds $0.97$ for broadband SPDC ($T_e \sim 100$~fs) with high source purity.
one_liner: Entangled-photon Bell-state phase selectively addresses bright versus dark biexciton eigenstates in monolayer WSe2.
why_it_matters: It shows a genuinely nonclassical optical control knob for excitonic state preparation in a solid-state quantum material.
detailed_summary: The work predicts that the phase of a polarization-entangled photon pair controls biexciton eigenstate populations created by entangled two-photon absorption in monolayer WSe2. In a frequency-nondegenerate ladder scheme, the K and K' valley pathways lack a shared intermediate state, so the biphoton Bell-state phase directly sets their relative excitation amplitude. In the valley-symmetric limit, this yields a selection rule where the symmetric Bell state excites bright biexcitons while the antisymmetric Bell state drives the exchange-dark eigenstate. Including dephasing and intervalley scattering at 4 K, the predicted phase-scan visibility remains above 0.97 for broadband SPDC with high source purity.
main_result: The Bell-state phase of an entangled photon pair selectively populates distinct biexciton eigenstates, including bright and exchange-dark states, in monolayer WSe2.
application_angle: This offers an application route for entanglement-enabled spectroscopy and quantum-state-selective control in 2D excitonic and valleytronic devices.

title: Electronic and Photonic Integration of Single Quantum Emitters in 2D Materials
abstract: Single-photon sources that are bright, pure, and interference-ready are essential for quantum communication and photonic quantum information processing, but many solid-state platforms still rely on bulky optical excitation, careful alignment, and post-selection to achieve useful linewidth, stability, and brightness. Scalable quantum photonics instead requires turnkey quantum-light engines that can be triggered on demand, stabilized against environmental noise, and efficiently interfaced with fibers or photonic circuits. This review surveys recent progress in electronic and photonic integration of single quantum emitters in two-dimensional materials, focusing on localized excitonic emitters in transition metal dichalcogenides and defect-based color centers in hexagonal boron nitride. On the electronic side, we discuss electrical injection, fast modulation, electrostatic stabilization, and Stark tunability as routes to suppress blinking, spectral wandering, and charge-noise-induced broadening. On the photonic side, we review waveguide and resonator platforms that funnel emission into well-defined optical modes and, in some cases, enhance radiative rates through the Purcell effect. We connect these integration strategies to key source metrics, including single-photon purity, brightness, spectral stability, and photon indistinguishability. We conclude that the next stage of progress will depend on co-designed electronic and photonic architectures that jointly optimize on-demand operation, stabilization, tunability, and packaging-compatible optical interfacing.
one_liner: Review of how 2D-material single emitters are being electrically driven, stabilized, and photonicly integrated for scalable quantum-light sources.
why_it_matters: It lays out the engineering path to compact, fiber- and chip-compatible single-photon sources with better purity, stability, and on-demand operation.
detailed_summary: This review covers localized excitonic emitters in transition metal dichalcogenides and defect emitters in hexagonal boron nitride, emphasizing co-integration with electronics and photonics. It surveys electrical injection, fast modulation, electrostatic stabilization, and Stark tuning for suppressing blinking, charge noise, and spectral wandering. On the photonics side, it discusses waveguides and resonators that improve collection efficiency and can enhance radiative rates via the Purcell effect. The paper frames future progress around co-designed architectures that jointly optimize brightness, purity, indistinguishability, tunability, and packaging-ready optical interfacing.
main_result: The paper synthesizes recent progress showing that combined electronic and photonic integration can directly improve key single-photon-source metrics in 2D emitters.
application_angle: It is directly relevant to deployable quantum-network light sources and photonic quantum chips that avoid bulky free-space optics.

title: Hybrid integrated narrow linewidth semiconductor laser based on the distributed feedback from an external deformed microcavity
abstract: Optical microcavities with rotational symmetry have been widely used for narrowing linewidth and reducing frequency noise, however, the narrow but wavelength dependent optical feedback restricts the narrow linewidth laser works only at some discrete wavelength matching the resonance of the microcavity. Here, we demonstrate a narrow linewidth semiconductor laser with continuous wavelength tunability by hybrid integrating a DFB laser chip with a deformed microcavity fabricated on a 220 nm SOI wafer. The deformed microcavity with vortex radius demonstrates the unique characteristics of unidirectional energy storage, wavelength self-adaptivity, and self-focusing of the Rayleigh scattering based distributed feedback. In addition, the strength of Rayleigh scattering is also significantly enhanced by the high numerical aperture silicon waveguide. The optical feedback signal measured by the optical frequency domain reflectometry (OFDR) shows that the deformed microcavity can effectively lengthen the equivalent propagation distance without wavelength dependence. With the wavelength self-adaptive optical feedback from the deformed microcavity, the intrinsic linewidth of a DFB laser diode is narrowed to 525 Hz and the side mode suppression ratio (SMSR) is improved to 76 dB in a maximum allowable continuous wavelength tuning range of 2.25 nm. The frequency noise and relative intensity noise (RIN) are reduced to 2.98 Hz2 /Hz and -148.74 dB/Hz at the offset frequency of 1 MHz, respectively. The work demonstrated here paves a new way for integrated tunable narrow linewidth lasers, which are of crucial importance in high-speed communication and high-precision spectroscopy
one_liner: A hybrid SOI-integrated DFB laser uses a deformed microcavity for self-adaptive feedback, reaching 525 Hz linewidth with continuous tuning.
why_it_matters: Integrated narrow-linewidth tunable lasers are key components for precision photonics, coherent links, and spectroscopy systems that must scale on chip.
detailed_summary: The work hybrid-integrates a DFB semiconductor laser with a deformed microcavity on a 220 nm SOI platform to realize wavelength-self-adaptive distributed optical feedback. Unlike conventional resonant microcavity feedback, the deformed cavity provides effective feedback over a continuous wavelength range while leveraging enhanced Rayleigh scattering and a high-NA silicon waveguide. The authors report an intrinsic linewidth narrowed to 525 Hz, SMSR of 76 dB, and continuous tuning over 2.25 nm. They also measure reduced frequency noise and RIN, indicating a low-noise chip-scale coherent source.
platform_or_system: DFB laser plus deformed SOI microcavity
main_result: The device achieves 525 Hz intrinsic linewidth, 76 dB SMSR, and 2.25 nm continuous tuning using external deformed-microcavity feedback.
application_angle: Such sources are useful for coherent communications, integrated LiDAR, microwave photonics, and high-resolution spectroscopy.

title: Room temperature Purcell enhanced single erbium ions in silicon-carbide-on-insulator microring resonators
abstract: Spin-carrying single-photon emitters operating in the telecommunication C-band (1530-1565nm) are prime candidates for integrated spin-photon interfaces, offering seamless compatibility with existing fiber-optic infrastructure, an essential component for future quantum networks. In this context, erbium-dopants ($\text{Er}^{3+}$) are particularly compelling due to their exceptional emitter properties, including small spectral diffusion and long spin coherence times. However, their low C-band photon-emission rate and operation at cryogenic temperatures has limited the realization of this technology. In this work, we demonstrate fully integrated single-photon emission from an ion implanted $\text{Er}^{3+}$-embedded into a 4H-silicon-carbide-on-insulator (4H-SiCOI) microring resonator operating at room temperature. By optimizing the mode overlap between the resonator and the $\text{Er}^{3+}$-defect, we achieved a $\sim$70$\times$ Purcell enhancement and recorded small spectral diffusion of $\sim$54 MHz. We further characterize the $\text{Er}^{3+}$ single photon emission via photon correlation g$^{(2)}$-histograms and investigate its performance under varying magnetic-field, demonstrating Zeeman splitting on single emitters.
one_liner: Single Er3+ ions in SiC microrings emit room-temperature telecom-band photons with about 70x Purcell enhancement and low spectral diffusion.
why_it_matters: It moves a telecom-wavelength spin-photon interface toward practical quantum networking by combining room-temperature operation with integrated resonators.
detailed_summary: The authors implant single Er3+ ions into 4H-silicon-carbide-on-insulator microring resonators and demonstrate fully integrated single-photon emission in the telecom C-band at room temperature. By optimizing emitter-mode overlap, they obtain roughly 70-fold Purcell enhancement while maintaining small spectral diffusion around 54 MHz. Photon antibunching is verified through g^(2) histograms, confirming single-emitter operation. Magnetic-field measurements further reveal Zeeman splitting on individual emitters, supporting their use as spin-photon interfaces.
main_result: The paper demonstrates room-temperature telecom-band single-photon emission from single erbium ions in SiC microrings with about 70x Purcell enhancement.
application_angle: This is a strong step toward fiber-compatible quantum repeaters and on-chip quantum nodes that use existing telecom infrastructure.

title: Pontus-Mpemba effect in cavity quantum electrodynamics
abstract: The quantum Pontus-Mpemba effect is a counterintuitive phenomenon in which a quantum system relaxes faster through a two-step evolution protocol than through a single, unquenched relaxation. This work proposes its realization in cavity quantum electrodynamics using the Jaynes-Cummings model with photon loss. The model captures the coherent interaction between a two-level atom and a single quantized mode of a lossy cavity, providing a minimal yet realistic setting to explore dissipative quantum dynamics. Restricting the analysis to the single-excitation sector, the dynamics feature damped vacuum Rabi oscillations for weak dissipation that transition to near-exponential atomic decay under strong dissipation.
