A sudden quench of the cavity decay rate generates distinct relaxation trajectories from the same initial atom-cavity state. The atomic excitation then displays a non-monotonic, accelerated decay, where a trajectory with a quenched dissipation relaxes faster than fixed-loss evolution. The effect originates from the interplay between coherent atom-photon exchange and cavity dissipation, establishing a clear and experimentally accessible realization of the quantum Pontus-Mpemba effect in both optical and circuit QED platforms.
one_liner: A Jaynes-Cummings cavity-QED system with photon loss is proposed as an experimentally accessible platform for the quantum Pontus-Mpemba effect.
why_it_matters: It connects an unusual nonequilibrium quantum-relaxation effect to realistic optical and circuit-QED hardware that can test dissipative control ideas.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a two-level atom coupled to a lossy cavity mode in the single-excitation sector and analyzes relaxation under varying cavity decay. In the weak-loss regime the dynamics show damped vacuum Rabi oscillations, while strong loss produces nearly exponential atomic decay. A sudden quench in cavity dissipation creates a two-step trajectory whose relaxation can outpace fixed-loss evolution from the same initial state. The acceleration arises from the interplay of coherent atom-photon exchange and engineered dissipation, making the effect testable in optical and circuit QED setups.
platform_or_system: optical or circuit Jaynes-Cummings cavity QED
main_result: The authors show that a dissipation quench in a lossy Jaynes-Cummings system can produce faster relaxation than unquenched evolution, realizing the quantum Pontus-Mpemba effect.
application_angle: It suggests new ways to engineer relaxation pathways and dissipation-assisted protocols in photonic and superconducting quantum devices.

title: Three wave mixing vacuum squeezing generation in a SNAIL-based Traveling-Wave Parametric Amplifier with alternated flux polarity
abstract: Recent demonstrations of squeezing generation using Traveling Wave Parametric Amplifiers (TWPAs) have opened the way for the application of broadband microwave squeezing in quantum sensing, quantum-enhanced detection, and continuous-variable quantum information. Here we demonstrate vacuum squeezing generation via residual three-wave mixing (3WM) in a Josephson TWPA based on superconducting nonlinear asymmetric inductive elements (SNAILs) with alternated magnetic flux polarity. By investigating competition between four-wave mixing (4WM) and 3WM nonlinearities, we prove that vacuum squeezing generation via residual 3WM is possible when a careful choice of the operating flux point is adopted. Our study provides valuable insights on the impact of competing nonlinearities on TWPA squeezers, potentially extending the range of applications in the framework of microwave photonics.
one_liner: A SNAIL-based TWPA with alternating flux polarity generates vacuum squeezing through residual three-wave mixing despite competing nonlinearities.
why_it_matters: Broadband microwave squeezing is a practical resource for quantum-limited measurement and sensing, so understanding how to generate it in TWPAs matters.
detailed_summary: This work demonstrates vacuum squeezing generation in a Josephson traveling-wave parametric amplifier built from SNAIL nonlinear elements with alternating magnetic-flux polarity. The analysis focuses on the coexistence and competition of four-wave mixing and residual three-wave mixing processes. By choosing an appropriate operating flux bias, the authors show that residual 3WM can be harnessed to produce squeezing rather than being treated purely as a parasitic effect. The study clarifies how nonlinear-process competition shapes TWPA squeezer behavior and design space.
main_result: The paper demonstrates that careful flux-point selection enables vacuum squeezing generation via residual three-wave mixing in an alternating-polarity SNAIL TWPA.
application_angle: The result supports broadband squeezed microwave sources for quantum sensing, readout enhancement, and continuous-variable microwave quantum information.

title: Surface-Code Thresholds and Qubit Footprints in Shuttling-Based Spin-Qubit Railways
abstract: We present a fault-tolerant mapping of rotated surface codes onto a $2\times N$ silicon spin-qubit railway architecture, utilizing electron shuttling to resolve the wiring fan-out bottleneck. Employing circuit-level noise modeling, we evaluate threshold performances across various noise biases. We demonstrate that shuttling check qubits instead of data qubits fundamentally improves system thresholds. Crucially, under a noise model biased towards dephasing for spin-qubit shuttling, the non-CSS XZZX surface code outperforms standard CSS variants. By tailoring the topological code to this specific inherent bias, we show that the Megaquop footprint is achievable with a distance 7 code requiring a $p = 10^{-3}$ physical error rate, highlighting a pathway for substantial hardware reductions in early fault-tolerant quantum processors.
one_liner: Surface codes mapped onto 2xN silicon spin-qubit railways show improved thresholds when shuttling check qubits, with XZZX favored under dephasing bias.
why_it_matters: It translates a constrained spin-qubit architecture into concrete fault-tolerance and footprint numbers, which is exactly what hardware roadmaps need.
detailed_summary: The paper presents a fault-tolerant mapping of rotated surface codes onto a 2xN silicon spin-qubit railway architecture that uses electron shuttling to ease wiring fan-out limits. Using circuit-level noise simulations, the authors compare thresholds across different bias models and show that moving check qubits rather than data qubits materially improves performance. Under dephasing-biased shuttling noise, the non-CSS XZZX code outperforms standard CSS surface-code variants. They estimate that a Megaquop footprint is achievable with distance 7 at a physical error rate of 10^-3, indicating substantial hardware savings.
platform_or_system: silicon spin-qubit railway with electron shuttling
main_result: The main result is that shuttling-based silicon railways can support more efficient surface-code operation, with XZZX reaching a Megaquop footprint at distance 7 for p=10^-3.
application_angle: This gives a practical architecture-level route to smaller early fault-tolerant spin-qubit processors with reduced wiring overhead.

title: Local droplet etching-assisted quantum dot epitaxy for telecom C-band quantum light emitters
abstract: Significant progress in quantum light sources for quantum communication applications requires reproducible and symmetric quantum emitters acting as single-photon sources capable of generating entangled photons on demand at specific telecom wavelengths. Here, we propose telecom-emitting epitaxial quantum dots (QDs) fabricated using the local droplet etching (LDE) approach. The resulting well-defined, low-density ($10^9$/cm$^2$) QDs based on In$_{x}$Ga$_{1-x}$As are formed in symmetric LDE nanoholes (in-plane aspect ratio of 1.14) in In$_{0.52}$Al$_{0.48}$As. Detailed transmission electron microscopy provides comprehensive insight into the structural integrity, interface quality, and compositional profiles of the QDs, which underpin their promising optical properties. Photoluminescence spectroscopy reveals narrow emission lines (0.2 meV) and high optical quality, while second-order autocorrelation measurements confirm clear single-photon emission, with $g^{(2)}(0)=0.07\pm0.02$ under above-band continuous-wave excitation and $g^{(2)}(0)=0.16 \pm 0.18$ under pulsed excitation. Precise numerical modeling, combining multiband $\boldsymbol{k} \cdot \boldsymbol{p}$ and configuration-interaction methods, supports the optical characterization and identifies thermal excitation pathways that explain the persistence of emission up to liquid-nitrogen temperatures. These results highlight the versatility of the LDE approach for integrating new material systems and pave the way toward scalable fabrication of quantum light sources with tailored emission properties.
one_liner: LDE-grown InGaAs/InAlAs quantum dots deliver telecom C-band single-photon emission with narrow lines and strong antibunching.
why_it_matters: It advances scalable telecom-band quantum light sources needed for fiber-based quantum networks, while keeping symmetry and optical quality high.
detailed_summary: The work uses local droplet etching to form symmetric nanoholes in In0.52Al0.48As and then epitaxially grows low-density InxGa1-xAs quantum dots targeting telecom-band emission. TEM resolves clean interfaces and composition profiles, while photoluminescence shows narrow 0.2 meV lines. Autocorrelation confirms single-photon emission with g^(2)(0)=0.07±0.02 under cw excitation and 0.16±0.18 under pulsed excitation. Multiband k·p plus configuration-interaction modeling explains the optical spectra and the persistence of emission up to liquid-nitrogen temperatures.
main_result: The authors demonstrate telecom C-band epitaxial quantum dots with narrow linewidths and clear single-photon antibunching using an LDE fabrication route.
application_angle: These emitters are directly relevant to deployable quantum communication because they target low-loss fiber wavelengths and support scalable source fabrication.

title: Near-unity efficiency optical vortex generation in van der Waals materials
abstract: Optical spin-orbit coupling provides a promising, fabrication-free route for developing ultra-compact optical vortex generators. However, the conversion efficiency has been theoretically limited to 0.5. Here, we demonstrate enhanced vortex generation efficiency by employing a Bessel beam as the input and propagating it through van der Waals (vdW) crystals. The large birefringence of vdW crystals and the single transverse wave vector of a Bessel beam allow a near unity spin-orbit conversion efficiency and a topological charge transition of $\ell \rightarrow \ell + 2$. Through combined analytical and experimental investigations, we demonstrate a conversion efficiency of up to 0.82 in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) crystals with a thickness of $27.4\,μ\mathrm{m}$. The higher efficiency of Bessel input beams over Gaussian beams is attributed to their distinct transverse wave vector distribution of constituent plane wave components. Furthermore, we demonstrate the dependence of conversion efficiency on the numerical aperture (NA) of the objective lens, which is in good alignment with theoretical predictions. These demonstrations provide a fabrication-free route to highly efficient optical vortex generation via microscale vdW materials platforms.
one_liner: van der Waals crystals driven by Bessel beams generate optical vortices with spin-orbit conversion efficiency up to 0.82.
why_it_matters: It offers a compact, fabrication-free route to efficient vortex generation, useful for structured-light photonics and integrated optical manipulation.
detailed_summary: The paper shows that using a Bessel beam instead of a Gaussian beam lifts the usual 0.5 efficiency limit for spin-orbit conversion in birefringent van der Waals media. In 27.4 um thick hBN, the authors experimentally reach 0.82 conversion efficiency while observing the expected topological-charge shift l to l+2. The mechanism is tied to the single transverse wave vector of the Bessel input and the strong birefringence of the crystal. They also map the dependence on objective numerical aperture and find good agreement with theory.
main_result: The study experimentally demonstrates near-unity-class optical vortex generation in vdW materials, achieving 0.82 efficiency in hBN with Bessel-beam excitation.
application_angle: High-efficiency microscale vortex generation can simplify compact structured-light sources for imaging, trapping, communications, and on-chip photonics.

title: Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction as a coherence diagnostic for chirality-induced spin selectivity
abstract: Whether chirality-induced spin selectivity (CISS) reflects coherent SU(2) spin rotation or incoherent spin-dependent filtering is a central unresolved question in molecular spintronics, with implications ranging from asymmetric chemistry to quantum information. We show that these two scenarios are distinguishable by a sharp symmetry criterion on the superexchange interaction mediated by a chiral molecular bridge. Coherent CISS, implemented as a unitary spin rotation of the tunneling electron, generates a giant Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction with ratio |D|/JH up to 3, which is two orders of magnitude beyond intrinsic Rashba spin-orbit coupling in Si/SiGe. Incoherent CISS, represented by any Hermitian (non-unitary but spin-diagonal) tunneling matrix, produces D = 0 identically; we prove this as a structural theorem, reinforced by a Lindblad argument that dissipative spin filtering cannot modify virtual-tunneling-mediated superexchange. The DM interaction thus serves as a coherence order parameter, nonzero only when quantum amplitudes for opposite-spin transmission maintain a fixed relative phase. We derive closed-form angular, enantiomeric, and sensitivity signatures and show that the critical coherent rotation angle lies two orders of magnitude below current transport-inferred values and is accessible to existing 10 kHz exchange spectroscopy in gate-defined quantum dots. Five candidate molecules are predicted to exceed this threshold by one to two orders of magnitude even in a conservative interface-amplification scenario. The proposed measurement converts a long-standing transport controversy into a binary spin-qubit experiment with quantum-amplitude resolution.
one_liner: A giant Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction is proposed as a binary experimental test for coherent versus incoherent CISS.
why_it_matters: It turns a disputed molecular spin-transport mechanism into a measurable spin-qubit signature accessible with existing exchange spectroscopy tools.
detailed_summary: The authors derive a sharp distinction between coherent and incoherent chirality-induced spin selectivity by analyzing superexchange through a chiral molecular bridge. A unitary coherent spin rotation produces a giant DM term with |D|/JH up to 3, whereas any Hermitian spin-diagonal filtering model gives D=0 exactly. They further show that the required coherent rotation angle is far below current transport-inferred values and within reach of 10 kHz exchange spectroscopy in gate-defined quantum dots. Candidate molecules are predicted to exceed the detection threshold by one to two orders of magnitude.
platform_or_system: Chiral molecules coupled to gate-defined quantum dots
main_result: The main result is a symmetry-based proposal showing that nonzero DM exchange is an unambiguous indicator of coherent CISS and is experimentally accessible.
application_angle: This provides a practical qubit-based diagnostic for molecular spin interfaces, which could guide coherent spin-device design and quantum molecular hardware.

title: Syndrome resampling enhances quantum error correction thresholds
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC) enables fault-tolerant quantum computation but requires operating quantum hardware at physical error rates below code-dependent thresholds, which remains challenging for current devices. We introduce syndrome resampling, a general method that increases QEC thresholds of any decoder and suppresses logical errors without additional hardware, decoding modifications, or code-specific assumptions beyond syndrome statistics. The method exploits the fact that syndromes with low probability are likely to lead to logical failure, therefore biasing syndrome averages towards most likely syndromes effectively increases logical fidelities. We establish a direct connection between the Rényi coherent information (RCI) and powers of the syndrome probability distribution, showing that resampling syndromes according to these powers combined with maximum likelihood decoding (MLD) realizes a family of optimal thresholds associated with phase transitions in the RCI. Numerical simulations of surface codes demonstrate that syndrome resampling substantially increases thresholds for both optimal and suboptimal decoders and reduces logical error rates by up to four orders of magnitude in experimentally relevant regimes. We further show that syndrome resampling can be effectively implemented from finite data and combined with decoding-based post-selection to achieve additional gains. Finally, applying the method to existing experimental QEC data yields up to two orders of magnitude reduction in logical error rates without requiring additional measurements. Our results provide a practical and decoder-agnostic route to improved logical fidelities in near-term QEC experiments.
one_liner: Syndrome resampling boosts QEC thresholds and cuts logical error rates by up to four orders of magnitude without extra hardware.
why_it_matters: It is a decoder-agnostic software-layer improvement that can materially enhance near-term fault-tolerant experiments using existing syndrome data.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces syndrome resampling, which biases decoding toward more probable syndromes to improve logical fidelity without changing the hardware or the decoder internals. The authors connect the method to Rényi coherent information and show that resampling by powers of the syndrome distribution defines a family of optimal thresholds under maximum-likelihood decoding. Surface-code simulations show substantial threshold increases for both optimal and suboptimal decoders and logical error suppression up to four orders of magnitude. Applying the method to prior experimental QEC datasets also yields up to two orders of magnitude improvement without additional measurements.
main_result: The method raises effective QEC thresholds and strongly suppresses logical failures using only post-processing of measured syndrome statistics.
application_angle: Because it works from finite data and existing measurements, it can be integrated quickly into current QEC experiments to improve hardware-level performance.

title: Universal Analog Quantum Simulation
abstract: Analog quantum simulators emulate complex many-body dynamics through native continuous-time evolution under hardware-defined interactions. Yet once a platform is specified, its interaction structure is largely fixed by the underlying hardware, restricting the Hamiltonians that can be realized and limiting programmability. Here we introduce universal analog quantum simulation (UAQS), a hybrid framework that systematically expands the range of accessible quantum evolutions within a given analog platform. UAQS employs optimized continuous-time control fields to engineer target dynamics directly, avoiding decomposition into discrete gate sequences. By preserving native analog evolution while extending the set of achievable Hamiltonians, UAQS transforms fixed-interaction analog devices into programmable simulators. Numerical studies on representative architectures, including superconducting circuits and Rydberg-atom arrays, show that UAQS accurately reproduces non-trivial many-body dynamics beyond the intrinsic interaction structure of the hardware. These results establish UAQS as a practical route toward programmable analog quantum simulation.
one_liner: Universal analog quantum simulation uses optimized continuous-time control to make fixed-interaction analog platforms programmable.
why_it_matters: It extends what analog quantum devices can simulate without gate decompositions, improving programmability on realistic superconducting and Rydberg hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors propose UAQS, a framework that uses optimized continuous-time control fields to engineer target Hamiltonian dynamics directly on analog hardware. Rather than Trotterizing into digital gate sequences, the method preserves native continuous evolution while expanding the reachable set of effective interactions. Numerical studies on superconducting circuits and Rydberg-atom arrays show faithful reproduction of nontrivial many-body dynamics beyond each platform's intrinsic coupling graph. The result is a hybrid route to more programmable analog simulators.
main_result: UAQS demonstrates that optimized analog control can emulate target many-body dynamics not directly supported by the hardware's native interaction structure.
application_angle: More programmable analog simulators could broaden near-term quantum advantage experiments in materials, chemistry, and nonequilibrium many-body physics.

title: Gated QKAN-FWP: Scalable Quantum-inspired Sequence Learning
abstract: Fast Weight Programmers (FWPs) encode temporal dependencies through dynamically updated parameters rather than recurrent hidden states. Quantum FWPs (QFWPs) extend this idea with variational quantum circuits (VQCs), but existing implementations rely on multi-qubit architectures that are difficult to scale on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices and expensive to simulate classically. We propose gated QKAN-FWP, a fast-weight framework that integrates FWP with Quantum-inspired Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (QKAN) using single-qubit data re-uploading circuits as learnable nonlinear activation, known as DatA Re-Uploading ActivatioN (DARUAN). We further introduce a scalar-gated fast-weight update rule that stabilizes parameter evolution, supported by a theoretical analysis of its adaptive memory kernel, geometric boundedness, and parallelizable gradient paths. We evaluate the framework across time-series benchmarks, MiniGrid reinforcement learning, and highlight real-world solar cycle forecasting as our main practical result. In the long-horizon setting with 528-month input window and 132-month forecast horizon, our 12.5k-parameter model achieves lower scaled Mean Square Error (MSE), peak amplitude error, and peak timing error than a suite of classical recurrent baselines with up to 13x more parameters, including Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks (25.9k-89.1k parameters), WaveNet-LSTM (167k), Vanilla recurrent neural network (11.5k), and a Modified Echo State Network (132k). To validate NISQ compatibility, we further deploy the trained fast programmer on IonQ and IBM Quantum processors, recovering forecasting accuracy within 0.1% relative MSE of the noiseless simulator at 1024 shots. These results position gated QKAN-FWP as a scalable, parameter-efficient, and NISQ-compatible approach to quantum-inspired sequence modeling.
one_liner: A single-qubit quantum-inspired fast-weight model matches strong sequence-learning baselines and runs on IonQ and IBM hardware.
why_it_matters: It shows a NISQ-compatible route to useful sequence modeling with low parameter count and near-simulator performance on real processors.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces gated QKAN-FWP, which combines fast-weight programming with single-qubit data re-uploading circuits used as nonlinear activations. A scalar-gated update rule is analyzed theoretically for adaptive memory behavior, boundedness, and parallelizable gradients. On time-series, reinforcement learning, and especially long-horizon solar forecasting, the 12.5k-parameter model beats several larger classical recurrent baselines. The trained fast programmer is also executed on IonQ and IBM Quantum hardware, recovering accuracy within 0.1% relative MSE of noiseless simulation at 1024 shots.
platform_or_system: Single-qubit data-reuploading circuits on IonQ and IBM devices
main_result: The model achieves competitive long-horizon sequence forecasting with a compact architecture and preserves near-simulator accuracy when deployed on real quantum processors.
application_angle: Its small footprint and hardware validation make it a practical candidate for near-term quantum-enhanced temporal modeling in scientific and industrial forecasting.

title: Modular wedge localization, Majorana fields and the Tsirelson limit of the Bell-CHSH inequality
abstract: The massive Majorana field in $1+1$ dimension is employed to investigate the violation of the Bell-CHSH inequality in relativistic Quantum Field Theory. We give an explicit rapidity-space realization of the Summers-Werner modular-localization construction and reduce the vacuum Bell-CHSH correlator to a single spectral weight $h^2(ω)$ for the modular operator. The resulting analytic families approach the Tsirelson bound in the vacuum state as their spectral weight concentrates near $ω\approx0$, corresponding to the eigenvalue $λ^2 \approx 1$ of the modular operator.
one_liner: A modular-localization analysis of a 1+1D Majorana field shows vacuum Bell-CHSH correlations can approach the Tsirelson bound.
why_it_matters: It gives an explicit field-theoretic route to near-maximal nonlocal correlations, sharpening benchmarks for relativistic quantum information and analog simulation.
detailed_summary: The paper studies Bell-CHSH violation in relativistic QFT using the massive Majorana field in 1+1 dimensions and an explicit rapidity-space construction of modular-localized observables. The vacuum correlator is reduced to a single modular spectral weight h^2(ω), which parameterizes analytic families of Bell correlations. As the spectral weight concentrates near ω≈0, corresponding to modular eigenvalue λ^2≈1, the correlator approaches the Tsirelson limit. This provides a concrete spectral mechanism for near-maximal nonlocality in a continuum field setting.
main_result: They show that modular-localized vacuum Bell-CHSH correlations in the Majorana field can be tuned arbitrarily close to the Tsirelson bound through spectral-weight concentration near ω≈0.
application_angle: The result informs relativistic quantum-information protocols and analog platforms that aim to emulate field-theoretic nonlocality limits.

title: Finite-size general security for differential phase shift keying via variable-length quantum key distribution
abstract: Differential phase shift keying (DPSK) constitutes a pathway towards practical quantum key distribution by using affordable commercial technologies, and robust theoretical foundations. Recent advances in the security of DPSK have proven its security against general adversaries, albeit requiring limitations, including strong repetition rate constraints at the security proof and costly statistical estimators. In this work, we overcome said limitations by leveraging recent techniques in variable-length general security by using entropy accumulation techniques based on Rényi leftover hashing, together with conic optimization methods. Our approach achieves secret key rates with $10^5$ signals beyond 12 dB, constituting a robust proof of the experimental implementability of industrial-grade DPSK.
one_liner: A finite-size security proof makes DPSK quantum key distribution practical with short blocks and industrial-grade hardware assumptions.
why_it_matters: It removes key proof bottlenecks for deployable DPSK-QKD, enabling useful secret-key rates with only 10^5 signals at realistic loss.
detailed_summary: The work develops a general finite-size security proof for differential phase shift keying using variable-length QKD techniques, Rényi-leftover-hashing entropy accumulation, and conic optimization. Unlike earlier approaches, it avoids strong repetition-rate constraints and expensive statistical estimators that limited practical deployment. The analysis yields positive secret key rates with 10^5 signals beyond 12 dB channel loss. That combination is directly relevant to short-block, experimentally realistic QKD implementations based on commercial components.
main_result: The authors prove general finite-size security for DPSK-QKD and obtain secret-key rates with 10^5 signals at losses exceeding 12 dB.
application_angle: This strengthens the case for low-cost, industrial DPSK systems in real fiber-network quantum cryptography deployments.

title: Beating noise in frequency estimation with squeezing and memory in continuous-variable systems
abstract: Quantum metrology promises precision beyond classical limits, yet environmental noise typically degrades the quantum resources required for such enhancement. In this work, we investigate frequency estimation in noisy continuous-variable systems, focusing on two complementary strategies to mitigate decoherence: Hamiltonian engineering and the exploitation of non-Markovian dynamics. By embedding squeezing directly into the system Hamiltonian, we show that the quantum Fisher information (QFI) may acquire a tunable higher-order time dependence, leading to enhanced sensitivity in the short-time regime. Moving beyond the Markovian approximation, we employ the quantum Brownian motion model to demonstrate that structured environments with finite memory can induce information backflow, temporarily restoring and even improving estimation precision relative to the unitary limit. We further assess the achievability of these bounds via Gaussian measurements, identifying regimes where homodyne, heterodyne, and optimized general-dyne measurements saturate the QFI, and noting that stronger squeezing widens the gap, potentially requiring non-Gaussian measurement strategies. Our results establish that jointly tailoring system Hamiltonian and environmental memory offers a viable route toward robust quantum-enhanced frequency estimation in open systems.
one_liner: Squeezing in the Hamiltonian plus non-Markovian memory can boost noisy continuous-variable frequency estimation beyond standard open-system limits.
why_it_matters: It identifies concrete control and environment-engineering strategies for robust quantum-enhanced sensing in realistic bosonic platforms.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze frequency estimation in noisy continuous-variable systems under two mitigation strategies: embedding squeezing directly in the system Hamiltonian and exploiting finite-memory environments via quantum Brownian motion. Hamiltonian squeezing produces tunable higher-order short-time scaling of the quantum Fisher information, while non-Markovian dynamics can generate information backflow that transiently restores or surpasses unitary-limit precision. They also compare Gaussian readout strategies, showing regimes where homodyne, heterodyne, and optimized general-dyne measurements attain the QFI. At stronger squeezing, the gap to the QFI widens, indicating when non-Gaussian measurements may become necessary.
main_result: They show that tailored squeezing and environmental memory can enhance noisy frequency-estimation precision and, in some regimes, temporarily outperform the nominal unitary benchmark.
application_angle: The framework is directly relevant to photonic and bosonic quantum sensors where decoherence control and practical Gaussian readout determine usable sensitivity.

title: Elastic and structural anisotropy in silica thin films for gravitational-wave detectors
abstract: The thermal noise of mirror coatings for gravitational-wave detectors critically depends on the elastic properties of the constituent materials. Data analyses and theoretical models typically assume each material is homogeneous and isotropic, but isotropy has never been explicitly verified. Using Brillouin light scattering (BLS), we demonstrate for the first time that ion-beam-sputtered SiO2 -- a material still viable for future mirror coatings -- exhibits cylindrical elastic symmetry, with in-plane isotropy but a notable 6% compressive anisotropy along the film normal. This anisotropy remains unchanged after the post-deposition heat treatment currently used in ground-based detectors (500 $^\circ$C, 10 h) but is nearly eliminated at 900 $^\circ$C. Infrared reflectivity experiments support these findings by directly revealing heterogeneities in the distribution of bridging and non-bridging oxygen structures along the growth axis. While BLS measures the real part of the elastic constants at GHz frequencies, the data reveal negligible contributions from mechanical relaxations in the kHz-GHz range, making BLS a valid substitute for low-frequency properties obtained from standard anisotropy-insensitive techniques. Our results highlight that restoring isotropy through heat treatment -- by softening the material, enabling more than 7% out-of-plane expansion, and smoothing out structural heterogeneities -- may play a key role in reducing thermal noise. This proof-of-concept study extends beyond silica, providing critical insights for the design of future coatings.
one_liner: Brillouin and infrared measurements reveal that ion-beam-sputtered silica films for GW mirrors are elastically anisotropic along the growth axis.
why_it_matters: Quantifying coating anisotropy gives a concrete materials lever for lowering thermal noise in next-generation gravitational-wave detectors.
detailed_summary: Using Brillouin light scattering, the study finds that ion-beam-sputtered SiO2 thin films exhibit cylindrical elastic symmetry with in-plane isotropy but about 6% compressive anisotropy along the film normal. Infrared reflectivity links this behavior to depth-dependent distributions of bridging and non-bridging oxygen structures. The anisotropy survives the standard 500 C, 10 h post-deposition anneal used in current detectors but is nearly removed after 900 C treatment. The GHz elastic data also indicate negligible relaxation contributions across the kHz-GHz range, supporting BLS as a proxy for low-frequency coating mechanics.
main_result: The work experimentally demonstrates significant out-of-plane elastic and structural anisotropy in silica coating films and shows high-temperature annealing can largely restore isotropy.
application_angle: These results guide coating-process optimization for lower thermal-noise optics in gravitational-wave interferometers and other precision photonic cavities.

title: Macroscopic entanglement between two magnon modes via two-tone driving of a superconducting qubit
abstract: The cavity-mediated coupling between magnons in an yttrium-iron-garnet (YIG) sphere and a superconducting qubit has recently been demonstrated as a new platform for preparing macroscopic quantum states. Here, based on this system, we propose to entangle two magnon modes in two YIG spheres by driving the qubit with a two-tone field and by appropriately choosing the frequencies and strengths of the two driving fields. We show that strong entanglement can be achieved with fully feasible parameters. We further provide a detection scheme for experimentally verifying the entanglement. Our results indicate that macroscopic entanglement between two magnon modes in two millimeter-sized YIG spheres, involving more than $10^{18}$ spins, can be realized using currently available parameters, which finds promising applications in fundamental studies, such as macroscopic quantum mechanics and the test of unconventional decoherence theories.
one_liner: Two-tone driving of a superconducting qubit can generate strong entanglement between two spatially separated magnon modes in YIG spheres.
why_it_matters: It offers a feasible path to macroscopic entanglement in hybrid quantum hardware with direct relevance to quantum state engineering and decoherence tests.
detailed_summary: The proposal considers two magnon modes hosted in separate yttrium-iron-garnet spheres, both coupled through a cavity-mediated interaction with a superconducting qubit. By selecting appropriate frequencies and amplitudes for a two-tone qubit drive, the scheme engineers strong effective interactions that produce steady macroscopic magnon entanglement with experimentally feasible parameters. The paper also outlines a detection method to verify the generated entanglement. The predicted states involve collective excitations across more than 10^18 spins, pushing entanglement into a strongly macroscopic regime.
main_result: They show that currently accessible parameters should allow strong entanglement between magnon modes in two millimeter-scale YIG spheres via two-tone qubit driving.
application_angle: The scheme could enable hybrid quantum memories, transducers, and large-mass coherence experiments in superconducting-magnonic hardware.

title: Ablation Removal of Transport-Blocking Defects in Surface-Electrode Ion Traps
abstract: We demonstrate in situ removal of a transport-blocking defect on a surface-electrode ion trap device using a Q-switched Nd:YAG 532 nm pulsed ablation laser. This approach eliminates the need to vent and rebake the vacuum system, providing a low-overhead defect-remediation technique well suited for ion-shuttling architectures where system modifications typically incur substantial downtime - particularly in shuttling focussed experiments operating at temperatures that necessitate bakes. Additionally, the hardware used is readily available in many ion trap laboratories, making this solution attractive to experiments operating in such regimes. Following ablation, we observe near-unit shuttling success rates across the previously obstructed region and measure micromotion levels that remain within acceptable limits. This technique enables rapid, reliable restoration of transport pathways without interruption to experimental operation.
one_liner: In situ laser ablation removes a transport-blocking defect in a surface-electrode ion trap without venting the vacuum system.
why_it_matters: It provides a practical maintenance method that can quickly recover trapped-ion transport performance and reduce downtime in shuttling architectures.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates defect remediation on a surface-electrode ion trap using a Q-switched 532 nm Nd:YAG pulsed ablation laser applied in situ. This avoids breaking vacuum and eliminates the rebake cycle that normally makes such repairs costly, especially for temperature-sensitive shuttling experiments. After ablation, the previously blocked transport region supports near-unit shuttling success while maintaining acceptable micromotion levels. The method relies on hardware already common in many ion-trap labs, which improves its near-term practicality.
main_result: The authors restore ion transport across a previously blocked region with near-unit shuttling success using in situ pulsed-laser ablation.
application_angle: This is an operations-focused advance for scalable trapped-ion processors, where reliable transport and fast recovery from defects are critical.

title: Coherence limitations of a Fourier-engineered $\cos(2\varphi)$ transmon qubit
abstract: Intrinsically protected superconducting qubits are a promising route toward enhancing coherence times and advancing hardware towards applications in quantum computing. The $\cos(2\varphi)$ qubit achieves protection against qubit relaxation by allowing only the coherent tunneling of pairs of Cooper pairs, resulting in Cooper-pair parity symmetry and thereby suppressing charge-induced errors. In this work, we experimentally realize a $\cos(2\varphi)$ qubit by Fourier engineering the energy-phase relation in a multi-junction superconducting circuit. Using an interference-based architecture, we are able to suppress the odd harmonics of an effective qubit potential and we observe good agreement between the measured transition spectrum and the effective theoretical model. We further investigate the energy relaxation time as a function of external flux and find that the qubit lifetime at the flux symmetry point is limited by $1/f$ flux noise. This strong sensitivity arises from residual fluctuations in the first harmonic, which possesses a large prefactor despite being nominally canceled. In contrast, a fluxonium qubit with a similar energy spectrum and noise amplitude is less affected by flux noise, highlighting a key challenge for interference-based protection schemes.
one_liner: A Fourier-engineered superconducting cos(2phi) transmon realizes interference-based protection but shows coherence limited by residual flux sensitivity.
why_it_matters: It gives a concrete experimental readout of why harmonic-cancellation protection is hard to realize in practice for superconducting qubits.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally implement a cos(2phi) transmon by Fourier engineering the Josephson energy-phase relation in a multi-junction superconducting circuit. An interference architecture suppresses odd harmonics and the measured spectroscopy matches the effective model, confirming the intended protected potential. However, relaxation measurements versus flux show that at the symmetry point the lifetime is limited by 1/f flux noise because a residual first harmonic remains with a large prefactor. Comparison with a fluxonium of similar spectrum highlights that interference-based protection can leave strong practical sensitivity to imperfect harmonic cancellation.
main_result: They realize a cos(2phi) transmon experimentally and identify residual first-harmonic flux noise as the dominant coherence limitation at the symmetry point.
application_angle: The work informs superconducting-qubit design by pinpointing a hardware bottleneck that must be overcome before interference-protected qubits can deliver useful coherence gains.

title: Photonic-Implemented Efficient Deep Quantum Neural Network via Virtual-Driven Hilbert Space Expansion
abstract: The growing computational demands of classical neural networks have intensified the search for energy-efficient and powerful computational alternatives. Quantum neural networks (QNNs) implemented on integrated photonic platforms offer a compelling avenue, offering exceptional computational power enhancements, with inherent programmability and scalability of integrated architectures. A critical challenge, however, is implementing the fundamental non-unitary and nonlinear activation function of QNNs within a linear quantum photonic system. Existing strategies, such as the adding ancillary qubits and measurement-based feedback or forward are constrained by high qubit resource costs, overhead devices, and poor cascadability. Here, we propose a novel deep photonic QNN with an expanded computational Hilbert space via input replication and mode expansion, which enables the realization of effective non-unitary and nonlinear activation on a linear programmable quantum photonic chip. This approach eliminates the need for physical ancillary qubits, measurement-induced qubit consumption and the measurement device burden, thereby significantly reduce resource costs. The fabricated chip integrates four high-quality entanglement sources and a programmable high-dimensional interferometric network, enabling a two-hidden-layer QNN that exhibits dimension-enhanced expressivity over the existing QNN architectures. We demonstrate its capabilities across diverse tasks, including nonlinear classification, image generation, and quantum Gibbs state preparation. This work establishes a scalable and efficient architecture toward practical quantum deep learning systems capable of tackling problems beyond the reach of classical computation.
one_liner: An integrated photonic chip implements a deep quantum neural network using virtual Hilbert-space expansion instead of ancillas or measurement feedback.
why_it_matters: It reduces photonic QNN overhead while demonstrating multi-layer learning tasks on hardware, improving the path to scalable optical quantum AI.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a deep photonic quantum neural network architecture that realizes effective nonlinear, non-unitary activation through input replication and mode expansion in a larger virtual Hilbert space. This avoids physical ancilla qubits, measurement-induced qubit consumption, and feedback-heavy activation schemes that are difficult to cascade on chip. The fabricated device integrates four entangled-photon sources with a programmable high-dimensional interferometric network and supports a two-hidden-layer QNN. Hardware demonstrations include nonlinear classification, image generation, and quantum Gibbs state preparation, showing enhanced expressivity relative to earlier photonic QNN approaches.
main_result: They experimentally demonstrate a two-hidden-layer photonic QNN with virtual Hilbert-space expansion that performs several learning and state-preparation tasks without ancilla-based activation.
application_angle: This architecture is relevant for practical optical accelerators for quantum machine learning because it cuts resource overhead while remaining programmable and chip-scalable.

title: Revisiting the multi-mode rhombus circuit as a biased-noise qubit
abstract: In this work, we revisit the idea of using an interferometer of pairs of Josephson junctions as a protected rhombus qubit. Unlike in the original proposal, where the qubit states are encoded into odd and even parity charge states, here, we intentionally alter the energy of one of the junctions to investigate the soft version of the rhombus qubit. This approach allows us to directly probe the qubit transitions over several GHz and reduce the potential drawbacks of the interferometer-based protection. Away from a half flux quantum external field, the large shunting capacitors of the circuit ensure localized qubit states in different phase valleys, leading to a biased-noise qubit. In the realized circuit, we measure an average $T_1\approx500\,μ$s relaxation time in the biased-noise regime (with a Ramsey dephasing time of $T^{R}_\varphi\approx90\,$ns), while an average $T_1\approx27\,μ$s relaxation time at frustration (with $T^{R}_\varphi\approx670\,$ns). Our loss analysis on this multi-mode circuit indicates that at low frequencies, flux noise and quasiparticle tunneling limit the relaxation times, pointing toward the presence of an optimal operating regime of around a few GHz.
one_liner: A soft rhombus Josephson circuit exhibits biased-noise-qubit behavior with long T1 away from half-flux frustration and distinct dephasing tradeoffs.
why_it_matters: It maps out an experimentally accessible operating regime for protected superconducting circuits and clarifies the dominant low-frequency loss channels.
detailed_summary: The authors revisit the multi-mode rhombus circuit by intentionally detuning one junction to create a softer, directly probeable version of the protected rhombus qubit. Away from half-flux frustration, large shunt capacitances localize states in distinct phase valleys, producing a biased-noise operating regime. In this regime they measure average T1 around 500 microseconds with Ramsey dephasing around 90 ns, while at frustration they find T1 around 27 microseconds and Tphi^R around 670 ns. Their loss analysis indicates flux noise and quasiparticle tunneling dominate relaxation at low frequency, implying an optimal few-GHz operating window.
main_result: They demonstrate biased-noise-qubit operation in a soft rhombus circuit, with T1 reaching about 500 microseconds away from frustration and loss dominated by flux noise and quasiparticles.
application_angle: The results guide how to bias and engineer rhombus-style superconducting qubits for longer-lived operation in future protected quantum processors.

title: Tight Contraction Rates for Primitive Channels under Quantum $f$-Divergences
abstract: Data-processing inequalities capture the phenomenon that two probability distributions can only become less distinguishable under any common post-processing. For more fine-grained inequalities, one turns to strong data-processing inequality (SDPI) constants, which give the strongest inequalities for a given channel and reference state for a fixed measure of distinguishability. These quantities have been used to quantify the rate at which time-homogeneous Markov chains contract towards a fixed point both in the classical and quantum setting. In this work, we establish that quantum $f$-divergences satisfy a local reverse Pinsker inequality, which implies the asymptotic contraction rate of a primitive channel to its stationary state is upper bounded by the SDPI constant of any non-commutative $χ^2$-divergence. Using quantum-detailed balance, we establish a sufficient condition for these bounds to be tight. Finally, we apply these results to Petz, Matsumoto, and Hirche-Tomamichel $f$-divergences, establishing new and strengthening previously known results.
one_liner: This work derives tight asymptotic contraction bounds for primitive quantum channels using SDPI constants of quantum f-divergences.
why_it_matters: It sharpens convergence guarantees for noisy quantum processes, which matters for analyzing thermalization, channel mixing, and quantum information-processing primitives.
detailed_summary: The paper proves a local reverse Pinsker inequality for quantum f-divergences and uses it to upper-bound the asymptotic contraction rate of primitive channels toward stationarity by SDPI constants of non-commutative chi-squared divergences. Under quantum detailed balance, the authors provide a sufficient condition under which these bounds are tight. They then instantiate the framework for Petz, Matsumoto, and Hirche-Tomamichel f-divergences, extending and strengthening prior contraction results. Although theoretical, the results give a cleaner toolbox for characterizing dissipative quantum evolutions and convergence under realistic channels.
main_result: They establish general, and in detailed-balance cases tight, asymptotic contraction-rate bounds for primitive quantum channels under broad classes of quantum f-divergences.
application_angle: These bounds can inform performance analysis of noise, equilibration, and iterative quantum protocols used across experimental platforms and communication systems.

title: Affine Subcode Ensemble Decoding for Degeneracy-Aware Quantum Error Correction
abstract: Quantum low-density parity-check codes are promising candidates for low-overhead fault-tolerant quantum computing, but degeneracy is known to impair the convergence of belief-propagation (BP) decoding of these codes. In this work, we show that appending linearly independent rows to a check matrix of a stabilizer code can reduce the search space for a valid degenerate solution. Motivated by this, we extend the recently proposed affine subcode ensemble decoding technique from the classical to the quantum setting. Moreover, we employ overcomplete matrices for each decoding path. Monte-Carlo simulations on toric and generalized bicycle codes demonstrate improved convergence and reduced logical error rate.
one_liner: A degeneracy-aware affine subcode ensemble decoder improves BP-style decoding for quantum LDPC codes using augmented overcomplete check matrices.
why_it_matters: Better decoding directly reduces logical error rates and lowers the implementation barrier for practical fault-tolerant quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors show that appending linearly independent rows to a stabilizer check matrix can reduce the search space for degenerate solutions that otherwise hamper BP decoding. Building on that observation, they generalize affine subcode ensemble decoding from classical coding to quantum stabilizer codes and use overcomplete matrices along each decoding path. Monte Carlo simulations on toric and generalized bicycle codes show improved convergence and lower logical error rates. The method specifically targets the degeneracy pathology that makes standard BP unreliable on quantum LDPC families.
main_result: They introduce a degeneracy-aware affine subcode ensemble decoder that outperforms baseline BP decoding on toric and generalized bicycle code simulations.
application_angle: The decoder is practically relevant for fault-tolerant architectures because improved logical performance can reduce overhead in hardware using sparse quantum codes.

title: A Unified SU(2) Framework for Vector Beam Transformations and Complex Beam Shaping
abstract: We present a constructive framework for designing transformations between structured light fields using birefringent optical elements, formulated in terms of SU(2) operations on polarization. Within this framework, transformations between vector beams are treated as spatially varying SU(2) operations, leading to a direct procedure for designing doubly inhomogeneous waveplates (d-plates) that implement the desired mapping. We identify a condition under which a single element implements a prescribed transformation exactly, including the global phase, and provide an explicit prescription for constructing the corresponding doubly inhomogeneous waveplate (d-plate) when this condition is satisfied, along with its realization using a finite sequence of singly inhomogeneous plates, including a QHQ configuration. Within this formulation, a broad class of problems in structured light can be treated within a single framework, including vector beam transformations, spin-orbital dynamics, and complex beam shaping. Crucially, the same SU(2) operations directly realize quantum channels on the orbital angular momentum degree of freedom, with polarization serving as a physical ancilla. These results establish a unified and explicitly constructive route to complex beam shaping and vector beam transformations based on SU(2) parameter synthesis, and provide a systematic foundation for designing next-generation photonic elements for structured light and spin-orbit information processing.
one_liner: A unified SU(2) design framework constructs birefringent elements for exact vector-beam transformations and complex structured-light shaping.
why_it_matters: It offers a systematic route to fabricating custom structured-light optics for imaging, communications, and spin-orbit photonic information processing.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates transformations between structured light fields as spatially varying SU(2) operations on polarization, yielding a constructive method for designing doubly inhomogeneous waveplates. It identifies when a single optical element can implement a target transformation exactly, including global phase, and gives an explicit synthesis recipe for the corresponding d-plate. The authors also show how such devices can be realized with finite sequences of singly inhomogeneous plates, including QHQ configurations. The same formalism maps naturally onto quantum channels acting on orbital angular momentum with polarization as a physical ancilla.
main_result: They provide an explicit SU(2)-based synthesis procedure for optical elements that exactly implement targeted vector-beam and complex beam-shaping transformations.
application_angle: The framework can accelerate practical design of compact photonic components for OAM processing, advanced microscopy, beam engineering, and optical communication.

title: Giant-atom-enabled quantum optics with valley-polarized photons
abstract: Valleytronics and valley photonics exploit the valley degree of freedom to encode and manipulate information. Here we show that photonic valleys can be selectively addressed in quantum optics using a simple two-level emitter, provided it is coupled nonlocally to the field, thereby realizing a so-called giant atom. Specifically, we consider a qubit coupled at multiple points to an engineered honeycomb lattice of resonators with detuned sublattice frequencies. By tailoring the geometry of the coupling points, the giant atom can be made to emit selectively into a single valley. The emitted photons thereby acquire a well-defined valley character and inherit the associated Berry curvature. By placing the qubit near a domain wall between regions of opposite sublattice detuning, whose interface supports valley-polarized edge modes, emission becomes chiral along the domain wall. This provides a promising route toward implementation of single-photon disorder-robust chiral emission without breaking time-reversal symmetry of the electromagnetic medium in platforms such as circuit QED.
one_liner: A giant atom in a valley-engineered resonator lattice is shown to emit single photons selectively into one photonic valley and chirally along a domain wall.
why_it_matters: It offers a hardware-plactical path to disorder-robust chiral single-photon routing without magnetic bias, relevant to topological photonics and on-chip quantum links.
detailed_summary: The work studies a two-level emitter coupled nonlocally at multiple points to a detuned honeycomb resonator lattice, realizing a giant-atom interaction with geometry-dependent interference. By choosing the coupling-point arrangement, the emitter preferentially couples to one valley so emitted photons carry valley index and associated Berry-curvature properties. When the emitter is placed near a domain wall between opposite sublattice detunings, it excites valley-polarized edge modes and emits chirally along the interface without breaking time-reversal symmetry.
platform_or_system: Giant atom coupled to a honeycomb resonator lattice
main_result: The authors show that multi-point giant-atom coupling enables single-valley photon emission and chiral domain-wall emission in a valley photonic lattice.
application_angle: This could enable topological single-photon sources and robust directional routing in integrated microwave or photonic quantum networks, especially in circuit-QED-style hardware.

title: Mid-Circuit Measurements for Clifford Noise Reduction in Hamiltonian Simulations
abstract: Quantum simulation of fermionic Hamiltonians is a leading application of quantum computing, but accurate execution on present-day hardware is limited by error accumulation in deep Trotter circuits. We present a device-matched noise-reduction framework for encoded Hamiltonian simulation that combines symplectic-transvection-based Trotter synthesis in the Generalized Superfast Encoding (GSE) with Clifford Noise Reduction (CliNR) and Shor-style stabilizer verification enabled by mid-circuit measurement. We implement this approach for a six-qubit encoded Clifford Trotter step on a Barium development system similar to the forthcoming IonQ Tempo line and benchmark it against direct execution using both hardware experiments and a calibrated device-level noise model. The encoded CliNR execution achieves up to 54% lower logical error rate. Crucially, this advantage disappears when stabilizer readout is deferred to the end of the circuit, showing that timely mid-circuit fault detection, rather than verification overhead alone, drives the improvement. As a proof of concept, we further show that machine-learning-guided stabilizer selection can identify verification operators that outperform random choices. These results demonstrate that encoding-native verification combined with dynamic-circuit primitives can materially improve application-motivated quantum simulation without the full overhead of quantum error correction.
one_liner: Mid-circuit stabilizer checks plus Clifford Noise Reduction cut logical error rates in encoded Hamiltonian simulation on trapped-ion hardware by up to 54%.
why_it_matters: It gives a concrete near-term recipe for improving application-level quantum simulation using dynamic circuits, without full fault-tolerant overhead.
detailed_summary: The paper combines symplectic-transvection Trotter synthesis in the Generalized Superfast Encoding with Clifford Noise Reduction and Shor-style stabilizer verification. A six-qubit encoded Clifford Trotter step is implemented for a barium trapped-ion development system and benchmarked experimentally and with a calibrated device-level noise model. Encoded CliNR yields up to 54% lower logical error than direct execution, and the benefit vanishes if stabilizer readout is postponed to the circuit end, isolating mid-circuit fault detection as the key mechanism. The authors also show machine-learning-guided stabilizer selection can outperform random verification choices.
main_result: Device-matched encoded simulation with mid-circuit stabilizer verification reduces logical error by as much as 54% relative to direct execution.
application_angle: The method is directly relevant to near-term chemistry and materials simulation workloads on ion-trap systems that already support dynamic-circuit primitives.

title: Realistic Simulation of Quantum Repeater with Encoding and Classical Error Correction
abstract: Quantum repeaters are essential for scalable long-distance quantum networking. As quantum information processing moves toward fault-tolerant and error-corrected operations, it becomes increasingly important to study quantum repeaters that also move beyond raw physical entanglement and towards logical entanglement. In this paper, we implement and simulate the quantum repeater with encoding and classical error correction (QRE-CEC) protocol in SeQUeNCe, a discrete-event simulator of quantum networks. The protocol distributes logical Bell pairs, performs encoded entanglement swapping, and uses classical error correction for the decoding of entanglement swapping measurement outcomes to determine Pauli-frame corrections. For this study, we extend SeQUeNCe with a stabilizer-based backend, add support for CSS code-based encoded operations, and integrate gate, measurement, idle decoherence, and state-initialization noise models. Our simulation results show that QRE-CEC suppresses all modeled errors to the second order. Also, QRE-CEC can distribute logical Bell pairs with 0.91 fidelity over a distance of 2000 km under the parameter regimes we study. Beyond protocol-level performance evaluation, our implementation exposes practical simulator and control-plane challenges that are typically abstracted away in theoretical studies.
one_liner: A realistic repeater simulation shows encoded repeaters with classical error correction can distribute logical Bell pairs at 0.91 fidelity over 2000 km.
why_it_matters: It moves repeater analysis closer to deployable quantum networking by including encoded operations, realistic noise, and control-plane constraints.
detailed_summary: The authors implement the QRE-CEC protocol in the SeQUeNCe network simulator using a stabilizer-based backend and support for CSS-code encoded operations. Their model includes gate, measurement, initialization, and idle-decoherence noise while tracking logical Bell-pair generation, encoded swapping, and classical decoding for Pauli-frame updates. Simulations indicate that the protocol suppresses all modeled errors to second order and can distribute logical Bell pairs with 0.91 fidelity across 2000 km under the studied parameters. The implementation also exposes practical simulator and control-stack issues often neglected in abstract repeater theory.
main_result: The simulated QRE-CEC repeater distributes logical Bell pairs over 2000 km with 0.91 fidelity while suppressing modeled errors to second order.
application_angle: This provides a more realistic blueprint for long-distance fault-aware quantum networks and helps evaluate repeater architectures before hardware deployment.

title: Nonselective generalized measurements as a resource for quantum thermal machines in a double quantum dot
abstract: We investigated quantum thermal machines powered by sequential nonselective generalized measurements, taking a double quantum dot with coherent interdot tunneling as a working substance. In this platform, the competition between detuning and tunneling hybridizes the localized states and modifies the energetic response of the cycle, allowing us to analyze measurement-driven thermodynamics beyond simple diagonal qubit models. We formulate a three-stroke cycle composed of thermalization with a single reservoir and two generalized measurement channels, and derive the corresponding internal-energy and entropy variations in order to identify the operational regimes of the device. Depending on the measurement parameters, the system can operate as a heat engine, accelerator, heater, or refrigerator. We show that the introduction of tunneling not only reshapes the boundaries between these modes, but also generates refrigeration configurations that are absent in the purely detuned model. In addition, the performance maps reveal that temperature, detuning, and tunneling amplitude jointly control the most favorable regions for work extraction and cooling. Our results demonstrate that coherent interdot coupling acts as an important resource for optimizing measurement-powered quantum thermal machines and highlight double quantum dots as a promising setting for experimentally relevant implementations of measurement-assisted thermodynamic devices.
one_liner: A double quantum dot can operate as a measurement-powered heat engine or refrigerator, with coherent tunneling opening cooling regimes absent in simpler models.
why_it_matters: It identifies an experimentally plausible solid-state route to measurement-driven quantum thermodynamic devices with tunable operating modes.
detailed_summary: This work analyzes a three-stroke thermal cycle in a coherently coupled double quantum dot driven by sequential nonselective generalized measurements and thermalization to a single reservoir. The interplay of detuning and interdot tunneling hybridizes the localized states, reshaping the device's internal-energy and entropy flows. Depending on measurement settings, the system can function as a heat engine, accelerator, heater, or refrigerator, and finite tunneling creates refrigeration windows absent in the purely detuned limit. Performance maps show temperature, detuning, and tunneling jointly determine optimal work-extraction and cooling regimes.
main_result: The study shows that coherent interdot tunneling expands the operating phases of a measurement-driven thermal machine and enables refrigeration configurations unavailable in simpler models.
application_angle: The results point toward solid-state implementations of measurement-assisted nanoscale engines and coolers using tunable semiconductor quantum-dot platforms.

title: Hardware-Free Polarization Stabilization for Measurement-Device-Independent Quantum Key Distribution via Correlated Twirling
abstract: Measurement-Device-Independent Quantum Key Distribution (MDI-QKD) provides unconditional security against detector vulnerabilities, but its practical deployment is severely hindered by asymmetric channel turbulence. Fluctuations in optical fibers induce arbitrary polarization drift, degrading Hong-Ou-Mandel interference and forcing extensive calibration downtime. In this work, we propose a hardware-free polarization stabilization technique utilizing a Correlated Twirling protocol based on a unitary 2-design. By applying a synchronized, public twirling supermap, Alice and Bob mathematically transform deterministic, asymmetric geometric rotations into an isotropic Pauli depolarizing channel. Executed entirely as a virtual post-processing step during classical sifting, this protocol mathematically suppresses intrinsic channel noise by a factor of 2/3. We demonstrate through exact quantum state simulations that this induced symmetry neutralizes catastrophic axis-dependent failures, extending the Y-bias tolerance from 0.68 to 0.84 radians. Furthermore, the protocol passively extends the absolute angular misalignment tolerance for the 11% security threshold from $38.7^\circ$ to $47.9^\circ$, sustaining secure key distillation over extended fiber distances in highly turbulent regimes where standard architectures fail. Inherently compatible with decoy-state weak coherent pulses, this algorithmic approach provides a highly scalable, resource-efficient framework for robust long-distance quantum networks.
one_liner: A correlated-twirling post-processing protocol stabilizes polarization in MDI-QKD without extra hardware and raises misalignment tolerance in turbulent fibers.
why_it_matters: It reduces a major deployment bottleneck for secure fiber QKD by replacing active polarization control with an algorithmic, scalable workaround.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a synchronized public correlated-twirling supermap that converts asymmetric deterministic polarization rotations into an isotropic Pauli depolarizing channel during classical sifting. Because the method is virtual, it requires no added optical stabilization hardware and mathematically suppresses intrinsic channel noise by a factor of 2/3. Exact state simulations show Y-bias tolerance improves from 0.68 to 0.84 rad, and the absolute angular tolerance for the 11% security threshold increases from 38.7 degrees to 47.9 degrees. The approach is compatible with decoy-state weak coherent pulse implementations of MDI-QKD.
main_result: Correlated twirling extends secure MDI-QKD operation under polarization drift, significantly increasing axis-bias and angular-misalignment tolerance without hardware changes.
application_angle: This can lower operational overhead and downtime in deployed QKD links by improving robustness to asymmetric fiber turbulence through software-level post-processing.

title: Scalable Liquid-Crystal Integrated Silicon Nitride Photonic Circuits for Reconfigurable Quantum Interference
abstract: Integrated quantum photonics requires compact, efficient, and low-power phase modulators. While silicon nitride (SiN) is a promising platform, existing modulators suffer from high power consumption, thermal crosstalk, or high driving voltages. Liquid crystal (LC) offers a compelling alternative because of the large index changes and industrial maturity. However, their suitability for supporting various applications in the photonic quantum system has not been experimentally confirmed.Here, we report the first experimental demonstration that LC-based phase modulators integrated on a SiN platform show highly visible quantum interference. We fabricated a liquid-crystal integrated Mach-Zehnder interferometer (LC-MZI) that achieved CMOS-compatible performance with V_pi * L < 1 V-mm. In two-photon interference experiments, the devices exhibited high-visibility quantum interference (~98.5%) with voltage-tunable phase control. Furthermore, we validated the scalability of our approach by demonstrating wafer-scale fabrication using stepper lithography. This work establishes LC-integrated SiN photonics as a scalable, reconfigurable, and energy-efficient platform for quantum photonic circuits.
one_liner: Liquid-crystal phase shifters integrated on silicon nitride achieve CMOS-compatible efficiency and 98.5% two-photon interference visibility.
why_it_matters: It validates a low-power, wafer-scalable modulation technology for reconfigurable integrated quantum photonics beyond thermo-optic tuning.
detailed_summary: The authors fabricate a liquid-crystal integrated silicon-nitride Mach-Zehnder interferometer and experimentally test it in a quantum photonics setting. The device reaches CMOS-compatible modulation efficiency with V_pi times L below 1 V-mm while preserving high-visibility two-photon interference of about 98.5% under voltage-controlled phase tuning. They further demonstrate wafer-scale fabrication via stepper lithography, supporting scalability beyond single demonstrators. The results establish that LC tuning can satisfy both quantum-coherence and manufacturability requirements on SiN.
main_result: The work demonstrates voltage-tunable reconfigurable quantum interference on LC-integrated SiN with about 98.5% visibility and efficient phase modulation.
application_angle: This is useful for scalable low-power programmable quantum photonic processors, switches, and interferometric circuits built on a manufacturable SiN platform.

title: A Distributed Switching Protocol for Quantum Networks
abstract: With the advent of the construction and deployment of entanglement-based quantum networks, the efficient use of network resources will become a critical challenge for the scalable operation of such a system. Recently, architectures that incorporate memoryless optical switches have gained attention for forwarding entangled photons. By leveraging these architectures, costly resources such as high efficiency Bell State Analyzers (BSAs) can be shared across the network. Nevertheless, the introduction of switching substantially complicates the process of multiplexing and resource allocation compared to an individual link. In this work, we propose a switching protocol for unbuffered, multidrop quantum networks in a photonic synchronization domain that establishes a link between two end nodes using a shared BSA in the switched network. To achieve this, two end nodes cooperatively select the target BSA node with the lowest path cost and independently reserve each path within the network. Bi-path reservations are performed to allocate resources in a distributed manner. The proposed protocol is evaluated through simulation on Q-Fly network topologies under varying traffic conditions. The results demonstrate high link establishment success with stable performance even under increased network load. These capabilities which are driven by our proposed protocol are an essential way to realize large-scale, managed, and automated quantum networks.
one_liner: A distributed reservation-and-switching protocol improves link setup in entanglement-based photonic quantum networks using shared BSAs.
why_it_matters: It addresses a core control-plane bottleneck for scaling practical quantum networks that share expensive switching and Bell-state-measurement resources.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a distributed switching protocol for unbuffered multidrop quantum networks operating in a photonic synchronization domain. Two end nodes independently reserve bipaths to a shared Bell state analyzer, selecting the target BSA node by lowest path cost while allocating resources without central coordination. Simulations on Q-Fly topologies show high link-establishment success and stable behavior as traffic load increases. The work focuses on network-layer multiplexing and resource allocation rather than physical-link generation alone.
main_result: A distributed bi-path reservation protocol achieves high and stable entanglement-link establishment success in simulated switched quantum network topologies under varying traffic loads.
application_angle: The protocol is directly relevant to automated large-scale quantum internetworking where expensive BSAs and optical switches must be shared efficiently.

title: Towards second-long electron spin coherence of a telecom quantum emitter in naturally abundant CeO$_2$
abstract: Rare-earth-ion-doped crystals has emerged as a promising platform for quantum technologies, owing to their narrow telecom-band optical emission, long spin memory, and compatibility with silicon integrated photonic architectures. However, the realization of scalable quantum devices requires host materials with intrinsically dilute spin environments to suppress decoherence. In this context, erbium (Er$^{3+}$) doped cerium oxide (CeO$_2$) is an attractive candidate due to the ultra-low concentration of nuclear spins in the host matrix and its compatibility with silicon-based technologies. Here we perform a comprehensive investigation of the coherence properties of Er$^{3+}$ electron spin qubit in CeO$_2$ via semiclassical as well as detailed cluster correlation expansion simulations. By systematically exploring magnetic field strength, pulse sequences, erbium concentration, and spin temperature, we identify regimes where decoherence from the spin bath is strongly suppressed. Our investigations illustrate that at dilute doping concentration (of the order of 10 ppb) and sub-Kelvin temperatures, operation near clock transitions enables Hahn-echo coherence times to approach the second timescale even at natural isotopic abundance. Importantly, from a practical standpoint, coherence times on the order of $\sim 10$ ms are expected even at liquid helium temperature (about 2 K) for similar concentrations. Moreover, we demonstrate that an additional enhancement can be obtained with conventional multi-$π$-pulse dynamical decoupling protocols. Thus, our findings establish Er$^{3+}$ doped CeO$_2$ as a front-runner for realizing spin qubits, quantum memories, and integrated quantum networks.
one_liner: Simulations indicate Er3+:CeO2 can support telecom spin coherence from milliseconds at 2 K toward seconds near clock transitions.
why_it_matters: It identifies a silicon-compatible telecom rare-earth platform with coherence long enough for spin qubits, memories, and networked quantum nodes.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze the electron-spin coherence of Er3+ dopants in naturally abundant CeO2 using semiclassical modeling and cluster correlation expansion simulations. They map coherence versus magnetic field, pulse sequence, erbium concentration, and spin temperature, finding that dilute doping around 10 ppb and sub-Kelvin operation near clock transitions can push Hahn-echo T2 toward the second scale. Even at about 2 K, the study predicts coherence around 10 ms, with further extension from multi-pulse dynamical decoupling. The host's low nuclear-spin background and silicon compatibility make the platform notable for integrated telecom quantum devices.
main_result: The study predicts near-second Hahn-echo coherence at dilute doping and sub-Kelvin temperatures, and roughly 10 ms coherence at liquid-helium temperature in naturally abundant Er3+:CeO2.
application_angle: Long-lived telecom-band spins in a silicon-compatible oxide could enable practical quantum memories, repeater nodes, and integrated photonic-spin hardware.

title: Breaking mechanical dark mode via the Coulomb interaction
abstract: We propose a method to break the dark mode of two degenerate mechanical resonators (MRs) in optomechanical systems via the Coulomb interaction. Two degenerate MRs can be cooled to their ground-state simultaneously beyond the resolved sideband regime using the Coulomb interaction and an optical parametric amplifier (OPA). We show that strong and robust mechanical squeezing beyond 3 dB can be generated using the OPA and mechanical parametric amplification (MPA) introduced by the Coulomb interaction. Our results manifests that robust bipartite and genuine tripartite entanglement can be produced in a degenerate optomechanical system.
one_liner: Coulomb coupling breaks a mechanical dark mode, enabling cooling, squeezing, and multipartite entanglement in degenerate optomechanics.
why_it_matters: It offers a control mechanism for activating otherwise inaccessible optomechanical dynamics useful for nonclassical state generation.
detailed_summary: This work studies two degenerate mechanical resonators in an optomechanical system and shows that Coulomb interaction can lift the mechanical dark mode. With the addition of an optical parametric amplifier, both resonators can be simultaneously cooled to the ground state beyond the resolved-sideband regime. The same ingredients, together with Coulomb-induced mechanical parametric amplification, generate robust squeezing beyond 3 dB and support both bipartite and genuine tripartite entanglement. The proposal is primarily a state-engineering scheme for multimode cavity optomechanics.
main_result: By breaking the dark mode with Coulomb coupling, the scheme enables simultaneous ground-state cooling, strong squeezing, and multipartite entanglement in a degenerate optomechanical system.
application_angle: The approach could improve photonic-mechanical quantum transducers and continuous-variable state preparation in integrated optomechanical devices.

title: Hybrid Qubit-Qutrit Quantum Battery: Nonclassicality and Energy Performance
abstract: We propose and analyze a hybrid qubit-qutrit quantum battery (QB) based on a mixed spin-1/2 and spin-1 system interacting via an anisotropic Heisenberg exchange coupling in the presence of a homogeneous magnetic field.
